pages.label: articoli determinativi e indeterminativi
pages.format: Lesson page: rules + example words only, no exercise items. Serves as hub linking to the four A1 drill pages.
pages.skills_tested: Reference only: full definite article paradigm (il/i, lo/gli, la/le, l' elision) and indefinite paradigm (un, uno, una, un'), keyed to noun-initial sound.
pages.traps_and_distractors: n/a (no items), but the rule presentation itself defines the trap inventory used in the drills: lo/uno trigger letters, elision before vowels.
pages.steals: Example pairs worth reusing: 'l'errore' (masc elision) vs 'l'aula' (fem elision); 'uno zaino, uno scoiattolo' for uno; 'un'isola, un'automobile' for fem elision.
pages.lesson_rules: Definite: il/i before masc consonant (il libro/i libri); lo/gli before x, y, z, gn, pn, ps, s+consonant (page's explicit trigger list); la/le for feminine; lo/la elide to l' before vowels both genders. Indefinite: un for masc before vowels and most consonants (un aereo, un treno); uno before x, y, z, gn, pn, ps, sc- words (uno zaino, uno scoiattolo); una before fem consonant (una palla, una scena); una elides to un' before fem vowel (un'isola, un'automobile). Exception-cluster vocabulary the page supplies: gnomo, psicologi, spaghetti, zaino, scoiattolo.

pages.label: articoli
pages.format: Hot Potatoes gap-fill with dropdown per gap, 12 sentence items, instruction 'Choose the correct article.' (Dropdown option lists are JS-embedded and not extractable; the choice set is the article inventory incl. def/indef forms.)
pages.skills_tested: Definite vs indefinite choice driven by sentence context, not just noun shape: possessive+article ('__ mia macchina' -> La; '__ vostro libro' -> Il); first-mention indefinite ('Luca ha trovato __ lavoro' -> un; 'posso fare __ domanda?' -> una); anaphoric/specified definite ('Ti piace __ zuppa che ho preparato?' -> la, relative clause forces definite); habitual time ('__ venerdi sera di solito esco' -> Il); 'uno degli + plural' partitive-of ('__ degli studenti non è italiano' -> Uno); fixed expression ('Mio fratello ha __ influenza' -> l', avere l'influenza); measure phrase ('Ho bevuto __ bicchiere di vino rosso' -> un).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Baits definite where English speakers drop it (possessive: 'la mia macchina') and indefinite where the noun is contextually unique ('il nuovo Teatro Comunale' after 'è stato inaugurato' — inauguration of a named unique thing takes il, not un). 'Uno degli studenti' baits 'un/il'. 'ha l'influenza' baits 'un'influenza'. Habitual 'il venerdì' baits bare venerdì.
pages.steals: 'Ieri sera a Bologna è stato inaugurato __ nuovo Teatro Comunale.' (il — unique named referent despite first mention); '__ degli studenti non è italiano.' (Uno degli — pronoun-like indefinite before partitive).

pages.label: articoli
pages.format: Hot Potatoes gap-fill with dropdown, 12 sentence items, 'Choose the correct article.' Options JS-embedded (not extractable); set spans def/indef/partitive forms.
pages.skills_tested: Mixed definite/indefinite/partitive in context: possessive singular vs plural ('__ mio cantante preferito' -> Il; '__ suoi genitori' -> I); fem elision un' vs masc bare un ('Voglio __ automobile nuova' -> un'; 'Luigi ha __ amico peruviano' -> un — the classic apostrophe minimal pair, deliberately adjacent); uno before s+consonant ('Hanno appena costruito __ stadio nuovo' -> uno); gli before vowel plural ('Tu ami __ alberi e i fiori' -> gli, with 'i fiori' in the same sentence as contrast); plural indefinite/partitive ('Loro hanno __ figli molto intelligenti' -> dei; 'Voi siete __ ragazze molto interessanti' -> delle); 'Ho mangiato __ lasagne' (le/delle); repeats 'ha __ influenza' and '__ degli studenti' from the sibling drill.
pages.traps_and_distractors: un vs un' apostrophe trap run as a back-to-back pair (automobile fem / amico masc); 'gli alberi e i fiori' baits i/gli confusion within one item; plural 'figli' baits bare-zero article vs dei; 'uno stadio' baits un (st- cluster).
pages.steals: 'Voglio un'automobile nuova.' immediately followed by 'Luigi ha un amico peruviano.' — the cleanest un/un' contrast pair on the site; 'Tu ami gli alberi e i fiori.' (self-contained i/gli contrast).

pages.label: articoli 2
pages.format: Hot Potatoes single-word gender-ID drill, dropdown (il/la) before each bare noun, 12 items. Instruction: 'Is the noun masculine or feminine? Choose the correct article.'
pages.skills_tested: Gender identification from noun ending, easiest tier: transparent -o masc (bambino, vino, libro, gatto), transparent -a fem (camera, sorella, professoressa, bottiglia), then the -e ambiguity introduced: il giornale, il dottore (masc -e) vs la madre, la lezione (fem -e, -zione rule).
pages.traps_and_distractors: The -e nouns are the whole trap: giornale/dottore bait la, madre/lezione bait il. 'lezione' plants the -ione => feminine heuristic without stating it.
pages.steals: The 12-word sequencing itself: 8 transparent anchors then 4 -e nouns — a good difficulty ramp template for atomised gender drills.

pages.label: articoli 3
pages.format: Same single-word il/la dropdown gender drill, 12 items, same instruction.
pages.skills_tested: Escalated gender traps: more -e nouns (il fiore, il sapone vs la chiave — chiave baits il hard), then abbreviated/invariable deceptive endings: la radio, la moto (fem despite -o), il cinema (masc despite -a), alongside transparent fillers (strada, professore, bambina, giardino, formaggio, carta).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Truncation traps: radio(fonia), moto(cicletta), cinema(tografo) — gender follows the full form, ending lies. 'la chiave' is the standout -e feminine bait.
pages.steals: The trap trio 'la radio, la moto, il cinema' as a canonical 'ending lies' item set; 'la chiave' as the -e feminine distractor magnet.

pages.label: articoli 4
pages.format: Same single-word gender drill, 12 items, but the answer set must include lo (sport), so it's il/lo/la.
pages.skills_tested: Hardest gender tier: la mano (-o fem), il poeta (-a masc, Greek-type), la foto (truncated fem), la pace vs il pane vs il cane (three -e nouns split by gender), la televisione (-ione fem again), la libertà (accented -à fem), consonant-final loanwords: lo sport (s+cons => lo!), il bar, il film. Transparent anchor: il gatto.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Loanword triple 'sport/bar/film' baits both gender AND il-vs-lo (lo sport is the only lo on the page); 'mano' and 'poeta' are the flagship reversed-ending traps; 'pace' vs 'pane' minimal-looking -e pair with opposite genders.
pages.steals: 'lo sport / il bar / il film' — one loanword set testing two rules at once; 'la pace / il pane' as a near-minimal -e gender pair.

pages.label: articolo, con o senza?
pages.format: Hot Potatoes gap-fill with dropdown, 12 sentence items, instruction 'Choose the correct option from the dropdown menu:'. Dropdowns necessarily include a zero/'—' option (con o senza).
pages.skills_tested: Article vs zero-article decisions: possessive+singular-kin zero ('Non ho mai conosciuto __ sua nonna' -> zero) vs possessive+non-kin article ('__ mio compagno' -> Il); regions take article ('...è __ Puglia' -> la) vs personal names zero ('con __ Giovanna' -> zero); small island zero ('__ Malta è un'isoletta' -> zero) vs archipelago article ('__ Mauritius si trovano...' -> Le, plural agreement clue in 'si trovano'); tutto paradigm three ways: 'tutta la Toscana e __ isola d'Elba' (l'), 'tutta __ città' (la), 'tutti __ giorni' (i) vs adverbial tutto zero ('è tutta __ strana' -> zero); article+cui possessive relative ('Maurizio, __ cui mamma lavora qui' -> la); artworks take article ('__ David di Michelangelo' -> il).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Every item is a use/omit coin-flip with a plausible wrong article: 'la sua nonna' bait (kin rule), 'Malta'/'le Mauritius' as paired island exceptions, adverbial 'tutta strana' baiting 'tutta la strana', 'il cui' vs 'la cui' agreement-with-following-noun bait, 'David' baiting zero because it's a personal name (but it's the statue).
pages.steals: 'La sua nuova ragazza è tutta __ strana.' (adverbial tutto, zero — rare, excellent trap); 'Maurizio, __ cui mamma lavora qui, è un mio caro amico.' (la cui — article agrees with mamma, not Maurizio); the Malta/Mauritius pair.

pages.label: articoli, uso e omissione
pages.format: Lesson page: two bulleted rule lists ('We use articles before...' / 'We do not use articles...') with examples; no exercise items.
pages.skills_tested: Reference for the C1 drill: geography, names, days, kin, tutto, demonstratives, compounds.
pages.traps_and_distractors: n/a, but each rule carries a named exception that the paired exercise weaponises (Israele, negli Stati Uniti, la Roma, il lunedì, la Luna/la Terra/il Sole).
pages.steals: The 'la Roma' note: cities take no article EXCEPT sports teams ('la Roma') — directly reused as 'il Bologna ha battuto la Juventus' in the drill.
pages.lesson_rules: USE article with: country names ('la Germania, la Svizzera, la Danimarca' — stated exception: Israele takes none); islands/archipelagos ('la Sicilia, la Sardegna, le Eolie, le Canarie'); mountains/rivers/volcanoes ('l'Everest, il Monte Rosa, il Tevere, l'Ebro, l'Etna, il Vesuvio'); districts/zones/monuments ('l'Upper East Side, il Colosseo, la Torre di Pisa'); surnames of famous people ('il Petrarca, i Savoia'); tutto+noun ('tutti i giorni', 'tutte le ragazze'). OMIT article with: small islands (Malta, Capri); days of week (lunedì, martedì) EXCEPT habitual 'Il lunedì vado in palestra'; continents/countries after 'in' ('in Italia, in Asia, in America') EXCEPT 'negli Stati Uniti'; singular kin terms with possessive ('mia madre, mia sorella, mio zio'); personal names (Antonio, Maura); planets/stars ('Sirio, Venere, Giove, Marte') EXCEPT 'la Luna, la Terra, il Sole'; di-compounds ('un gruppo di ragazze', 'un esempio di frase'); after questo/quello ('quel gatto', 'questo libro'); city names (Lecce, Bolzano, Bologna, Roma) EXCEPT sports teams ('la Roma').

pages.label: articoli, uso o omissione
pages.format: Hot Potatoes gap-fill with dropdown, 12 sentence items, 'Choose the best option from the dropdown menu:'. Several gaps require articulated prepositions or zero, so the option set mixes articles, preposition+article contractions, and bare prepositions.
pages.skills_tested: Direct operationalisation of the lesson's exception list: city zero (Bologna, sentence-initial gap = zero); small-island zero (Capri) vs region with in ('__ Sicilia si mangiano tanti dolci' -> In); kin-possessive zero ('__ mia sorella fa la professoressa', '__ suo padre si è arrabbiato') vs non-kin possessive article ('__ mio migliore amico' -> Il); habitual day ('__ domenica vado sempre' -> La); celestial exception ('__ Terra gira intorno al Sole' -> La); geography+preposition contraction: 'in vacanza __ Ibiza' (a, zero-article island treated like city), 'mi trasferirò __ Stati Uniti' (negli), 'viene __ Spagna' (dalla); team-vs-city ('Ieri __ Bologna ha battuto la Juventus' -> il).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Bologna appears twice with opposite answers (city zero in item 1, team 'il Bologna' in item 10) — deliberate same-word contrast; 'mia sorella fa la professoressa' plants article-with-profession-after-fare next to kin-zero in one sentence; Ibiza baits 'all'/'nella' (island but behaves as city); Stati Uniti baits 'in' bare; 'sempre' cues the habitual-la domenica against the days-take-no-article default.
pages.steals: 'Ieri il Bologna ha battuto la Juventus per 2 a 0.' vs 'Bologna è una bellissima città medievale.' — the same-toponym contrast pair; 'Il prossimo anno mi trasferirò negli Stati Uniti.' (the lesson's stated exception turned into an item).

pages.label: articoli con nomi propri e toponimi
pages.format: Short lesson page: one base rule plus four exception categories with bolded-article examples; no exercise items.
pages.skills_tested: Reference: stylistic/pragmatic article use with person and city names.
pages.traps_and_distractors: n/a (no items).
pages.steals: 'Il Fellini di Amarcord' / 'Lo Shakespeare di Romeo e Giulietta' — 'the X of Y' construction forcing an article onto a surname (and 'Lo' before Sh- is itself a bonus lo-trigger item); 'La Venezia del '700' vs bare 'Venezia'.
pages.lesson_rules: Base rule: 'Determinative articles must not be used with names of persons and towns.' Exceptions: (1) cities when characterised or historically situated: 'La magica Venezia', 'La Venezia del ’700'; (2) regional/colloquial article before female first names: 'La Chiara', 'La Francesca', 'La Vittoria'; (3) name preceded by adjective: 'Il buon Francesco', 'La bella Maria'; (4) specifying a facet/work of a person: 'Il Fellini di Amarcord', 'Lo Shakespeare di Romeo e Giulietta'. No word lists beyond examples; notably does NOT cover Il Cairo/La Spezia-type lexicalised city articles.

batch_tips: All seven exercises are Hot Potatoes dropdown gap-fills, always exactly 12 items, one skill-cluster per page — validates Linguics' atomised-drill sizing. Caveat: the dropdown option lists live in JS the fetcher cannot see, so distractor sets above are inferred from the sentences (the site appears to offer the full article inventory per gap rather than tailored 2-3 way contrasts — an easy place for Linguics to be better).

The site's A1 gender track ramps across three pages: transparent -o/-a, then -e nouns, then reversed-ending traps (mano, poeta, foto, moto, radio, cinema) and loanwords (lo sport, il bar, il film). Steal the ramp, and steal 'lo sport' as the item that tests gender and il-vs-lo simultaneously.

Recurring house trap: plant the same lexical item in two items with opposite answers (Bologna city vs il Bologna team; tutta la città vs tutta strana; un amico vs un'automobile). Same-surface-form contrast pairs are their highest-value design pattern.

Omission drills only work if 'no article' is an explicit dropdown option — the A2 con/senza page is built entirely on use-vs-zero coin flips with a plausible wrong article as bait; Linguics should make zero a first-class answer choice.

The C1 lesson's exception lists (Israele; negli Stati Uniti; la Roma as team; il lunedì habitual; la Luna/la Terra/il Sole vs bare planet names; Malta/Capri small islands) map one-to-one onto the C1 exercise items — the lesson-list-to-item pipeline is directly copyable.

Two constructions on these pages force definite articles in contexts learners expect zero and are rarely drilled elsewhere: article+cui ('la cui mamma') and article+surname-of-work ('Il Fellini di Amarcord').
new_micro_topics: Gender traps: truncated feminines (la foto, la moto, la radio) and inherited irregulars (la mano, il poeta, il cinema)
Loanword gender and article selection (lo sport, il bar, il film, lo yogurt)
un vs un' apostrophe minimal pairs (un amico / un'amica / un'automobile)
uno + s-consonant/z/ps/gn indefinites (uno stadio, uno zaino) vs un
Article with possessives: kin-singular zero vs everything else (mia sorella vs il mio compagno / il mio migliore amico)
Habitual time articles: il venerdì / la domenica vs bare weekday
tutto paradigm: tutto+article+noun vs adverbial tutto (tutta la città vs è tutta strana)
Article vs zero with geography: regions/countries vs cities vs small islands vs archipelagos (la Puglia, Bologna, Malta, le Mauritius) incl. in Italia vs negli Stati Uniti
Toponym re-categorisation: city zero vs team/qualified-city article (Bologna vs il Bologna, La Venezia del '700)
Article + cui in possessive relatives (la cui mamma)
uno degli/una delle + plural partitive-of construction
Fixed-expression articles (avere l'influenza, il David di Michelangelo)
Article before surnames and characterised names (il Petrarca, i Savoia, il buon Francesco, Il Fellini di Amarcord)
Celestial-body article exceptions (la Terra, il Sole, la Luna vs Marte, Venere)

pages.label: plurali lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: rules + example tables, no interactive exercise. Links out to the practice exercises in this batch.
pages.skills_tested: Reference lesson covering: -o→-i (usu. masc.), -a→-e (usu. fem.), -ista→-i (m) / -e (f), masc. -a nouns pluralizing in -i, fem. -o nouns, invariables (accented finals, consonant-final foreign words, truncated words), -co/-go dual outcomes, -ca/-ga→-che/-ghe.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Explicitly flags the pitfalls its exercises then bait: masculine -a nouns (problema), feminine -o nouns split into truly invariable truncations (radio, foto, moto, auto) vs regular la mano→le mani, and the unpredictable -co/-go split (-chi/-ghi vs -ci/-gi).
pages.steals: "il problema → i problemi"; "la radio → le radio" (truncated, invariable) contrasted with "la mano → le mani" (true fem. -o, regular); "l'artista → gli artisti" for -ista.
pages.lesson_rules: Rule list stated: (1) -o→-i; (2) -a→-e; (3) -ista→-i masc / -e fem; (4) some -a nouns are masculine → -i (il problema/i problemi); (5) truncated fem. -o words invariable (radio<radiofonia, foto, moto, auto) BUT la mano→le mani; (6) accent-final nouns invariable (città, virtù, papà); (7) consonant-final/foreign nouns invariable (computer, yogurt); (8) masc. -co/-go may go -chi/-ghi OR -ci/-gi (no reliable rule given); (9) fem. -ca/-ga always -che/-ghe. Examples: il cavallo/i cavalli, la carota/le carote, la città/le città.

pages.label: plurale dei nomi 1
pages.format: Typed gap-fill (Hot Potatoes-style): singular noun with article shown, learner types the full plural. Instruction: "Write the plural forms of these nouns:". 10 items, food/drink lexis.
pages.skills_tested: Within one food-vocab set: -a→-e with article la→le (pesca→pesche adds h; ciliegia→ciliegie keeps i after vowel; salsiccia→salsicce drops i after consonant; arancia→arance drops i), -o→-i, -e→-i regardless of gender (il limone→i limoni, il pesce→i pesci), article transformation l'→le.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Traps are orthographic, not choice-based: -ca needs inserted h (pesche); -cia/-gia i-retention split baited three times in one set (arancia, ciliegia, salsiccia — vowel-before keeps i, consonant-before drops it); pesca vs pesce adjacent tests -a vs -e class assignment.
pages.steals: Items 1/3/6/9 as a -cia/-gia micro-cluster: "l'arancia → le arance", "la pesca → le pesche", "la ciliegia → le ciliegie", "la salsiccia → le salsicce". Full set: arancia, limone, pesca, fagiolo, pesce, ciliegia, vino, pera, salsiccia, cornetto.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page; links back to the plurals lesson.

pages.label: plurale dei nomi 2
pages.format: Typed gap-fill, same template: "Write the plural forms of these nouns:", 10 food items with articles.
pages.skills_tested: Irregular/invariable cases hidden inside an innocuous food list: l'uovo→le uova (gender-switching irregular), il tè and il caffè invariable (accented final), il toast invariable (foreign consonant-final), la carne→le carni (-e→-i on a mass noun), lo→gli article shift (lo spaghetto→gli spaghetti), plus regular -a→-e and -o→-i fillers (lasagna, gelato, torta, pollo).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Baits *uovi for uova; *tè→tei/caffè→caffi; pluralizing toast; treating carne as -a class (*carne→carne/carna); note the page itself spells item 9 "il caffé" (wrong accent) — don't copy. Mixing invariables into a typing drill forces learners to actively decide 'no change' rather than always transforming.
pages.steals: "l'uovo → le uova" placed third in a routine food drill; the invariable trio in one set: "il tè", "il toast", "il caffè". Full set: lasagna, tè, uovo, spaghetto, gelato, torta, toast, pollo, caffè, carne.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: plurale dei nomi 3
pages.format: Typed gap-fill, same template, 10 food items.
pages.skills_tested: -ca→-che with h-insertion (zucca→zucche), mass/uncountable nouns given formal plurals (acqua→acque, farina→farine, carne-type semantics), -e→-i on masculine -ante (lo spumante→gli spumanti), l'→le article with vowel-initial fem. (l'oliva→le olive, l'acqua→le acque), regular -a→-e volume (fragola, spremuta, tagliatella, patata).
pages.traps_and_distractors: zucca baits *zucce (missing h); acqua/farina bait 'uncountable so invariable' thinking — the drill wants the mechanical form; item 4 'tagliatella' is printed without article, and the singular of a word learners only know as plural (tagliatelle) — reverse-frequency trap.
pages.steals: "la zucca → le zucche" (h-insertion); "tagliatella → tagliatelle" (singular of a plural-dominant word). Full set: fragola, spumante, spremuta, tagliatella, dessert, farina, oliva, patata, zucca, acqua — note 'il dessert' invariable foreign word again.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: plurale regolare
pages.format: Typed gap-fill: "Write the plural form of these nouns:", 10 bare nouns WITHOUT articles (unlike nomi 1-3).
pages.skills_tested: Core class endings without article support: -o→-i (bambino, tavolo, libro, teatro, momento), -a→-e (donna, lettera), -e→-i both genders (pesce, valle — valle is feminine, tests that -e→-i is gender-blind), amico→amici (the -co→-ci minority pattern inside a 'regular' drill).
pages.traps_and_distractors: amico is the planted trap: learners who over-apply the 'add h' rule write *amichi (amico/amici/nemici/porci/greci is the classic exception cluster to -co→-chi). valle tests fem. -e nouns that learners wrongly send to -e (*valle→valle) or -a logic.
pages.steals: "amico → amici" sitting at item 7 of an otherwise fully regular list; "valle → valli" as a feminine -e→-i probe. Full set: bambino, tavolo, libro, donna, lettera, teatro, amico, momento, pesce, valle.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: plurale irregolare
pages.format: Typed gap-fill: "Write the plural form of these nouns:", 10 bare nouns, all-irregular set.
pages.skills_tested: One item per irregularity type: zio→zii (-io with stressed i keeps double i, vs studio→studi), problema→problemi (masc. -a), analisi→analisi (fem. -i invariable), braccio→braccia and dito→dita (masc. sing → fem. -a plural body parts), faccia→facce (-cia after consonant drops i), bosco→boschi (-co→-chi), amica→amiche (-ca→-che, contrasts amico→amici in the sibling exercise), re→re (monosyllable invariable), pioggia→piogge (-gia after i... actually after g+consonant cluster: i drops).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Each item baits a specific overgeneralization: *zii→zi, *problema→probleme, *analisi→analise, *braccio→bracci, *dita→diti, *facce→faccie, *boschi→bosci, *amiche→amice, *re→ri, *piogge→pioggie. amica→amiche placed one exercise after amico→amici is a deliberate cross-exercise contrast pair.
pages.steals: The whole 10-item taxonomy is worth stealing as a coverage checklist: zio, problema, analisi, braccio, dito, faccia, bosco, amica, re, pioggia — one exemplar per irregular class in a single A1 drill.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: plurale delle parole 1
pages.format: Matching (select letter A-N): "Match the singular and plural forms", 14 singulars matched to a shuffled bank of 14 plural forms with articles.
pages.skills_tested: Recognition (not production) of article+noun plural pairs recycling the nomi-1/nomi-2 lexis: l'arancia→le arance, l'uovo→le uova, il toast→i toast, il caffe→i caffe [sic, accent dropped], la salsiccia→le salsicce, il pesce→i pesci vs la pesca→le pesche in the same bank.
pages.traps_and_distractors: The answer bank is the distractor set: 'le pesche' (M) vs 'i pesci' (D) both live options for pesca/pesce confusion; 'le arance' vs 'le salsicce' vs 'le olive' force noticing i-drop; invariables (i toast, i caffe, i dessert, le uova as shape-changer) prevent matching by ending-similarity alone.
pages.steals: Bank design: put both 'i pesci' and 'le pesche' in the same option list under singulars 'la pesca' and 'il pesce'. Full option bank: le arance, le uova, le spremute, i pesci, i caffe, i toast, le lasagne, le fragole, i gelati, le salsicce, le olive, i vini, le pesche, i dessert.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: plurale delle parole 2
pages.format: Matching (select letter A-M): "Match the singular and plural forms of these words", 13 singulars, shuffled 13-option plural bank.
pages.skills_tested: Recognition of: gli with s+consonant (lo spaghetto→gli spaghetti, lo spumante→gli spumanti), invariable accented 'il te→i te' [sic, page drops the accent], la carne→le carni (-e→-i fem.), la ciliegia→le ciliegie (i kept after vowel), regular -o/-a sets (limone→limoni via -e→-i, fagiolo, pera, cornetto, torta, pollo, tagliatella, farina).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Bank includes 'le carni' and 'le ciliegie' whose endings can't be guessed from the majority -e pattern; article matching (gli vs i vs le) is itself the cue/trap — learners matching by noun alone must still pick the row with the right article.
pages.steals: "la carne → le carni" and "la ciliegia → le ciliegie" in the same bank; gli-article pair "lo spaghetto → gli spaghetti / lo spumante → gli spumanti". Full bank: le farine, le torte, i polli, le tagliatelle, le carni, i limoni, i te, gli spumanti, le pere, i cornetti, gli spaghetti, le ciliegie, i fagioli.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: plurale dei nomi composti
pages.format: Lesson page: rule taxonomy with example word-lists, no exercise on page.
pages.skills_tested: Reference for compound-noun pluralization: which element inflects (second only, both, neither, first only for capo-), driven by the compound's internal structure (V+N, Adj+N, N+Adj, N+N same/different gender, capo- semantics).
pages.traps_and_distractors: The capo- section is the trap goldmine: i capistazione (first element) vs i capicuochi (both) vs i capoluoghi (second only) vs le capofamiglia (invariable for female leader) — four different behaviors from one prefix.
pages.steals: "il capostazione → i capistazione" vs "il capoluogo → i capoluoghi" as a minimal pair on leadership semantics; "la cassaforte → le casseforti" and "la mezzaluna → le mezzelune" for double inflection.
pages.lesson_rules: Invariable when: 2nd element is a verb (belvedere, saliscendi, fuggifuggi); 2nd is adverb (sottosopra, posapiano); 2nd is already plural/uncountable (cavatappi, lavastoviglie, appendiabiti, asciugacapelli); verb + singular FEMININE noun (battistrada, battiscopa, tosaerba). Pluralize 2nd element only when: no longer felt as compound (ferrovia→ferrovie); N+N same gender (cavolfiore→cavolfiori); Adj+masc.N (bassorilievo→bassorilievi); Adj+Adj (chiaroscuro→chiaroscuri); verb + masc. noun or 'mano' (passaporto→passaporti, grattacielo→grattacieli); uncountable N + N (soprannome→soprannomi). Pluralize BOTH: N+Adj (cassaforte→casseforti); Adj+fem.N (mezzaluna→mezzelune). Capo-: male leader → capi- (capostazione→capistazione); female leader → invariable (la capofamiglia→le capofamiglia); hierarchy job → both (capocuoco→capicuochi); capo- not meaning leader → 2nd only (capoluogo→capoluoghi).

pages.label: nomi irregolari: maschile o femminile?
pages.format: Dropdown multiple choice in sentence context: "Choose the correct option from the dropdown menu", 12 sentences each with one gap. (Dropdown option lists are populated by JS and were not exposed in any fetch; sentence stems captured in full.)
pages.skills_tested: Gender-pair homographs — same root, different gender, different meaning (il manico/la manica type). Each sentence forces the meaning-appropriate gender: 1 'della camicia è troppo corta' (la manica sleeve vs il manico handle), 2 'della tua scarpa si è scollata' (la suola vs il suolo), 3 'Massaggiare ... del piede' (la pianta vs il pianto), 4 'caduto a causa di ... sulla strada' (una buca vs un buco), 5 'fino ... della strada' (alla fine vs al fine), 7 '... della spesa è molto pesante' (la busta vs il busto), 10 '... di una bottiglia ... di sughero' (il tappo vs la tappa), 11 'al centro della foto c'è ... di un fiore' (il gambo vs la gamba), 12 'Roma è ... dell'Italia' (la capitale vs il capitale). [Pairings inferred from stems; options not visible.]
pages.traps_and_distractors: Distractor is always the opposite-gender twin with a real but wrong meaning — plausible morphology, semantic mismatch. Sentence context (camicia, scarpa, sughero, Roma) is the only disambiguator.
pages.steals: "Roma è ___ dell'Italia" (la capitale vs il capitale = money) and "___ di una bottiglia di vino è spesso di sughero" (il tappo vs la tappa = leg of a journey) — clean, self-contained context sentences.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: nomi irregolari
pages.format: Typed transformation: "Make these nouns feminine, or find a suitable alternative:", 10 bare masculine nouns.
pages.skills_tested: Suppletive/lexical feminines rather than -o→-a morphology: dio→dea, celibe→nubile (marital-status pair), stregone→strega, montone→pecora, toro→vacca/mucca, eroe→eroina, maiale→scrofa, scapolo→zitella/nubile, caprone→capra, cane→cagna. Tests knowing that the feminine is a different word, not an inflection.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Every item baits the naive -a swap: *dio→dia, *celibe→celiba, *torо→tora, *eroe→eroa, *maiale→maiala (colloquial/pejorative), *cane→cana. 'o find a suitable alternative' in the rubric signals the suppletion explicitly.
pages.steals: "celibe → nubile" and "scapolo → zitella" (marital-status suppletives, register contrast); the animal block montone/toro/maiale/caprone→pecora/vacca/scrofa/capra as a themed set.
pages.lesson_rules: The rubric itself is the rule of thumb: some masculines have no morphological feminine — you must 'find a suitable alternative' (suppletive lexeme).

pages.label: parole composte
pages.format: Lesson page: formation + pluralization rules with a 16-word vocabulary list, no exercise on page.
pages.skills_tested: Simplified (4-rule) version of the C2 compound-plural system: V+N (pluralize noun only if singular), N+N same gender (2nd changes), N+N different gender (1st changes), N+Adj (both change).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Key bait pair stated: il portachiavi (chiavi already plural → invariable) vs l'asciugamano (mano singular → gli asciugamani); and pescespada where the FIRST element pluralizes (i pescispada) against the usual last-element instinct.
pages.steals: "il portachiavi → i portachiavi" vs "l'asciugamano → gli asciugamani" minimal pair; "il pescespada → i pescispada" (different-gender N+N, first element inflects).
pages.lesson_rules: Rules verbatim: (1) V+N: 'the name is the only part that changes, but it happens only if it is singular' (portachiavi invariable; asciugamano→asciugamani); (2) two nouns same gender: 'the second one changes' (banconota→banconote); (3) two nouns different gender: 'the only name that we have to change is the first one' (pescespada→pescispada); (4) N+Adj: 'the change is total' (cassaforte→casseforti). 16-word list given: portachiavi, tostapane, aspirapolvere, cacciavite, portaombrelli, videoregistratore, telecomando, asciugacapelli, pescespada, pianoforte, gentiluomo, asciugamano, cassaforte, supermercato, passamontagna, motociclista.

batch_tips: Site pattern worth copying: each typed drill plants exactly 1-3 exception items inside an otherwise regular set (amico in 'plurale regolare', uovo/tè/toast in 'nomi 2'), so learners can't autopilot; the exception density stays low enough that the regular rule still gets rehearsed.

The A1 drills use a single lexical field (food/menu vocab) across four exercises and then recycle the exact same words into the two matching exercises — production first, recognition as review. Good template for atomised drill sequencing.

The -cia/-gia i-retention rule is never stated in the A1 lesson but is tested repeatedly (arancia/ciliegia/salsiccia/faccia/pioggia/ciliegie); Linguics should state the rule explicitly (i kept after vowel: ciliegie; dropped after consonant: arance, facce, piogge) since the source tests it blind.
The site's own copy has accent errors to avoid replicating: 'il caffé' (should be caffè) in plurale_nomi2, and the matching exercises strip accents entirely ('il te → i te', 'i caffe').

B2 and C2 compound-noun lessons disagree in coverage: B2 gives 4 rules, C2 gives ~12 including the capo- semantics. The B2 'different gender → first element changes' rule (pescispada) is an oversimplification kept at C2 only via specific classes — worth reconciling before authoring.

Typed gap-fill answers are validated in JS and were not extractable; plural answers listed for typed drills are deterministic inferences from standard Italian, and the B1 dropdown option pairs are inferred from sentence context (flagged in that page entry).

Technical note for future audits of this site: the exercises are Hot Potatoes-style JS pages; direct WebFetch returns only headers. Route through https://r.jina.ai/<url> with a unique cache-busting query param per attempt (the target ignores it), pace requests 75-110s apart to dodge 429s, and retry on intermittent CAPTCHA interstitials.
new_micro_topics: gender-pair homographs (il manico/la manica, il buco/la buca, la fine/il fine, il tappo/la tappa, il gambo/la gamba, la capitale/il capitale) tested via meaning-forcing context sentences
suppletive feminines of animate nouns (dio→dea, eroe→eroina, celibe→nubile, toro→vacca, maiale→scrofa, cane→cagna) as a typed-transformation micro-topic
compound noun plurals tiered: B2 four-rule core (portachiavi/asciugamani/banconote/casseforti) then C2 capo- semantics (capistazione vs capicuochi vs capoluoghi vs le capofamiglia)
-cia/-gia orthographic split as its own drill (vowel keeps i: ciliegie, valigie; consonant drops i: arance, facce, piogge, salsicce)

invariable-noun recognition drill mixing all three invariable classes in one set: accented finals (tè, caffè, città), foreign consonant-finals (toast, dessert, computer), truncations (foto, radio, moto, auto) — with la mano→le mani as the counter-item
singular-of-plural-dominant words (tagliatella, lasagna, spaghetto) — reverse transformation direction
masc.-singular → fem.-plural body parts (braccio→braccia, dito→dita) contrasted with regular bracci/diti figurative plurals at higher levels

pages.label: aggettivi
pages.format: Matching (JMatch-style): 'Match the adjectives and their opposites'. Exercise items are JavaScript-rendered and NOT extractable via WebFetch; only title, instruction and nav links are visible.
pages.skills_tested: Antonym-pair recognition for common A1 adjectives (vocabulary, not morphology). Item-level detail unavailable.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Not auditable — matching pairs live in JS arrays stripped by the fetch converter.
pages.steals: Nothing quotable; only the frame 'Match the adjectives and their opposites' was exposed.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page; links back to the site's general adjectives lesson.

pages.label: aggettivi e contrari
pages.format: Matching (JMatch-style): instruction 'Match the adjective to its opposite'. Items JS-rendered, not extractable via WebFetch.
pages.skills_tested: Same skill as Contrari_a1: antonym recognition among A1 adjectives. Apparently a second parallel set of pairs — the site duplicates the format across two pages.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Not auditable (JS-only content).
pages.steals: Nothing quotable beyond the instruction line.
pages.lesson_rules: None; links to lesson-06-adjectives and the exercise index.

pages.label: aggettivi e avverbi
pages.format: Lesson page (rules + example sentences), no interactive exercise. Links back to the general adjectives lesson only.
pages.skills_tested: Irregular comparatives/superlatives of adjectives (buono→migliore→il migliore, cattivo→peggiore→il peggiore) and, crucially, the adjective-vs-adverb split: migliore/peggiore (adjectives) vs meglio/peggio (adverbs), bene vs buono, male vs cattivo — choosing the right word class in context.
pages.traps_and_distractors: The page names its own trap explicitly: 'The adverb forms are also irregular and confusingly similar to the adjectives' — i.e. migliore~meglio, peggiore~peggio confusion is the intended bait for any drill built from this.
pages.steals: 'Questo è un buon libro.' / 'Parli l'italiano bene.' / 'Guidi peggio di un italiano!' — the last one is a nice comparative-adverb frame (guidare + peggio/meglio + di).
pages.lesson_rules: Quoted lists: 'buono – good, migliore – better, il migliore – the best'; 'bene – well, meglio – better'; 'male – badly, peggio – worse'; plus the warning that adverb forms are 'confusingly similar to the adjectives'.

pages.label: aggettivi possessivi lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: explanatory text + full forms table (article included) for all persons, singular/plural x masculine/feminine (il mio/la mia/i miei/le mie ... il loro). No embedded exercises.
pages.skills_tested: Gender/number agreement of possessive with the THING possessed (not the possessor); obligatory article before possessives; the singular-family-noun article drop; loro as the absolute exception (always article, invariable).
pages.traps_and_distractors: N/A (lesson), but the rule set defines the trap hierarchy for drills: (a) agreeing with possessor instead of possessed, (b) dropping/keeping the article wrongly with family nouns, (c) forgetting loro overrides the family-noun exception.
pages.steals: 'il mio cane = my dog, le mie scarpe = my shoes, un mio amico = my friend' — note un mio amico showing indefinite article + possessive, a pattern worth a drill of its own.
pages.lesson_rules: GOLD: (1) 'Always put an article before the possessive adjective.' (2) Family exception: 'when possessive adjectives are used with singular family nouns such as sorella – sister, marito – husband, zia – aunt, nonno – grandfather and so on. In this case no article is used.' (3) 'before the possessive adjective loro, there is ALWAYS an article.' Full paradigm table given with articles baked in.

pages.label: aggettivi possessivi
pages.format: Dropdown gap-fill: instruction 'Choose the correct possessive adjective from the menu:'. Sentences and dropdown options are JS-rendered and not extractable via WebFetch (three attempts).
pages.skills_tested: Selecting the correct possessive form (presumably gender/number agreement and article behaviour, matching its companion A2 lesson). Item-level detail unavailable.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Not auditable; dropdown format implies each gap carries 3-4 competing possessive forms (mio/mia/miei/mie type contrasts).
pages.steals: Nothing quotable beyond the instruction line.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: aggettivi determinativi e relazionali
pages.format: Lesson page: two-section explanation (determinative vs relational adjectives) with derivation examples. No embedded or linked exercises.
pages.skills_tested: Classifying adjective types: determinative (demonstrative/possessive/indefinite/interrogative, pre-nominal) vs relational (noun-derived, post-nominal, literal/objective meaning); recognising relational-adjective suffixes; deriving adjectives from nouns.
pages.traps_and_distractors: N/A (lesson). The implied trap for drills: putting a relational adjective before the noun (una nazionale squadra*) — the page flags post-nominal position as the norm 'although there are exceptions'.
pages.steals: Derivation triplets worth reusing as items: 'nazione>nazionale', 'arte>artistico', 'sole>solare'.
pages.lesson_rules: GOLD suffix list for relational adjectives: '-ale, -are, -istico, -ista, -ano, -oso, -aro, -ico, -ato, -ivo'. Rules: determinatives 'can usually be found before the noun'; relationals are 'normally placed after the noun they refer to (although there are exceptions)' and 'derived from nouns and therefore they generally convey an objective, literal meaning'.

pages.label: posizione degli aggettivi lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: rules + minimal-pair examples. No embedded exercises.
pages.skills_tested: Position-determines-meaning: figurative sense → pre-nominal, literal sense → post-nominal; ordering when stacking two adjectives (both literal → both after; one figurative + one literal → figurative before, literal after).
pages.traps_and_distractors: The lesson's own minimal pair defines the trap: same adjective, two positions, two meanings — drills should bait the wrong position for the intended meaning.
pages.steals: 'Un pover'uomo = figurative use, expresses our sympathy' vs 'Un uomo povero = literal use, the man has no money' — a perfect minimal pair. Also stacked-adjective frames: 'un pover'uomo italiano', 'una bella donna mediterranea' (figurative before + literal/relational after).
pages.lesson_rules: GOLD: 'If the meaning of the adjective is figurative, it comes before the noun. Whereas if the meaning of the adjective is literal, it comes after the noun.' + 'If we are using more than one qualifying adjective, both with literal meanings, they are placed after the noun.' + mixed case: 'figurative adjective before the noun and the literal one after it.' (Note: page does NOT give the classic povero/grande/vecchio/certo/unico change-of-meaning word-list — only povero is exemplified; that list would have to come from elsewhere.)

pages.label: posizione degli aggettivi
pages.format: Matching: title 'Position Of Adjectives - Matching Exercise', instruction 'Match the phrases according to their meaning'. Items JS-rendered, not extractable.
pages.skills_tested: Format is telling even without items: learners match NP-with-adjective-position to its meaning gloss (e.g. presumably 'un vecchio amico' ↔ 'a long-standing friend' type pairs) — i.e. it tests semantic interpretation of position, not production. Pairs with the B2 position lesson.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Not auditable, but the matching-by-meaning design implies each phrase pair (adj-before vs adj-after) is present with two competing glosses — the cross-match is the trap.
pages.steals: The task design itself is the steal: 'Match the phrases according to their meaning' — position minimal pairs vs meaning glosses is a strong comprehension-side drill format for Linguics.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: aggettivi di relazione irregolari
pages.format: Matching: title 'Adjectives From Nouns', instruction 'Match the nouns to their corresponding adjectives'. Items JS-rendered, not extractable.
pages.skills_tested: Noun → irregular (suppletive/Latinate) relational adjective mapping — the cases where the adjective is not transparently derived from the noun (the fegato→epatico, cavallo→equino, latte→lattiero class). Item list unavailable.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Not auditable; in this format the natural distractors are the other adjectives in the same matching pool, so semantically-near Latinate adjectives compete.
pages.steals: Task frame only: noun-to-Latinate-adjective matching at C1 is a distinct micro-topic worth replicating with a curated pair list.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: aggettivi indefiniti
pages.format: Gap-fill: title 'Indefinite Adjectives - Gapfill Exercise', instruction 'Complete the sentences using qualunque, chiunque, ciascuno or nessuno'. Sentences JS-rendered, not extractable.
pages.skills_tested: The instruction reveals the exact micro-skill: a FOUR-WAY forced choice among qualunque / chiunque / ciascuno / nessuno — i.e. adjective-vs-pronoun status (chiunque is pronoun-only), person-vs-thing reference, distributive (ciascuno) vs negative (nessuno) vs free-choice (qualunque).
pages.traps_and_distractors: The four options ARE the distractor set for every gap: chiunque baited where qualunque (+ noun) is needed and vice versa is the classic trap this pool creates.
pages.steals: The option set itself: {qualunque, chiunque, ciascuno, nessuno} as a closed gap-fill bank is a ready-made Linguics drill spec.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page; links to the indefinite-pronouns lesson.

pages.label: pronomi e aggettivi indefiniti
pages.format: Lesson page: word-by-word definitions with example sentences, split into a pronouns section and an adjectives section. No embedded exercises; links back to lesson 44 (indefinite pronouns).
pages.skills_tested: Pronoun vs adjective status of each indefinite; person vs place reference (qualcuno=people, dovunque/ovunque=places); qualche + obligatory SINGULAR noun; qualche vs alcuni quantity nuance; qualsiasi/qualunque position flexibility (before or after noun); the subjunctive trigger rule for chiunque/qualunque/dovunque.
pages.traps_and_distractors: N/A (lesson), but it hands you the traps: qualche + plural noun* (blocked by the singular rule); indicative after chiunque/qualunque/dovunque* (blocked by the subjunctive rule); qualcuno used for things.
pages.steals: 'Qualsiasi cosa faccia, la fa sempre con grande impegno.' and 'Chiunque sia, digli che non ci sono.' — compact subjunctive-trigger frames. Also 'Nessuno studente ha superato la prova...' (nessuno as adjective + singular noun).
pages.lesson_rules: GOLD: (1) 'Qualche... indicates an indefinite – but not large – quantity. Note that the following noun is always singular.' (2) 'qualunque/qualsiasi, chiunque, dovunque/ovunque in a subordinate sentence need to be followed by a subjunctive.' (3) 'Qualsiasi/Qualunque can be used before or after the noun.' (4) Word-role list: pronouns = qualcuno/a, chiunque, dovunque/ovunque, ognuno, nulla/niente; adjectives = qualche, qualsiasi/qualunque, alcuno/a/i/e, ogni, nessuno/a.

pages.label: indefiniti, pronomi e aggettivi
pages.format: Dropdown gap-fill: title 'Indefinite Pronouns & Adjectives - Dropdown Exercise', instruction 'Choose the best option from the dropdown menu:'. Items JS-rendered, not extractable.
pages.skills_tested: Companion exercise to the C1 lesson above — choosing among indefinites per sentence context (presumably testing the singular-after-qualche, subjunctive-trigger and pronoun/adjective distinctions the lesson teaches). Item detail unavailable.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Not auditable; dropdown format implies per-gap option sets of near-synonym indefinites.
pages.steals: Nothing quotable beyond the instruction line.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: pronomi dimostrativi
pages.format: Lesson page organised as four register-based groups of demonstrative pronouns, each with example sentences. No exercises linked.
pages.skills_tested: Register-sensitive demonstratives, not the basic questo/quello paradigm: (1) literary subject pronouns quegli/questi (singular 'the former/the latter person'); (2) bureaucratic/legal medesimo, sottoscritto; (3) archaic/pejorative costui, costei, costoro, coloro; (4) holophrastic questo ('almeno questo è...', 'ma questo non...').
pages.traps_and_distractors: N/A (lesson). Implied trap: reading literary 'questi' as plural — it is a SINGULAR subject pronoun ('Lasciatemi in pace!' urlò questi).
pages.steals: '"Non ne so niente!" rispose quegli risoluto.' and 'Il medesimo ne ha rilasciato testimonianza.' — rare authentic frames for C2 register items. Also 'Costoro non dovrebbero parlare in sua presenza in questo modo' (pejorative costoro).
pages.lesson_rules: Organising rule quoted: 'These pronouns can be divided into four groups according to their meanings' — groups: literary (quegli, questi), bureaucratic/legal/scientific (medesimo, sottoscritto), archaic-with-negative-connotation (costui, costei, coloro/costoro), and holophrastic questo. No forms tables given; basic questo/quello paradigm is NOT covered here.

batch_tips: All seven /free_italian_exercises/*.html pages are Hot Potatoes-style JS exercises: WebFetch's HTML-to-markdown conversion strips the script arrays holding items/options, and direct HTTP to onlineitalianclub.com is blocked by this session's egress policy (403 on CONNECT), so item-level auditing of those pages needs a JS-rendering fetch or an archive mirror. Titles and instruction lines WERE recoverable and often reveal the exact option bank (e.g. aggettiviindefiniti.html: 'complete using qualunque, chiunque, ciascuno or nessuno').

The site's pattern in this topic area: WordPress lesson pages (/free-italian-exercises-and-resources/...) carry all the rules and word-lists; the .html pages are thin drill companions. For Linguics, mine the lesson pages for rule content and reconstruct the drills from the instruction lines + lesson rules.

The B2 position lesson conspicuously lacks the standard change-of-meaning adjective list (grande/vecchio/certo/unico/semplice/diverso...); it only exemplifies povero. Any Linguics position bucket should supply that list from another source — this site won't give it.

Instruction lines double as drill specs: 'Match the phrases according to their meaning' (position minimal pairs vs glosses) and the closed four-option indefinite gap-fill are both directly reusable item formats.
new_micro_topics: migliore vs meglio / peggiore vs peggio (adjective vs adverb forced choice)
article + possessive: singular family noun drop vs plural family nouns vs modified family nouns
loro overrides the family-noun article drop (il loro fratello)
indefinite article + possessive (un mio amico pattern)
qualche + obligatory singular noun vs alcuni + plural
chiunque vs qualunque: pronoun-only vs adjective, person vs free-choice
subjunctive after chiunque / qualunque / dovunque / ovunque in subordinate clauses
four-way indefinite forced choice: qualunque / chiunque / ciascuno / nessuno
adjective position changes meaning: figurative-before vs literal-after (pover'uomo vs uomo povero)
stacking two adjectives: figurative before + literal after the noun
relational adjectives: noun-derived, post-nominal, suffix set -ale/-are/-istico/-ista/-ano/-oso/-aro/-ico/-ato/-ivo
irregular (Latinate) noun-to-relational-adjective mapping (fegato→epatico class)
C2 register demonstratives: quegli/questi (literary singular), costui/costei/costoro (pejorative), medesimo/sottoscritto (bureaucratic), holophrastic questo

pages.label: avverbi in -mente lesson
pages.format: Lesson page (rules + examples), no interactive items; links to the A1 practice exercise.
pages.skills_tested: Understanding that -mente attaches to the FEMININE SINGULAR adjective form (lento -> lenta -> lentamente); that -e adjectives take -mente unchanged (felice -> felicemente); that -ile/-ale adjectives drop the final -e before -mente (suffix goes on the root); and that adverbs are invariable and modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs.
pages.traps_and_distractors: None (no exercise). Implied trap for authoring: learners attaching -mente to the masculine form (*lentomente) or failing to truncate -ile/-ale (*facilemente instead of facilmente).
pages.steals: The three-step derivation display 'lento -> lenta -> lentamente' contrasted with 'felice -> felice -> felicemente'; example sentence 'Lui parla chiaramente (He speaks clearly)'.
pages.lesson_rules: Rule 1: 'Adverbs are formed by adding the suffix -mente to the feminine singular form of the adjective.' Rule 2: 'If the adjective ends in -ile or -ale, you simply add the suffix to the root of the adjective' (i.e. drop the -e: facile -> facilmente, speciale -> specialmente). Caveat stated: 'not all the adjectives can turn into adverbs and not all the adverbs are derived from adjectives.' Definition: an adverb is an invariable part of the sentence that modifies how an action is done or specifies something about a verb, an adjective or another adverb.

pages.label: avverbi
pages.format: Type-in transformation drill (8 items): given an adjective, type the -mente adverb. Instruction: 'Form the correct adverb, as in the example: dolce - dolcemente'. Items are JS-rendered (invisible in plain fetch; recovered via rendered fetch).
pages.skills_tested: Pure morphological transformation adjective -> adverb: masculine -o adjectives requiring the feminine stem (caldo -> caldamente, freddo -> freddamente, noioso -> noiosamente, allegro -> allegramente, leggero -> leggermente, pesante -> pesantemente) vs -e adjectives taking -mente directly (felice -> felicemente, triste -> tristemente). Note leggero -> leggermente is itself a mild irregular (leggera -> leggermente, with e).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Free-production so no listed distractors, but the item set deliberately mixes -o class (needs -a-) with -e class (no change): felice, caldo, triste, freddo, noioso, allegro, leggero, pesante. Predictable wrong answers: *caldomente, *felicemente misspelled, *leggeramente (the standard form is leggermente).
pages.steals: Item list worth reusing wholesale: felice, caldo, triste, freddo, noioso, allegro, leggero, pesante (4 -o class, 3 -e class, 1 semi-irregular leggero).
pages.lesson_rules: Only the worked example 'dolce - dolcemente'; rules live on the companion lesson page.

pages.label: il senso degli avverbi 1
pages.format: Matching exercise, 8 pairs: match adverb to Italian paraphrase of its meaning ('Match the adverbs to the meaning'). Items JS-rendered; recovered via rendered fetch.
pages.skills_tested: Semantic knowledge of high-frequency sentence/degree adverbs, tested Italian-to-Italian (no English): purtroppo = sfortunatamente; volentieri = accetto con piacere; spesso = di frequente; abbastanza = a sufficienza; parecchio = molto; giustamente = in modo onesto, corretto; personalmente = secondo la mia opinione; davvero = veramente.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Distractor pressure comes from near-synonym crowding within the same set: abbastanza vs parecchio (both degree words, definitions 'a sufficienza' vs 'molto'); davvero vs giustamente (both could tempt toward 'veramente'); personalmente baited toward literal 'in person' reading instead of 'secondo la mia opinione'.
pages.steals: The pair 'personalmente = secondo la mia opinione' (opinion-marker reading, not physical presence) and 'parecchio = molto' vs 'abbastanza = a sufficienza' as a minimal degree contrast.
pages.lesson_rules: None stated; the definition set itself doubles as a paraphrase glossary worth keeping.

pages.label: il senso degli avverbi 2
pages.format: Matching exercise, 8 pairs: adverb to Italian definition. Items JS-rendered; recovered via rendered fetch with answer key.
pages.skills_tested: Meaning of time/manner adverbs including two classic false friends: attualmente = 'al momento, di questo periodo' (NOT 'actually'); presto = 'in anticipo, rapidamente'; plus probabilmente = 'che puo accadere, che e quasi sicuro'; intorno = 'nella zona circostante'; quasi = 'circa, un po' meno'; esattamente = 'in modo preciso, con esattezza'; ora = 'adesso'; stranamente = 'in modo bizzarro'.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Temporal near-synonyms packed together to force fine discrimination: ora ('adesso') vs attualmente ('al momento, di questo periodo') vs presto ('in anticipo') — an English speaker mapping actually/now/soon will cross-wire these. quasi vs intorno both flirt with 'approximately'.
pages.steals: 'Attualmente = al momento, di questo periodo' as an explicit anti-false-friend item; the ora/attualmente/presto three-way contrast within one item set.
pages.lesson_rules: None stated.

pages.label: locuzioni avverbiali
pages.format: Lesson page: definition plus a glossed word-list of 18 adverb phrases; one example sentence; no exercise items.
pages.skills_tested: Recognition of multi-word fixed adverbial units functioning as single grammatical elements.
pages.traps_and_distractors: None (lesson). Authoring note: the list contains a built-in antonym pair (al dettaglio 'retail' vs all'ingrosso 'wholesale') and a near-synonym pair (a poco a poco vs poco alla volta) that make natural distractors for each other.
pages.steals: The full 18-item glossary: fino ad ora (until now), all'insaputa (without sb's knowledge), a crepapelle (laugh till one's sides ache), alla cieca (blindly), di malavoglia (reluctantly), a poco a poco (inch by inch), a squarciagola (at the top of one's voice), alla buona (folksy/simple), in seguito (later), per tempo (in time), or ora (just now), in fretta e furia (with breathless haste), in gran parte (mostly), a bizzeffe (abundantly), poco alla volta (piecemeal), al dettaglio (retail), all'ingrosso (wholesale), alla rinfusa (higgledy-piggledy). Example sentence: 'Ho conosciuto un uomo all'antica.'
pages.lesson_rules: Definition stated: 'Adverb phrases are units formed by two or more words which create an adverbial meaning.' The 18-phrase list above is the page's real asset and is exactly the pool the C2 exercise draws from.

pages.label: locuzioni avverbiali
pages.format: Dropdown gap-fill, 12 sentences ('Choose the best option from the dropdown menu:'). Sentences recovered via rendered fetch; the dropdown option lists themselves are injected client-side and were not recoverable, but gaps map cleanly onto the C1 lesson's 18-phrase list.
pages.skills_tested: Choosing the idiomatically correct fixed locuzione for a context: per tempo (booking ahead: 'Ti conviene prenotare ___ se vuoi cenare in quel ristorante: e sempre molto affollato'), in gran parte (statistics), in fretta e furia (packing while late), or ora ('Siamo arrivati ___ da Milano'), all'insaputa dei genitori (secret party), di malavoglia + al contrario (disliked activity contrast), a squarciagola (singing at a concert), in seguito (per ora... ___ business letter register), alla buona (unpretentious people vs snob e arroganti), a poco a poco / poco alla volta (students finishing gradually), alla rinfusa (messy room: 'tutto e gettato ___ e niente e in ordine'), a bizzeffe (old clothes in the attic: 'ce ne sono ___').
pages.traps_and_distractors: Dropdowns almost certainly draw cross-distractors from the same 18-phrase pool; several gaps are semantically close enough to confuse (per tempo vs per ora vs or ora; a poco a poco vs poco alla volta; in gran parte vs a bizzeffe for quantity). Sentence 8 explicitly pairs 'Per ora' in-text with gap answer 'in seguito', baiting the per-tempo/per-ora confusion.
pages.steals: 'La vostra camera e un casino: tutto e gettato [alla rinfusa] e niente e in ordine' (visual, memorable); 'Se vai in soffitta di vecchi abiti ce ne sono [a bizzeffe]' (tests postposed quantity locuzione with ne).
pages.lesson_rules: None on the exercise page; relies on the C1 lesson list.

pages.label: comparativo di bene/male, buono/cattivo
pages.format: Word-bank cloze: continuous narrative text with 15 gaps, bank of 15 forms given ('Complete the text with one of the following:'). Bank: buoni - migliore - bene - buonissime - meglio - ottima - ottimi - cattivo - pessima - malissimo - male - peggio - pessimo - peggiori - cattivo.
pages.skills_tested: The full good/bad system in one exercise: adjective vs adverb choice (buono vs bene, cattivo vs male); irregular comparatives migliore/peggiore (adj) vs meglio/peggio (adv); irregular absolute superlatives ottimo/pessimo vs regular buonissimo/malissimo; gender-number agreement of the adjectival forms (buonissime, ottima, ottimi, peggiori); relative superlative frame 'la cuoca migliore del mondo'.
pages.traps_and_distractors: The word bank IS the distractor set: (a) adjective/adverb morphological confusion (buoni vs bene, pessimo vs malissimo — malissimo is the adverbial 'very badly', pessimo the adjectival 'very bad'); (b) agreement traps via inflected variants in the bank (ottima/ottimi/buonissime/peggiori must land on matching nouns); (c) comparative vs superlative overlap (migliore vs ottima); (d) 'cattivo' appears TWICE in the bank, signalling two slots and punishing single-use bookkeeping; (e) adverb gradation meglio vs peggio anchored by discourse ('e andata molto [bene]... e andato ancora [peggio]').
pages.steals: Gap frames worth stealing: '(1) la cuoca ___ del mondo' [migliore — relative superlative without piu], '(6) e andata molto ___ / (7) e andato ancora ___' [bene then peggio — 'ancora peggio' escalation], '(11) si prepara sempre molto ___' [bene — adverb after reflexive verb where an adjective is tempting].
pages.lesson_rules: None stated on page; exercise presumes the migliore/meglio, peggiore/peggio, ottimo/pessimo, buonissimo/malissimo paradigm.

pages.label: comparativo e superlativo
pages.format: Gap-fill, 8 sentences; instruction: 'Complete the sentences using di, an articulated form of di, or che:'. Items recovered via rendered fetch.
pages.skills_tested: The di/che selection rules, one per item: di before a proper name ('ha corso meno velocemente [di] Dario'); che between two nouns with the same function ('piu studentesse [che] studenti'); articulated di before article ('piu comodo [del] tuo letto'; 'meno lungo [del] fiume Po'); che before an infinitive ('meno difficile scalare una montagna [che] farlo ragionare'); che between two adjectives of one referent ('piu vivace [che] intelligente'); che before a prepositional phrase ('piu spesso con le sue amiche [che] con il suo ragazzo'); di before a stressed pronoun ('piu medaglie [di] me').
pages.traps_and_distractors: Three-way forced choice (di / articulated di / che). Traps: item 3 and 7 bait bare 'di' where the following article demands del; items 5-6 bait 'di' where the second term shares the verb (two qualities, repeated preposition -> che); item 4 baits 'di' before an infinitive; comparing quantities of people (item 2) baits 'di' but needs che.
pages.steals: 'Il cane di mio zio e senza dubbio piu vivace ___ intelligente!' (che — two adjectives, same noun) and 'piu spesso con le sue amiche ___ con il suo ragazzo' (che — repeated preposition), the two rules learners fail most.
pages.lesson_rules: None stated, but the 8 items systematically encode the full rulebook: di + name/pronoun; di + article -> articulated; che before infinitives, prepositions, second adjectives, and paired nouns.

pages.label: superlativo relativo e assoluto
pages.format: Binary classification exercise, 6 sentences: 'Is the meaning of the superlative form relative (REL) or absolute (ASS)?'. Items recovered via rendered fetch.
pages.skills_tested: Distinguishing superlativo relativo (piu + adj + di/tra group frame) from assoluto, INCLUDING non--issimo absolute forms: adjective reduplication ('Il suo gattino era tenero tenero!!' = ASS), prefixation ('Sono arcistufo di tutte le tue lamentele!!' = ASS with arci-), -issimo ('gli spaghetti... sono buonissimi!!' = ASS), vs REL frames 'Tra le signore presenti alla cena Anna era la piu elegante', 'Marco e lo studente piu diligente della sua classe', 'il mestiere piu bello del mondo'.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Trap 1: 'arcistufo' — arci- prefix as absolute superlative, unfamiliar surface form. Trap 2: 'tenero tenero' — doubling as superlative, no morphology at all. Trap 3: item 4 contains 'di tutte le tue lamentele', a di-phrase that mimics the relative-superlative group frame but is just the complement of stufo — punishes pattern-matching on 'di' alone. Trap 4: REL items vary the frame word (tra vs di vs del mondo).
pages.steals: 'Sono arcistufo di tutte le tue lamentele!!' (ASS despite the di-phrase) and 'Il suo gattino era tenero tenero!!' (reduplication = assoluto) — both perfect for a same-form/different-mechanism drill.
pages.lesson_rules: Implicit taxonomy encoded by the items: absolute superlative realised three ways (-issimo, prefix arci-/stra-, adjective doubling); relative superlative = definite article + piu + adjective + group phrase (tra.../della.../del mondo).

batch_tips: All the interactive exercises on this site are JS-rendered (Hot Potatoes style): a plain fetch returns only the title and instruction line. Route item extraction through a rendering reader (r.jina.ai worked, though heavily rate-limited, ~1 request per few minutes) or budget for manual capture; plan the whole batch around this.

The site's design pattern worth copying: lesson page holds the rule + exception/word list, exercise page holds only items — and the C2 dropdown exercise draws its answers AND distractors from the exact 18-phrase list on the C1 lesson page. Pairing a closed word-list lesson with a cross-distractor exercise is cheap to author and effective.

Best distractor engineering seen in this batch: the A2 bene/male word-bank cloze, where the bank itself is the distractor set (adjective vs adverb, agreement variants, and a deliberately duplicated 'cattivo'). Word-bank duplication to signal multiple slots is a steal-worthy mechanic.

The es1 di/che exercise is a model of rule-per-item coverage: 8 items, each isolating exactly one selection rule (name, pronoun, article, infinitive, second adjective, repeated preposition, paired nouns). Atomises almost directly into Linguics buckets.

Italian-to-Italian paraphrase matching (senso degli avverbi 1-2) quietly targets false friends (attualmente, presto) without ever mentioning English — a nice B1 pattern: bury the false friend among genuine temporal near-synonyms (ora/adesso/presto/attualmente).
new_micro_topics: -mente formation: feminine-stem rule (-o class) vs unchanged -e class, plus -ile/-ale truncation (facile -> facilmente) and the irregular leggero -> leggermente
adjective vs adverb choice in the good/bad system: buono/bene, cattivo/male, migliore/meglio, peggiore/peggio, ottimo vs malissimo (adjectival vs adverbial absolute superlatives)
gender-number agreement of comparative/superlative adjectives (ottima/ottimi/buonissime/peggiori) inside cloze contexts
di vs che selection: di + name/pronoun, articulated di + article, che + infinitive, che + repeated preposition, che between two adjectives of one referent, che between paired nouns
relative superlative without piu marker on the surface: 'la cuoca migliore del mondo' (irregular migliore fills the piu+buono slot)
superlativo assoluto realised without -issimo: adjective reduplication (tenero tenero) and intensifying prefixes (arcistufo, and by extension stra-/ultra-)
REL vs ASS classification when a misleading di-phrase follows an absolute form (arcistufo DI tutte le tue lamentele)
sentence-adverb paraphrase pairs, Italian-to-Italian: purtroppo=sfortunatamente, personalmente=secondo la mia opinione, parecchio=molto vs abbastanza=a sufficienza
temporal adverb false friends and near-synonyms: attualmente (=al momento, not 'actually'), presto (=in anticipo/rapidamente), ora=adesso, quasi vs intorno

fixed locuzioni avverbiali as a closed C1/C2 list: per tempo, or ora, in seguito vs per ora, in fretta e furia, a squarciagola, alla rinfusa, a bizzeffe, all'insaputa di, di malavoglia, alla buona, a poco a poco vs poco alla volta, al dettaglio vs all'ingrosso

pages.label: essere
pages.format: Multiple-choice gap-fill (Hot Potatoes dropdown), 6 items, instruction 'Choose the correct form of the verb'. Each blank offers 3 conjugated forms of essere.
pages.skills_tested: Present-tense essere across persons with varied subject types: implicit io after a preamble ('Mi chiamo Irina e ___ russa' -> sono); inanimate 3sg subjects (a school, a city: 'Parigi ___ la capitale' -> e); noi -> siamo; voi in an inverted question ('Voi di dove ___?' -> siete); compound proper-name subject -> 3pl ('Ana e Raquel ___ due ragazze spagnole' -> sono). Tests subject identification more than form memorisation.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Exploits the sono ambiguity (1sg AND 3pl) by putting 'sono' among distractors for 3sg items (q2 options: sono/siete/e; q6: sei/sono/e). Person-adjacent distractors (siamo vs sono for noi; sei vs siete). Implicit-subject items bait learners who match the nearest noun ('Irina... russa' baits e).
pages.steals: 'Mi chiamo Irina e ___ sono russa.' (recover io from mi chiamo); 'Ana e Raquel ___ due ragazze spagnole.' [sono] (two names = loro); 'Voi di dove ___?' [siete] (inverted question word order).
pages.lesson_rules: None on page; links out to a general Italian verbs lesson.

pages.label: avere
pages.format: Multiple-choice gap-fill (Hot Potatoes dropdown), 6 items, 'Choose the correct form of the verb'. (Dropdown options not recoverable through the fetch, sentences are.)
pages.skills_tested: Present-tense avere with heavy use of avere-idioms where English uses 'to be': age ('Sara e Alina ___ venticinque anni' -> hanno; 'Mi chiamo Marco e ___ trentadue anni' -> ho), thirst ('Se ___ sete, bevi un bicchiere d'acqua!' -> hai), desire ('Se voi ___ voglia di uscire' -> avete), plus plain possession negated ('Noi non ___ una macchina' -> abbiamo). Also implicit-subject recovery (io from 'Mi chiamo Marco').
pages.traps_and_distractors: Item 4 is 'Voi di dove ___?' -- an essere sentence planted inside the avere drill, baiting avete when siete is required; it forces essere-vs-avere discrimination, not just conjugation. The age/thirst/voglia idioms bait anglophone 'to be' transfer. 'Sara e Alina ___ venticinque anni e sono della Bulgaria' pairs hanno and sono in one sentence, contrasting the two verbs.
pages.steals: 'Mi chiamo Marco e ___ trentadue anni.' [ho] (age idiom + implicit subject); 'Voi di dove ___?' [siete] embedded in an avere drill (cross-verb trap worth copying); 'Se voi ___ voglia di uscire, andiamo insieme a bere qualcosa.' [avete].
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: essere o avere?
pages.format: Gap-fill, 8 items: 'Complete the sentences with the correct form of the auxiliary verb essere or avere'. Learner must both pick the auxiliary AND conjugate it (e/sono/ho/hai/ha) for passato prossimo.
pages.skills_tested: Auxiliary selection focused almost entirely on the dual-auxiliary class cominciare/iniziare/ricominciare/finire/smettere: avere when a person starts/stops doing something ('Io ___ smesso di fumare' -> ho; 'tu quando ___ cominciato a studiare' -> hai; 'La testa ___ cominciato a farmi male' -> ha), essere when the thing itself begins/restarts ('Le lezioni ___ ricominciate' -> sono, with -e agreement; 'I problemi ___ iniziati' -> sono; 'Il film ___ gia iniziato' -> e). One movement verb ('Matthew ___ arrivato' -> e) and one impersonal weather item ('___ finito di piovere solo qualche minuto fa'). Past-participle agreement (ricominciatE, iniziatI) signals the auxiliary.
pages.traps_and_distractors: 'La testa ha cominciato a farmi male' is the killer item: inanimate subject baits essere, but cominciare a + infinitive keeps avere. Conversely 'Il film e gia iniziato' baits avere because the same verb took avere two items earlier. Participle endings (-e, -i) are the giveaway learners miss.
pages.steals: 'La testa ___ cominciato a farmi male questa mattina.' [ha]; 'Sbrigati! Il film ___ gia iniziato da cinque minuti!' [e]; 'Le lezioni di italiano ___ ricominciate la prima settimana di gennaio.' [sono, agreement clue in -e].
pages.lesson_rules: None stated; the drill implicitly teaches: same verb, avere with an object/di+infinitive, essere when the subject is what begins/ends.

pages.label: c'e/ci sono lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: rules plus example sentences, no exercises.
pages.skills_tested: Teaches c'e (singular) vs ci sono (plural) as 'there is/are' stating 'the presence or existence of someone or something'; negation; use in questions.
pages.traps_and_distractors: N/A (lesson page).
pages.steals: 'C'e troppo zucchero nel mio caffe' (uncountable -> singular); 'Non ci sono penne nell'astuccio' (negated plural); question form 'C'e qualcuno in cucina?' answered 'Non c'e nessuno in cucina' (qualcuno/nessuno both singular).
pages.lesson_rules: Stated rules: (1) c'e is 'the short form of ci e', ci sono is 'the plural form'; (2) the pronoun ci 'refers to previously mentioned places and things in order not to repeat them'; (3) negation: 'you just need to put the particle NON before c'e / ci sono'. Note qualcuno/nessuno are modelled as singular even though they refer to people.

pages.label: c'e o ci sono
pages.format: Binary multiple choice (c'e vs ci sono), 12 gap-fill sentences, 'Choose the best option'.
pages.skills_tested: Number agreement with the postposed subject across noun-phrase types: singular indefinite ('Nella stanza ___ un tavolo'); plural quantified ('___ tanti ragazzi alla festa', 'molti esercizi', 'molti turisti', 'molti studenti'); mass nouns -> singular ('molto traffico', 'il latte'); collective singular 'molta gente' (semantically plural, grammatically singular); plural definite ('Nella torta ___ le mele'); singular person ('qualcuno alla porta'); negation ('Purtroppo non ___ il latte per colazione').
pages.traps_and_distractors: The molto/molti minimal pair is the core trap: 'molta gente' and 'molto traffico' bait ci sono because the meaning is plural/large; only the article/adjective ending disambiguates. 'le mele' after singular 'torta' baits c'e (nearest-noun error).
pages.steals: '___ molta gente al mercato il sabato mattina.' [c'e] (gente = singular collective); 'Nella torta ___ le mele.' [ci sono]; 'Purtroppo non ___ il latte per colazione.' [c'e] (negation + mass noun).
pages.lesson_rules: None on page; links to the c'e/ci sono lesson.

pages.label: c'e o ci sono 1
pages.format: Binary multiple choice (c'e vs ci sono), 12 gap-fill sentences, 'Choose the best option'. All framed as place facts ('In classe...', 'A Bologna...', 'In Finlandia...').
pages.skills_tested: Same singular/plural agreement, but via different NP types: explicit numerals ('___ tre banchi', 'circa cinquanta studenti' -> ci sono); mass/abstract nouns ('molta umidita', 'tanta neve' -> c'e); plural named landmarks ('le Due Torri' -> ci sono); singular definites/superlatives ('la piu antica Universita', 'il cinema' -> c'e); collective singular ('una grande comunita indiana' -> c'e).
pages.traps_and_distractors: 'A Londra ___ una grande comunita indiana' baits ci sono (a community = many people, and 'indiana/comunita' feel plural-ish); 'tanta neve' and 'molta umidita' bait ci sono via the 'large quantity = plural' fallacy; 'circa cinquanta studenti' checks that approximation words don't change agreement.
pages.steals: 'A Londra ___ una grande comunita indiana' [c'e] (collective noun trap); 'In Russia ___ tanta neve.' [c'e] vs 'In Finlandia ___ molti laghi.' [ci sono] as a minimal contrast pair on quantity words.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page; links back to the c'e/ci sono lesson.

pages.label: c'e o ci sono 2
pages.format: Binary multiple choice (c'e vs ci sono), 12 gap-fill sentences, 'Choose the best option'. Geography/society facts frame.
pages.skills_tested: Extends agreement to abstract mass nouns ('molto disordine', 'molto traffico', 'molta criminalita', 'molta povertà' -> c'e); coordinated singular nouns -> plural ('Nel cappuccino ___ latte e caffe' -> ci sono); plural definites ('le Piramidi', 'i pinguini', 'le renne', 'molte persone obese', 'tanti motorini' -> ci sono); singular institutions/features ('il Parlamento', 'la foresta amazzonica' -> c'e).
pages.traps_and_distractors: 'latte e caffe' is the standout trap: two singular mass nouns joined by e require ci sono -- catches learners who apply the mass-noun-singular rule word-by-word. The abstract mass nouns (povertà, criminalita) bait ci sono because they describe widespread phenomena. 'la foresta amazzonica' (huge thing) vs 'i pinguini' checks size does not equal plurality.
pages.steals: 'Nel cappuccino ___ latte e caffe.' [ci sono] (coordination overrides mass-noun singularity); 'In Africa ___ molta povertà.' [c'e] (abstract mass noun).
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: lei, first meetings
pages.format: Lesson page: rule explanation plus parallel informal/formal dialogue pairs organised by function (greeting, name, origin, residence, spelling, age, job, farewell). No exercises.
pages.skills_tested: Register switching: rendering the same first-meeting question in tu-form and Lei-form; recognising 3rd-person verb endings as formality markers (stai->sta, chiami->chiama, sei->e, vivi->vive, hai->ha, fai->fa); possessive shift tuo->suo; register-matched greetings/farewells (Ciao vs Buongiorno; 'Ciao, alla prossima volta!' vs 'Arrivederla, spero di vederla presto!').
pages.traps_and_distractors: N/A (lesson page), but the pairs define the trap space for drills: 2sg vs 3sg endings differ by one vowel (stai/sta, hai/ha, fai/fa), and 'Di dove e?' with no expressed subject looks like a 3rd-person question about someone else.
pages.steals: Transformation pairs ready to drill: 'Come ti chiami?' -> 'Lei come si chiama?'; 'Quanti anni hai?' -> 'Quanti anni ha?'; 'Come si scrive il tuo cognome?' -> 'Come si scrive il suo cognome?'; formal farewell 'Arrivederla, spero di vederla presto!' (arrivederla and enclitic -la worth teaching).
pages.lesson_rules: Core rule verbatim: 'In Italian you use the second person form tu (you) when speaking to someone you know or someone of your own age or younger, but the third person form lei (he/she) when being formal.' Formality = substitute 3rd-person conjugations for 2nd-person ones; possessives shift tuo->suo. English analogy given: like saying 'If Sir would kindly refrain from smoking his cigarettes...'.

batch_tips: The site's exercise pages are Hot Potatoes JS quizzes: plain HTML-to-markdown fetches return only the title and instruction line. Sentences are recoverable via a rendering fetch; dropdown options only via raw HTML (recovered for essere_a1: distractors are always other real conjugations, 3 options per gap, exploiting the sono 1sg/3pl ambiguity).

The site deliberately plants cross-verb traps inside single-verb drills: 'Voi di dove ___?' (needs siete) sits in the AVERE quiz. Steal this pattern: one out-of-paradigm item per drill kills pattern-matching.

All three c'e/ci sono drills are 12-item binary MC; difficulty comes entirely from the noun phrase after the gap. The distractor axis is: mass noun (molto traffico), collective singular (gente, comunita), coordinated singulars (latte e caffe), numeral (tre banchi), plural definite (le mele), negation (non c'e il latte). That list is the atomisation blueprint for existentials.

The A2 essere/avere auxiliary drill skips the usual andare/venire movement verbs entirely and drills the dual-auxiliary class (cominciare, iniziare, ricominciare, finire, smettere) plus one arrivare -- a much higher-yield micro-topic; past-participle agreement (-e/-i) doubles as the answer clue.
Geographic/cultural fact frames ('A Bologna...', 'Al Polo Sud...', 'In Egitto ___ le Piramidi') keep vocabulary A1-safe while varying the target NP, and add mild general-knowledge engagement.
The avere drill leans on avere-idioms where English uses BE (hanno venticinque anni, hai sete, avete voglia) -- anglophone transfer is its main distractor engine.
new_micro_topics: Coordinated singular nouns take ci sono (latte e caffe -> ci sono) even when each noun is mass/singular
Collective and mass nouns take c'e despite plural meaning (gente, comunita, neve, traffico, povertà)
Avere idioms where English uses 'to be': avere X anni, avere sete/fame, avere voglia di
Dual-auxiliary verbs in passato prossimo: cominciare/iniziare/ricominciare/finire/smettere (avere with person+di/a+infinitive or object, essere when the subject itself starts/ends)
Implicit-subject recovery: 'Mi chiamo X e ___ ...' requires 1sg despite no io
tu->Lei transformation pairs for first-meeting questions (stai->sta, ti chiami->si chiama, tuo->suo, ciao->buongiorno/arrivederla)
Negated existentials: non c'e / non ci sono, with nessuno/qualcuno as grammatically singular
sono as deliberately ambiguous distractor (1sg vs 3pl of essere) in conjugation MC

pages.label: presente indicativo 1
pages.format: Hot Potatoes type-in gap-fill, 10 items. Infinitive given in parentheses after/inside the sentence; person occasionally given explicitly, e.g. '(avere, voi)'. Learner types the conjugated form.
pages.skills_tested: Mapping full lexical subjects to person: two proper names -> 3pl ('Mariarosa e Giovanni... andare' -> vanno), 'Io e i miei amici' -> 1pl NOT 3pl, animal subject -> 3sg ('Il cane di Gabriele... amare'). Person recovery from a later clause: 'Quando (dormire) troppo mi sento intontito' -> 'mi sento' forces io -> dormo. Mix of -are/-ere/-ire including the -isc split within one set: dormire (dormo) vs pulire (puliamo, no -isc in noi anyway) vs capire (capiscono).
