Negation does not change form: 'Giovanni e Carlo non (capire)' -> capiscono.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Type-in, so no option lists; traps are built into sentence design: (a) 'Io e i miei amici' baits 3pl organizzano instead of organizziamo; (b) 'Quando dormire troppo' has no visible subject, baiting 3sg dorme until the learner reads 'mi sento'; (c) capire baits capono (no -isc) from learners who over-generalise the dormire pattern; (d) '(avere, voi)' in a question baits 'hanno' since no voi pronoun appears in the sentence.
pages.steals: "Io e i miei amici ___ (organizzare) una festa per festeggiare le nostre lauree." (compound subject with io -> noi). "Quando ___ (dormire) troppo mi sento sempre intontito." (person recoverable only from the reflexive in the second clause). "Giovanni e Carlo non ___ (capire) cosa devono fare a lavoro." (-isc verb, 3pl, under negation).
pages.lesson_rules: None on page; links back to the site's verb lesson only.

pages.label: presente indicativo 2
pages.format: Hot Potatoes type-in gap-fill, 9 items, same 'conjugate the verb in parentheses' pattern, with explicit person hints where the sentence is ambiguous: '(andare – noi)', '(vendere - io)'.
pages.skills_tested: Regular/irregular mix in one drill: partire, scrivere, suonare, abitare, insegnare, vendere alongside irregular venire (3sg viene) and uscire (3sg esce). 'Tu e Sergio' -> voi (abitate). Inanimate 3sg subject placed AFTER a fronted time adverbial: 'La settimana prossima (uscire) il nuovo album dei Coldplay' -> esce, with postverbal subject. Presente used with future time reference ('La settimana prossima Miriam e Alex partono'). Person from discourse: 'Tu sei molto bravo nella musica: (suonare) la chitarra' -> suoni, subject stated only in the preceding clause.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Type-in traps: (a) 'La settimana prossima... il nuovo album' baits a future form or 3pl (dei Coldplay looks plural); (b) 'Tu e Sergio' baits 2sg abiti or 3pl abitano instead of abitate; (c) venire/uscire bait regularised *vene/*usce; (d) fronted adverbial hides that the subject follows the verb.
pages.steals: "La settimana prossima ___ (uscire) il nuovo album dei Coldplay." (irregular esce + inanimate postverbal subject + present-for-future). "Tu e Sergio ___ (abitare) in una zona molto antica della città." (tu+X -> voi).
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: presente indicativo 3
pages.format: Dropdown multiple choice ('Choose the correct conjugation from the menu'), 12 items. Dropdown option lists are injected by JavaScript and were not recoverable in any fetch, only the carrier sentences.
pages.skills_tested: Whole-sentence person identification with no infinitive hint (learner must pick both verb and form from menu): 'Lui ___ Giovanni.' (si chiama, not è); possessive-NP subjects ('I miei amici ___ tutte le sere', 'Mio fratello ___ ingegneria', 'I miei nonni ___ in montagna'); modal-type sapere + infinitive ('Loro ___ cucinare molto bene' -> sanno); volere/potere + infinitive ('Tu ___ venire a casa mia'); vocative -> tu ('Gianna, ___ a fare la spesa?!' -> vai, not va); negative 2pl 'Voi non ___ inglese'.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Option lists unavailable, but sentence design shows the baits: 'Lui ___ Giovanni' baits 'è' vs 'si chiama'; 'Gianna, ___ a fare la spesa?!' baits 3sg 'va' (name looks like a subject but is a vocative); 'Loro ___ cucinare molto bene' baits conjugated cucinano vs sanno+inf; 'Io e la mia famiglia ___ a Parigi' baits 3sg/3pl instead of 1pl.
pages.steals: "Gianna, ___ a fare la spesa?!" (vocative forces tu; cleanest vocative-vs-subject trap in the whole batch). "Lui ___ Giovanni." (chiamarsi vs essere at the very first item).
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: presente verbi regolari
pages.format: Dropdown multiple choice ('Choose the correct option'), 12 items; carrier sentences recovered, dropdown options not recoverable (JS-injected).
pages.skills_tested: Regular-verb agreement with syntactically tricky subjects: postverbal subject in questions ('A che ora ___ tua sorella?' -> 3sg); interrogative chi -> 3sg even with plural object ('Chi ___ i piatti stasera?' -> lava, not lavano); plural inanimate subject ('I prezzi ___ sempre di più' -> aumentano); object NP masquerading as subject ('Come ___ le tue serate?' -> passi tu, 'le tue serate' is the object); da + presente for duration ('Loro ___ l'italiano da molti anni' -> studiano, present not passato); routine 3sg/3pl with adverb sempre ('Giulia ___ sempre sotto la doccia', 'I miei genitori ___ sempre prima delle 20').
pages.traps_and_distractors: Built-in baits: 'Chi ___ i piatti' -> plural object baits lavano; 'Come ___ le tue serate?' -> plural NP baits passano; 'da molti anni' baits a past-tense option if the menu offers one; 'A che ora ___ tua sorella?' baits 2sg (question feels addressed to tu).
pages.steals: "Chi ___ i piatti stasera?" (chi = 3sg despite plural object - reusable trap template). "Loro ___ l'italiano da molti anni." (da+presente duration, pairs presente topic with a tense-choice trap).
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: presente verbi irregolari (hub)
pages.format: Hub/menu page, not itself a quiz: 'Click the links to conjugate these irregular Italian verbs' followed by 10 links, each opening a full-paradigm type-in drill (all six persons of one verb).
pages.skills_tested: Full paradigm production, one verb at a time, for the canonical A1 irregular set: andare, venire, sapere, uscire, fare, dare, dire, dovere, potere, volere.
pages.traps_and_distractors: None on the hub itself; the child drills are pure recall (type all six forms), no distractors.
pages.steals: The ten-verb list itself is the steal: andare, venire, sapere, uscire, fare, dare, dire, dovere, potere, volere - OIC's implicit definition of 'the A1 irregular verbs'. Note volere/dovere/potere are treated as ordinary irregulars here, not separated into a modals unit.
pages.lesson_rules: The 10-verb list functions as an exception word-list: these are the irregulars OIC thinks an A1 learner must conjugate cold.

pages.label: presente verbi irregolari
pages.format: Dropdown multiple choice, 12 items; several sentences have the gap in initial position with NO subject pronoun at all. Options JS-injected, not recoverable.
pages.skills_tested: Irregular forms where person must be recovered from possessives or pragmatics: '___ sempre con i miei amici il sabato sera' -> 1sg (esco) cued only by 'i miei'; '___ finire i compiti prima di uscire con i tuoi amici' -> devi, cued by 'i tuoi'; formal Lei in a question: 'Scusi professore ___ ripetere la domanda?' -> può (Scusi signals Lei); -go/-gono class: 'Loro ___ sempre le soluzioni più difficili' (scelgono), 'Il ministro ___ una nuova legge elettorale' (propone); modal + infinitive: 'Loro non ___ rimanere alla festa' (vogliono/possono); noi with fronted adverbial: 'Ogni mattina noi ___ a scuola a piedi' (andiamo).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Sentence-initial gaps with zero subject are the signature trap: person is recoverable ONLY from a possessive later in the sentence ('i miei' -> io, 'i tuoi' -> tu, 'i vostri' -> voi elsewhere in the series). 'Scusi professore' baits 2sg puoi instead of formal può. scegliere/proporre bait regularised *sceglono/*propona-type forms.
pages.steals: "Scusi professore, ___ ripetere la domanda?" (formal Lei triggered by 'Scusi' - best formal-register item in the batch). "___ sempre con i miei amici il sabato sera." (zero-subject sentence, person from possessive only).
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: presente verbi riflessivi
pages.format: Dropdown multiple choice, 12 items; the gap covers pronoun+verb together (learner selects e.g. 'si svegliano' as a unit). Options JS-injected, not recoverable.
pages.skills_tested: Double agreement (reflexive pronoun AND ending must both match the subject): 'Paolo e Maria ___ tardi stamattina' (si svegliano); person recovery from subordinate clause: 'A che ora ___ quando vai al lavoro?' -> ti alzi (vai forces tu); possessive cue for voi: 'Perché ___ sempre con i vostri figli?' (vi arrabbiate); verbs with prepositional government: ricordarsi DI ('Noi ___ sempre del tuo compleanno' -> ci ricordiamo), preoccuparsi PER ('Maria ___ sempre per i suoi figli'); reciprocal si: 'Loro ___ da molti anni' (si conoscono); body-part construction: 'Marco ___ i capelli due volte al mese' (si taglia i capelli, definite article not possessive); mettersi a + infinitive: 'Appena arriva a casa la mamma ___ a fare le pulizie' (si mette).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Pronoun-mismatch is the evident distractor design (e.g. *si alzi, *ti alza combinations for the tu item); 'Loro ___ da molti anni' baits non-reciprocal readings; 'Marco ___ i capelli' baits a non-reflexive taglia; zero-subject item 6 'Spesso ___ dopo mezzanotte' relies on the option set to fix person.
pages.steals: "A che ora ___ quando vai al lavoro?" (person only from 'vai' in the subordinate clause). "Marco ___ i capelli due volte al mese." (reflexive + body part + definite article - direct template for Linguics riflessivi items).
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: verbi intro
pages.format: Lesson page: prose rules + 10 full conjugation tables + study tips. No embedded exercise.
pages.skills_tested: Reference material, not testing: full paradigms of essere, avere, fare, volere, chiamarsi, parlare, abitare, lavorare, prendere, preferire (note: preferire given with -isc from the very first lesson, and chiamarsi introduces reflexives inside the intro lesson).
pages.traps_and_distractors: N/A (lesson).
pages.steals: The verb SELECTION is the steal: OIC's first-ten verbs for a beginner are essere, avere, fare, volere, chiamarsi, parlare, abitare, lavorare, prendere, preferire - i.e. 4 irregular + 1 reflexive + 3 regular -are + 1 -ere + 1 -isc verb, all before the 'three groups' lesson.
pages.lesson_rules: Rules stated verbatim: infinitives end in '-are, -ere or -ire'; regular verbs 'follow a pattern', irregular verbs 'deviate from the pattern'; "You don't need to use the pronoun 'io' in Italian... unless you want to emphasise who you're talking about... the conjugation of the verb makes it obvious" (pro-drop rule); "if you know how to say 'Vado' (I go), that's good also to mean 'I'm going' or even 'I will go' most of the time" (present covers progressive and near-future); Lei/Loro are formal 'no matter if the third person is male or female'; pronoun list: io - tu - lui - lei - Lei - noi - voi - loro - Loro. Study tip: paper-scrap self-testing, re-test next day and a week later (spaced repetition).

pages.label: verbi regolari, i tre gruppi
pages.format: Lesson page: three-conjugation rule + 4 model paradigms (abitare, perdere, dormire, preferire). No embedded exercise.
pages.skills_tested: Reference: model paradigms for -are (abitare), -ere (perdere), -ire without -isc (dormire), -ire with -isc (preferire).
pages.traps_and_distractors: N/A (lesson).
pages.steals: The four-model layout with dormire and preferire side by side is the key pedagogical move: it makes the -isc split visible as a fourth column rather than a footnote. Model verbs to reuse: abitare/perdere/dormire/preferire.
pages.lesson_rules: Rules verbatim: 'All Italian regular verbs can be divided into three groups, as classified according to the ending of their infinitive forms'; group examples given as word-lists: -are = abitare, mangiare, lavare; -ere = perdere, correre; -ire = dormire, aprire; and the -isc rule: 'some verbs, such as preferire, add the suffix -isc between the root and the declination'. NOTE: OIC gives no list of WHICH -ire verbs take -isc and no rule that -isc drops in noi/voi is stated explicitly (only shown in the table) - a gap Linguics should fill.

pages.label: verbi irregolari
pages.format: Lesson page: 'a list of five more irregular italian verbs' - five full paradigms, minimal prose, link out to an exercise.
pages.skills_tested: Reference: capire (capisco...capiscono), conoscere (conosco/conosci - written-c sound alternation), dovere (devo/dobbiamo/devono - three stems), rimanere (rimango/rimangono - g-insertion in 1sg/3pl only), stare (sto, stai, sta, stiamo, state, stanno).
pages.traps_and_distractors: N/A (lesson).
pages.steals: dovere paradigm quoted in full (devo, devi, deve, dobbiamo, dovete, devono) - the dobbiamo/devono contrast is a ready-made distractor pair. rimanere shows the g-insertion pattern (rimango/rimani/rimane/rimaniamo/rimanete/rimangono) - the 1sg+3pl-only distribution is the testable regularity.
pages.lesson_rules: Notable authoring caveat rather than a rule: OIC classifies capire as 'irregular' (it is a regular -isc verb) - i.e. the site's irregular/-isc boundary is fuzzy and Linguics should not copy this taxonomy. The implicit rule shown by rimanere: -go appears only in io and loro forms.

pages.label: verbi irregolari (esercizio)
pages.format: Hot Potatoes type-in gap-fill, 7 items, infinitive (+ sometimes person) in parentheses.
pages.skills_tested: Harder irregular classes than the A1 set: g-insertion salire ('Molti turisti ogni giorno (salire) sulla Torre di Pisa' -> salgono), -gliere ('Anna e Marco (scegliere)' -> scelgono), -porre ('Le insegnanti (proporre)' -> propongono); compound subjects again: 'Io e Jennifer (fare)' -> facciamo, 'Tu e tua moglie (dare)' -> date; sapere 2sg in a question '(tu, sapere) cosa sta succedendo là fuori?' -> sai; uscire tu cued by discourse contrast ('Io di solito il sabato mangio una pizza... Tu (uscire) il sabato sera?').
pages.traps_and_distractors: Type-in traps: salire baits *salono/*saliscono (learners apply -isc); scegliere baits *scegliono; proporre baits *proporono; 'Tu e tua moglie' baits 2sg dai or 3pl danno instead of date; quantified subject 'Molti turisti' baits 3sg.
pages.steals: "Molti turisti ogni giorno ___ (salire) sulla Torre di Pisa." (salgono - g-insertion with a quantified plural subject). "Le insegnanti ___ (proporre) di fare un esercizio per imparare il congiuntivo." (propongono - -porre class, rare in A-level materials).
pages.lesson_rules: None on page; implicitly extends the irregular canon beyond the ten-verb A1 hub with salire, scegliere, proporre, rimanere-type -go verbs.

batch_tips: Fetch mechanics: the /free_italian_exercises/ pages are Hot Potatoes quizzes whose items live in JavaScript; plain WebFetch returns only headers. Rendering via r.jina.ai prefix recovers the carrier sentences but NEVER the dropdown option lists, so for all OIC multiple-choice pages the distractor sets are unrecoverable and must be inferred from sentence design. Jina rate-limits hard (~1-2 req/min); add a cache-busting query param when retrying because WebFetch caches the 429 for 15 minutes. Direct curl to onlineitalianclub.com is blocked by egress policy.

OIC's house style for person-selection difficulty is consistent and worth systematising in Linguics: (1) compound subjects 'io e X' -> noi and 'tu e X' -> voi appear in almost every gap-fill; (2) zero-subject sentences where person is recoverable only from a possessive (i miei/i tuoi/i vostri) or from a verb in a subordinate clause ('quando vai' -> tu); (3) postverbal subjects after fronted adverbials ('La settimana prossima esce il nuovo album'); (4) vocatives that look like subjects ('Gianna, vai...?'); (5) chi -> 3sg with a plural object bait ('Chi lava i piatti?').

OIC's implicit A1 irregular canon is exactly ten verbs (andare, venire, sapere, uscire, fare, dare, dire, dovere, potere, volere) drilled as full paradigms, with essere/avere/stare handled earlier and the -go/-gliere/-porre classes (salire, rimanere, scegliere, proporre) deferred to A2. Modals are not a separate unit - vogliono/devi/può appear mixed into ordinary irregular drills, always followed by an infinitive.

Gap in OIC coverage that Linguics can exploit: the -isc rule is stated in one sentence ('some verbs, such as preferire, add -isc') with no list of common -isc verbs, no statement that -isc drops in noi/voi, and capire is even mislabelled 'irregular' on the A2 lesson page. A clean -isc vs plain -ire discrimination drill (capire/preferire/pulire/finire vs dormire/aprire/partire/sentire) does not exist on the site.

Item-count norms: OIC drills are 7-12 items per page; type-in pages give the infinitive in parentheses (plus explicit person when the sentence underdetermines it, e.g. '(andare - noi)'), dropdown pages give no hint at all so the learner also chooses the lexical verb. That second format - choose verb AND form - is a distinct, harder task type worth replicating.

The intro lesson's sequencing is notable: reflexive chiamarsi and -isc preferire are introduced among the first ten paradigms, BEFORE the three-conjugation lesson. Also quotable rules for lesson blurbs: pro-drop ('the conjugation of the verb makes it obvious') and present-for-future ('Vado is good also to mean I'm going or even I will go').
new_micro_topics: compound subject person mapping: 'io e X' -> noi, 'tu e X' -> voi
person recovery from possessives in zero-subject sentences (i miei -> io, i tuoi -> tu, i vostri -> voi)
person recovery from a verb in an adjacent/subordinate clause ('quando vai...' -> tu; '...mi sento' -> io)
vocative vs subject: 'Gianna, vai?' -> tu not 3sg
chi + 3sg verb despite plural object ('Chi lava i piatti?')
postverbal/inverted subjects after fronted adverbials ('La settimana prossima esce il nuovo album'; 'A che ora arriva tua sorella?')
presente with future time adverbials (present-for-future)
da + presente for ongoing duration ('studiano l'italiano da molti anni')
-isc vs plain -ire discrimination, including -isc absent in noi/voi
g-insertion irregulars limited to io/loro forms (salgo/salgono, rimango/rimangono, scelgo/scelgono, propongo/propongono)
-gliere and -porre conjugation classes at A2 (scegliere, proporre)
formal Lei triggered by register cues ('Scusi professore, può...?')
sapere + infinitive = 'know how to' vs conjugated lexical verb
reflexives: pronoun+ending double agreement as a single choice unit
reciprocal si ('si conoscono da molti anni')
reflexive + body part with definite article ('si taglia i capelli')
mettersi a + infinitive as lexicalised reflexive pattern
reflexive verbs with prepositional government (ricordarsi di, preoccuparsi per, arrabbiarsi con)
essere vs chiamarsi for introducing names ('Lui si chiama Giovanni' not 'è')
quantified plural subjects ('molti turisti' -> 3pl) and animal/inanimate subjects -> 3sg/3pl agreement

pages.label: passato prossimo lesson
pages.format: lesson page with rules and examples, no interactive exercise
pages.skills_tested: n/a (lesson): formation aux+participio; regular participle endings by conjugation; essere-vs-avere selection; participle agreement with essere
pages.traps_and_distractors: n/a (lesson)
pages.steals: Agreement minimal pair worth stealing: 'Paola è andata al cinema. / Mario è andato al cinema. / Io e Maria siamo andate al cinema.' (same verb, three agreement endings incl. feminine plural noi). Also 'Paola ha dormito a lungo. / Mario ha visitato un museo.' as avere baseline.
pages.lesson_rules: Formation: 'essere OR avere + participio passato'. Regular participles: '-are → ato (mangiare-mangiato); -ere → uto (avere-avuto); -ire → ito (dormire-dormito)'. Aux choice: 'The majority of verbs use avere'; essere with '– verbs of movement – verbs of changing state – reflexive verbs'. Agreement: 'With essere the ending of the past participle changes to reflect the gender and singular/plural of the subject.' Two irregular-participle lists WITH aux marked inline, e.g. 'fare – (avere) fatto; mettere – (avere) messo; venire – (essere) venuto' — the aux-tagged irregular list format is worth copying.

pages.label: verbi modali al pp lesson

pages.steals: the paired absolute-construction examples: transitive 'Mangiate le mele...' (participle agrees with OBJECT) vs intransitive 'Arrivate a casa...' (participle agrees with SUBJECT) — a minimal pair Linguics should replicate for C1
pages.lesson_rules: GOLD: classifies the bare participle clause into FIVE functions — 1) causal (paraphrase 'siccome'), 2) temporal (answers 'when?'), 3) concessive ('even though'), 4) relative/adjectival, 5) hypothetical ('if'). Agreement rule verbatim: 'when there is a past participle with a direct object, the ending of the past participle must be feminine or masculine according to the gender and singular/plural nature of the direct object'; with intransitives 'the ending...would instead agree with the subject'. Also restates -ato/-uto/-ito and lists irregulars: leggere→letto, fare→fatto, vedere→visto, scrivere→scritto.

batch_tips: Hot Potatoes drill pages on this site embed their items in JavaScript; WebFetch markdown only surfaces the title, instructions, and any worked example. Of 12 drill pages, full item sets were recoverable only for the rewrite exercise (passato_prossimo_a1(2).html). Direct curl to onlineitalianclub.com is blocked by egress policy (403 CONNECT), so items cannot be scraped another way from this environment — treat hidden-item pages as format+instruction evidence only.

The site's implicit progression is a good template: participle morphology in isolation (regolare, irregolare 1-2) → binary aux choice with participle given → full type-in conjugation → sentence rewrite → clitic+agreement → modals → reflexives. Linguics' atomised drills map 1:1 onto these stages.

Best trap harvested: transitive use of a 'known essere verb' — 'Oggi Claudia comincia una nuova dieta' requires HA cominciato. Pair every dual-aux verb item with its opposite-aux twin as the lesson page does (è cominciato / ho cominciato).

Modal lesson's agreement-on-the-modal examples (siamo dovuti venire, sono volute andare, non è voluta uscire) are the strongest A2 content in the batch; one sentence contrasting masc sg and fem pl in a single item ('Marco è voluto partire... Stefania e Giulia sono volute andare') is a stealable dual-item format.

Coverage gaps in the site Linguics can exploit: reflexive+modal auxiliary switch (mi sono dovuto alzare vs ho dovuto alzarmi) is covered nowhere; the reflexive lesson never teaches pp of reflexives though an A2 drill tests it; the dual-aux lesson lists only cominciare/finire/continuare; C1 lesson promises participio presente in its title but never delivers it.

Worked examples on this site often leak gender via a subordinate clause ('...perché mi sono svegliato tardi') — a clean technique for making type-in agreement answers unambiguous without stating the subject's gender.

reflexive verbs with modals: auxiliary switch by pronoun position (mi sono dovuto alzare vs ho dovuto alzarmi) — untaught gap on source site
clitic lo/la/li/le + obligatory participle agreement, including elided l'ho + -a/-e endings (l'ho spedita)

irregular participle regularisation traps: *vinciuto/vinto, *facuto/fatto, aux-tagged irregular lists (fare–(avere) fatto, venire–(essere) venuto)

pages.label: imperfetto lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: usage rules + full conjugation tables + translated example sentences; no interactive exercise.
pages.skills_tested: Regular imperfetto endings for all three conjugations (-avo/-evo/-ivo sets, all six persons); irregular imperfetto stems (essere, fare, dare, dire, bere); mapping four usage contexts to imperfetto: description of people/places/situations in the past, past habits, physical/psychological states, simultaneous ongoing actions.
pages.traps_and_distractors: N/A (lesson page, no answer options).
pages.steals: "Da piccolo vivevo in campagna in una grande fattoria" (habit/description trigger 'da piccolo'); "Mentre facevo le fotocopie, Silvia scriveva gli indirizzi sulle buste" (double imperfetto for two simultaneous actions with 'mentre').
pages.lesson_rules: Imperfetto used for: (1) people, animals, places and situations in the past; (2) habits in the past; (3) physical or psychological states; (4) actions taking place at the same moment. Endings: -are → avo/avi/ava/avamo/avate/avano; -ere → evo/evi/eva/evamo/evate/evano; -ire → ivo/ivi/iva/ivamo/ivate/ivano. Irregular list: essere (ero, eri, era, eravamo, eravate, erano), fare (facevo...), dare, dire (dicevo...), bere (bevevo...) — the classic hidden-stem set.

pages.label: imperfetto

pages.traps_and_distractors: Open-response, no distractors on hub. Implicit traps: learners wrongly inserting -isc- into finire (finivo not *finiscevo) and using surface stems for dire/fare/tradurre (*divo, *favo, *traducvo) instead of Latin-derived stems.
pages.steals: The verb inventory itself is the steal: cantare/vedere/sentire/finire (regolare) + essere/dire/fare/tradurre (irregolare) — a compact set covering every imperfetto stem irregularity type including -urre verbs.

pages.label: imperfetto o pp?
pages.format: Cloze/gap-fill: continuous first-person childhood-memoir narrative with 12 numbered gaps, infinitive in brackets, learner conjugates into imperfetto or passato prossimo.
pages.skills_tested: Tense selection driven by narrative role: background/habitual (imperfetto) vs plot-advancing completed event (passato prossimo); recognizing temporal-marker triggers ('un giorno' → pp; ongoing childhood frame → imperfetto); conjugating both tenses correctly once the tense is chosen (double jeopardy: selection + formation).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Marker-based baits: 'un giorno' flips a habitual passage into event mode mid-text; 'quando ero bambino' frame tempts overuse of pp; emotional/state verbs ('ero troppo felice') must stay imperfetto; 'da quel giorno non li ho lasciati mai più' — negative + 'mai più' after a punctual gift event baits imperfetto but needs pp.
pages.steals: "Quando (essere)(1) bambino (avere)(2) tanti giochi e mi piaceva stare all'aria aperta" — two imperfetto gaps anchored by an already-conjugated 'piaceva'; "Un giorno mia madre mi (regalare)(3) dei pattini e da quel giorno non li (lasciare)(4) mai più" — clean habit→event pivot in one sentence.
pages.lesson_rules: Instruction: 'Complete the text by conjugating the verb (in brackets) in the imperfect or past tense.' The memoir-arc structure (habits → 'un giorno' incident → outcome) is itself a reusable template.

pages.label: tempi passati, uso dei
pages.format: Gap-fill cloze: 15 numbered gaps across 3 short narrative passages (news-style stories); infinitives in brackets; three-way choice passato prossimo / imperfetto / trapassato prossimo.
pages.skills_tested: Three-way past-tense selection inside multi-sentence narratives; trapassato for anteriority to a past reference point; auxiliary choice (essere/avere) folded in; reading temporal markers: 'la settimana scorsa' (pp), 'da qualche tempo' (imperfetto, ongoing state), 'mai prima di allora' and 'pochi minuti prima' (trapassato).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Anteriority baits: 'mai prima di allora (avere) problemi' tempts imperfetto but requires trapassato ('non aveva mai avuto'); 'pochi minuti prima (acquistare)' tempts pp but the event precedes the narrative past → trapassato; 'da qualche tempo' tempts pp for duration but needs imperfetto.
pages.steals: "La settimana scorsa la polizia (1)(arrestare) l'amministratore" — punctual news lead → pp; "mai prima di allora (9)(avere) problemi del genere" — textbook trapassato trigger phrase; "pochi minuti prima (15)(acquistare) l'ultima copia" — 'X prima' as anteriority signal.
pages.lesson_rules: Instruction: 'Conjugate the verbs using the past, imperfect or past perfect tenses.' Implicit rule set encoded in markers: 'prima di allora' / 'poco prima' → trapassato; specific dated event → pp; open-ended background 'da + time' → imperfetto.

pages.label: trapassato lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: formation rule, auxiliary conjugation tables, agreement rule, examples embedded in a short narrative passage (a woman returning to find things undone/done); no exercise.
pages.skills_tested: Formation: imperfetto of avere/essere + past participle; participle agreement with essere; using trapassato for events prior to a past reference point, including reflexives (si era dimenticata) and negatives (non aveva aperto).
pages.traps_and_distractors: N/A (lesson).
pages.steals: "Non aveva aperto la posta" (negation + avere); "Si era dimenticata di contattare il professor Mizzi" (reflexive → essere + feminine agreement -a — packs auxiliary choice AND agreement into one item); "Aveva trascorso il tempo facendo solo cruciverba".
pages.lesson_rules: Formation: "formed using the auxiliary verb 'avere' or 'essere' in the imperfect tense, and the past participle." Agreement: "When you use the auxiliary verb 'essere', the past participle needs to 'agree' with the gender and singular or plural nature of the subject." Usage: shows "that a past event or state happened prior to a past reference point."

pages.label: trapassato prossimo
pages.format: Gap-fill cloze: 12 numbered gaps in a single comic narrative (cat Tomasino wrecks the house while Lucia shops); infinitives in brackets; learner types trapassato (frame verbs in pp).
pages.skills_tested: Trapassato formation in 3rd person singular; essere vs avere selection (reflexive divertirsi → essere; transitive rompere/rovesciare/scavare/sporcare → avere); irregular participles (rompere→rotto); distinguishing frame events (andare/lasciare in pp) from discovered-prior events (trapassato) — the 'return home and discover' schema.
pages.traps_and_distractors: First two gaps (andare, lasciare) are actually pp frame verbs, baiting learners into trapassato-everywhere; irregular participle rotto vs regular strappato side by side; reflexive divertirsi baits avere.
pages.steals: "Giovedì scorso Lucia (andare)(1) a fare la spesa e (lasciare)(2) a casa da solo il suo gattino Tomasino" — frame in pp before the trapassato list; "Inoltre (scavare)(7) nel vaso dei ciclamini e (sporcare)(8) tutto il pavimento".
pages.lesson_rules: Instruction: 'Complete the text by conjugating the verbs in the past perfect.' The discover-on-return narrative frame is a highly reusable trapassato scaffold.

pages.format: Lesson/reference page: usage note + regular conjugation tables for all three groups + a large catalogue of irregular verbs grouped into families; no exercises, no example sentences.

It is usually found in written texts, in literary or historical contexts (though it also has a spoken use in certain Italian regions)." Irregular inventory (gold list): essere, avere, fare, mettere, bere, venire, tenere, sapere, volere, scrivere, vivere, tradurre, dire, produrre, trarre, leggere, conoscere, crescere, prendere, scendere, accendere, uccidere, chiedere, decidere, perdere, correre, accorgersi, vincere, raggiungere, congiungere, aggiungere, convincere, scomparire, apparire, nascere, piacere.

batch_tips: Site's strongest asset is the narrative-cloze template: a 10-15 gap continuous story whose plot structure forces tense contrasts (childhood habits → 'un giorno' incident for imperfetto/pp; return-home-and-discover for trapassato; news reports with 'poco prima' flashbacks for three-way pp/imperfetto/trapassato). Atomised drills can reuse these frames at 2-3 gaps each.

Temporal markers are the load-bearing distractor mechanism across the batch: da piccolo/mentre/da qualche tempo → imperfetto; un giorno/la settimana scorsa/date → passato prossimo; mai prima di allora/pochi minuti prima/già → trapassato. A marker→tense mapping bank would generate most items.

All pure-selection exercises (dropdown pp-vs-imperfetto, three-way presente/pp/imperfetto) hide their items in JavaScript that fetch cannot extract — six pages audited yielded format and instructions only. Do not cite item-level content from those pages; the extractable gold is in the open-response cloze pages and the two lesson pages.

Cloze items here carry double jeopardy (choose tense AND conjugate). For atomised drills, split into two item types: form-given tense-choice (like their dropdowns) and tense-given form-production (like their paradigm drills).

Irregular-stem inventories to lift: imperfetto hidden stems (essere, fare→facevo, dire→dicevo, bere→bevevo, dare, tradurre→traducevo); remoto irregular families from the B2 lesson (36-verb list, groupable into -si, -ssi, -cqui, suppletive classes with the 1-3-3 person pattern).

Auxiliary choice and participle agreement are consistently folded into trapassato items (si era dimenticata; reflexive divertirsi vs transitive rompere) — trapassato drills should always mix essere/avere and include one feminine/plural agreement item.

-ere remoto double forms (credei/credetti, potei/poté) as an explicit acceptable-variants item type

pages.label: futuro semplice lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: rules + full conjugation tables by verb class and irregular sub-class + example sentences; no interactive exercise on-page.
pages.skills_tested: Formation micro-skills, taught in four tiers: (1) -are verbs change stem vowel a>e (studiare > studierò); (2) -ere and -ire keep their vowel (leggerò, partirò); (3) 'no-e' irregulars that drop the infinitive vowel: andare, avere, dovere, potere, vivere (avrò, andrò); (4) double-r irregulars: venire, volere, bere, rimanere (vorrò, verrò); plus fully irregular essere/fare/stare/dare/dire (sarò, farò, starò, darò, dirò). Also introduces the epistemic/conjecture use of future.
pages.traps_and_distractors: The tier structure itself is the trap map: learners must decide per-verb between -erò / -rò (syncopated) / -rrò (doubled). 'vivere > vivrò' hiding in the no-e list is a good low-frequency trap; 'bere > berrò' (stem change plus rr) is the hardest of the double-r set.
pages.steals: Epistemic-future dialogue item: 'Perché Antonio non è ancora tornato a casa? — Non lo so, forse avrà trovato molto traffico' (probability, not futurity). Also concessive future: 'Tu sarai bravo a cucinare, ma nelle pulizie di casa sei un disastro!' — future used for conceded supposition about the PRESENT.
pages.lesson_rules: Rule 1: -are verbs turn -a- into -e- before future endings (-erò, -erai, -erà, -eremo, -erete, -eranno). Rule 2 exception list (drop the infinitive vowel, no 'e'): andare, avere, dovere, potere, vivere. Rule 3 exception list (double r): venire, volere, bere, rimanere. Rule 4 memorize outright: essere (sarò), fare (farò), stare (starò), dare (darò), dire (dirò). Endings are identical for all verbs; only the stem varies.

pages.label: condizionale semplice lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: usage functions + conjugation tables by verb class and irregular sub-class + translated examples; no on-page exercise.
pages.skills_tested: (1) Three functions: politeness requests, advice (dovresti...), desire/wish (verrei volentieri). (2) Formation: -are a>e (mangiare > mangerei — note mangerei also tests the -giare i-drop), regular -ere/-ire; no-e irregular stems andr-/potr-/dovr-/sapr-/vedr-/cadr-; double-r stems verr- etc.; outright irregulars essere/dare/fare/stare. (3) The key transfer insight: conditional stems are identical to future stems, only endings differ (-ei, -esti, -ebbe, -emmo, -este, -ebbero).
pages.traps_and_distractors: noi -emmo (conditional) vs -emo (future): mangeremmo vs mangeremo differ by one m — the classic trap this paradigm sets up. Also -ebbe/-ebbero third-person endings invite *-erebbe on double-r verbs (*venirebbe vs verrebbe).
pages.steals: 'Fabio, dovresti cambiare lavoro' (advice function, dovere); 'Potrebbe darmi un bicchiere d'acqua?' (formal politeness, Lei); 'L'estate prossima verrei volentieri in vacanza con voi' (wish + double-r verb in one item).
pages.lesson_rules: Rule: same stems as futuro semplice; endings -ei -esti -ebbe -emmo -este -ebbero for ALL verbs. Exception list (drop vowel, no e): potere, dovere, sapere, vedere, cadere, andare. Exception list (double r): venire (verrei) and its pattern-mates. Memorize: essere (sarei), dare (darei), fare (farei), stare (starei). Functions taught: politeness, advice, desire.

pages.label: condizionale, progetti estate
pages.format: Connected-text cloze: a dialogue about summer holiday plans (a trip to Croatia) with 12 numbered gaps, verbs supplied in brackets, open response: 'Complete the text by conjugating the verbs in the conditional present tense'.
pages.skills_tested: Conditional present across mixed persons inside ONE coherent dialogue: volere 1pl (vorremmo fare un viaggio), venire 2pl (verreste), piacere 3sg (piacerebbe), spendere 3sg neg, dormire 1pl and 1sg neg, chiudere 1sg neg, potere 1pl neg (non potremmo), essere 3pl (sarebbero), trovare 1pl neg, avere 1pl (avremmo), essere 3sg (sarebbe). Five of twelve gaps are negated — tests that non + conditional is mechanical. Function tested: hypothetical planning/desire, not politeness.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Open cloze, so traps are formation: vorremmo/verreste/potremmo double-r + -emmo double-m cluster; piacerebbe needs impersonal 3sg with dative (ci/mi piacerebbe) even though the humans are plural in the discourse; person must be inferred from dialogue turns, not given pronouns.
pages.steals: The whole frame: 'Progetti per l'estate' dialogue where two speakers negotiate a Croatia trip and every verb of wanting/preferring/hypothesizing goes to conditional — e.g. gap pattern '(volere, noi) ___ fare un viaggio' ... '(venire, voi) ___ con noi?'. Also the deliberate 1sg-vs-1pl dormire contrast within one text.

pages.label: condizionale composto lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: formation rule, auxiliary tables (essere and avere), one table per verb class (cantare, mettere, aprire), usage list, translated examples.
pages.skills_tested: (1) Formation: conditional of avere/essere + past participle. (2) Agreement: with essere the participle agrees in gender/number (sarei stato/a, saremmo stati/e). (3) Three uses: unrealizable situations; future-in-the-past (reported/anticipated future from a past viewpoint); past expectations not fulfilled.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Auxiliary choice (essere vs avere) plus participle agreement is the trap cluster; future-in-the-past is the use English speakers miss (Italian requires 'avrebbe fatto' where English says 'would do').
pages.steals: 'Sarei venuto volentieri alla tua festa ma purtroppo avevo la febbre' (unfulfilled past wish, essere-aux, ma-clause giving the blocker); 'Non immaginavo che un giorno tu e Carla vi sareste sposati' (future-in-the-past with reflexive reciprocal + plural agreement 'sposati' — a dense, excellent item).
pages.lesson_rules: Rule quoted: 'The conditional past is formed with the conditional form of the auxiliary verb essere or avere and the past participle.' Agreement rule quoted: 'When you use the auxiliary verb essere, the past participle needs to be masculine or feminine according to the gender of the subject.' Usage list: unrealizable situations, the future in the past, past expectations which were not fulfilled.

pages.label: condizionale composto
pages.format: Gap-fill. Real title: 'Italian Grammar Exercise - Unreal Past - Gapfill Exercise'; instruction: 'Complete the sentences with verbs in the unreal past'. One item fragment visible: 'Se ieri mattina non avessi perso il treno...'. Remaining items JS-hidden.
pages.skills_tested: Third-type hypothetical (periodo ipotetico dell'irrealtà): se + congiuntivo trapassato in the protasis ('se non avessi perso il treno') with condizionale composto expected in the apodosis — so the page actually tests the full unreal-past frame, not the conditional in isolation, despite its 'condizionale composto' filename.
pages.traps_and_distractors: The protasis/apodosis split is the trap: learners put conditional in the se-clause (*se non avrei perso) — the visible item is engineered against exactly that error.
pages.steals: 'Se ieri mattina non avessi perso il treno, [non sarei arrivato/a in ritardo]-type completion' — the se-clause-given, apodosis-gapped design is directly reusable.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page; links to an 'unreal past' lesson page.

batch_tips: Site structural fact: onlineitalianclub exercise pages are Hot Potatoes-style with all items inside <script> blocks; WebFetch's markdown conversion strips scripts, so 11 of 16 pages yielded only title+instruction. Direct curl to the host is blocked by egress policy (403 CONNECT). Lesson pages and a few HTML-rendered clozes (progetti_estate_a2) are fully harvestable; treat titles/instructions of the rest as format metadata only.

Filenames lie, titles don't: uso_condizionale_b1.html is really 'Unreal Present/Past Gapfill' (present-vs-past conditional selection) and condizionale_composto_b1.html is really 'Unreal Past' (se + congiuntivo trapassato frame). Use the real titles when mapping coverage.

The site's implicit curriculum for both futuro and condizionale is a four-tier stem taxonomy — regular / no-e (andr-, avr-, dovr-, potr-, sapr-, vedr-, cadr-, vivr-) / double-r (verr-, vorr-, berr-, rimarr-) / memorized (sar-, far-, star-, dar-, dir-) — with identical stems across the two tenses. Drill sets should mirror this: same nine exemplar verbs (cantare, credere, sentire, finire, avere, essere, andare, venire, fare) in both tenses, contrasting only endings (-emo vs -emmo, -ò/-à accent placement).

High-value item patterns to replicate: epistemic future for present probability ('forse avrà trovato traffico'), connected-dialogue cloze with negation and person inferred from discourse (progetti_estate), apodosis-gapped se-clauses, and situation-to-utterance pragmatics matching for polite conditionals.
new_micro_topics: futuro epistemico / concessivo: future tense for present-time conjecture ('avrà trovato traffico') and concession ('sarai bravo, ma...') — taught at A2 here, usually deferred to B1 elsewhere
accent-as-morpheme typing skill: -ò vs -à as the sole io/lui distinction in the future; site grades accented characters explicitly
-isc- suppression in future/conditional: finire > finirò/finirei, never *finiscerò — worth an atomised drill
futuro vs condizionale ending discrimination on shared stems: -emo/-emmo, -ai/-esti, -anno/-ebbero minimal pairs
condizionale presente vs composto selection cued by time adverbials (unreal present vs unreal past)
future-in-the-past: condizionale composto after past-tense reporting verbs ('Non immaginavo che... vi sareste sposati')
participle agreement inside condizionale composto with essere and reflexive reciprocals (sareste sposati/e)
protasis-apodosis mood split in periodo ipotetico III: se + congiuntivo trapassato (never conditional) + condizionale composto

pages.label: congiuntivo presente lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: usage rules, trigger-phrase list, full conjugation tables (3 regular groups + irregulars), example sentences. No embedded exercises.
pages.skills_tested: Formation of congiuntivo presente: -are verbs flip to -i endings (compri/compri/compri/compriamo/compriate/comprino), -ere and -ire flip to -a (prenda, parta); io/tu/lui-lei share one identical form; noi form equals the indicative; voi takes -iate. Recognising trigger predicates of opinion/doubt. The same-subject constraint: subjunctive only when main and subordinate subjects differ.
pages.traps_and_distractors: No exercise items, but the lesson's own rule set defines the trap axes: (a) same subject -> must switch to 'di + infinitive' instead of 'che + congiuntivo' (Credo di essere vs *Credo che io sia); (b) the shared io/tu/lui form means subject pronouns become obligatory for disambiguation; (c) noi form is identical to indicative (compriamo) so learners can't tell mood from that cell.
pages.steals: "Mi sembra che l'appartamento non abbia un balcone." and "Credo che si trasferisca per trovare lavoro." — both from a coherent apartment-hunting scenario, nice model for themed item sets. Also "È importante che sia una zona ben collegata al centro" (impersonal trigger + essere).
pages.lesson_rules: Subjunctive = mood of 'doubt, likeliness or uncertainty'. Hard rule stated: 'the subject of the main phrase must be different from the subject in the subordinate clause'; when subjects match, use 'di + infinitive'. Trigger list given: credo che, spero che, mi/ti/le/gli/ci/vi sembra che, penso che, è importante/fondamentale che, suppongo che. Irregular presente verbs listed and fully conjugated: essere, avere, fare, dare, potere.

pages.label: congiuntivo imperfetto lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: usage rule contrasting imperfetto vs passato subjunctive, trigger list, conjugation tables (3 regular + 5 irregular), contrastive example pairs. No embedded exercises.
pages.skills_tested: Formation: -assi/-essi/-issi paradigms (ballassi, perdessi, dormissi); io/tu share one form (fossi = both 'I was' and 'you were'); irregular stems fossi/facessi/stessi/dessi/dicessi. Crucially tests consecutio temporum: choosing imperfetto vs passato subjunctive based on main-verb tense.
pages.traps_and_distractors: The lesson's own contrast pair defines the trap: 'crede che... abbia detto' vs 'credeva che... fossi' — baiting passato subjunctive after a past-tense main verb (or vice versa). Also stessi vs dessi (stare/dare both take -essi despite being -are verbs) is a built-in confusion pair; dicessi from dire (latin stem) baits *diressi/*dissi.
pages.steals: Minimal pair worth stealing verbatim: 'Veronica non credeva che fossi il fratello della sua migliore amica' vs 'Veronica non crede che io le abbia detto la verità' — same trigger verb, tense of credere flips the required subjunctive tense. Also 'Era necessario che Paolo si impegnasse nello studio per non essere bocciato di nuovo' (impersonal past trigger + reflexive).
pages.lesson_rules: Core rule verbatim: 'the subjunctive past is used with a main verb (I think, I believe, etc.) in the present tense, the subjunctive imperfect is used when the main verb (I thought, I believed, etc.) is in a past tense.' Trigger list: credevo che, era importante che, non immaginavo che, era necessario che, avevo paura che. Irregular list: essere->fossi, fare->facessi, stare->stessi, dare->dessi, dire->dicessi. CAUTION: the page's dire table contains an error ('loro dessero' printed where dicessero belongs) — verify before reuse.

pages.label: congiuntivo passato lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: formation rule, usage rule, auxiliary-agreement note, three worked example sentences with translations. No conjugation mega-tables, no exercises.
pages.skills_tested: Building congiuntivo passato = presente subjunctive of avere/essere + past participle (abbia detto, sia scappato, siano usciti); auxiliary choice; participle agreement with essere (siano usciti — masc. plural); restriction to present-tense main verbs.
pages.traps_and_distractors: The stated rules imply the traps: unagreed participle after essere (*siano uscito), wrong auxiliary (*abbia scappato), and using passato after a past main verb where imperfetto is required.
pages.steals: 'Penso che i miei genitori siano usciti prima da lavoro per prepararmi questa festa di compleanno a sorpresa' — packs auxiliary essere + plural agreement into one natural item. 'Filippo pensa che il suo cane sia scappato mentre era in giardino a giocare' — subordinate indicative 'era' alongside subjunctive 'sia scappato' in the same sentence, good for mood-discrimination items.
pages.lesson_rules: Verbatim: 'The subjunctive past is formed using the present subjunctive of the auxiliary verb and the past participle of the main verb'; 'used to express opinions or assumptions about something that happened in the past'; 'used only if the verb it follows in the main sentence (I think, I believe, etc.) is in the present tense'; with essere 'the ending of the past participle will change according to whether the subject is singular, plural, masculine or feminine'.

pages.label: congiuntivo trapassato lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: formation rule, three-use list, agreement note, five conjugation tables (essere, avere, suonare, friggere, soffrire), four example sentences incl. a third-conditional. No exercises.
pages.skills_tested: Building trapassato = imperfetto subjunctive of auxiliary + past participle (fossi stato/a, avessi avuto); irregular participles deliberately included in the tables (friggere->fritto, soffrire->sofferto); anteriority in the past; use in unreal past conditionals and after magari / vorrei che / senza che.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Implied trap axes from the rules: auxiliary in congiuntivo presente instead of imperfetto (*abbia avuto for avessi avuto — i.e. passato/trapassato confusion), participle agreement with essere (fossi stato/a), and conditional-clause direction (subjunctive in the se-clause, condizionale passato in the main clause, never *se avrebbe visto).
pages.steals: 'Se Luca mi avesse visto si sarebbe fermato a parlare un po'' — clean periodo ipotetico III template. 'Magari avessimo saputo che ci sarebbe stato un test a sorpresa oggi!' — magari + trapassato for regret. 'Ero partito senza che tu mi avessi dato il via' — senza che as a non-opinion trigger, rare in coursebooks and worth copying.
pages.lesson_rules: Verbatim: 'formed with the subjunctive imperfect of the auxiliary verb and the past participle of the main verb'. Three uses listed: (1) 'to express something that came before another action in the past or is unrealizable', (2) 'to express wishes or desires', (3) 'in unreal past conditionals'. Essere note: participle 'needs to be masculine or feminine according to the subject'.

batch_tips: All eight drill pages on this site render their item banks via JavaScript (Hot Potatoes-style); WebFetch's markdown conversion strips them and direct curl / reader proxies are egress-blocked, so only formats, instructions and titles are auditable — steal rules and sentences from the four lesson pages instead and author items fresh.

The site's structural pattern per tense is worth copying: one type-in production drill + one dropdown recognition drill, ~two sets for the presente, each preceded by a lesson page — a clean production/recognition split per micro-skill.

The single most drillable distinction across the whole batch is consecutio temporum, stated verbatim in the imperfetto lesson: present-tense main verb -> congiuntivo passato, past-tense main verb -> congiuntivo imperfetto. Build minimal pairs on the Veronica model (crede che abbia detto / credeva che fossi).

The A2/B1 lesson states the same-subject rule as absolute: different subjects -> che + congiuntivo, same subject -> di + infinitivo. This is a high-yield trap axis (Credo di essere vs *Credo che io sia) the site's own exercises apparently never drill.

Person-syncretism is a hidden design constraint: presente io/tu/lui-lei share one form, imperfetto io/tu share one form — every item needs an explicit subject pronoun, and 'which persons could this form be?' is itself a stealable item type.

Site's irregular shortlists to seed drills: presente lesson — essere, avere, fare, dare, potere; presente drill hub adds andare, venire, dovere, volere plus -isc- verb finire; imperfetto — fossi, facessi, stessi, dessi, dicessi (note stessi/dessi: stare and dare take -essi despite -are infinitives, a ready-made confusion pair).

For compound tenses the site foregrounds essere-participle agreement (siano usciti, fossi stato/a) and auxiliary tense (abbia fatto = passato vs avessi fatto = trapassato) — those two axes should generate most compound-tense distractors.
Trust but verify: the imperfetto lesson's dire table prints 'loro dessero' where dicessero belongs — do not scrape site tables into answer keys unchecked.
new_micro_topics: consecutio temporum: selecting subjunctive tense from main-clause tense (crede che vs credeva che)
di + infinitivo vs che + congiuntivo (same-subject transformation)
senza che + congiuntivo and other conjunction triggers (beyond opinion verbs)

pages.format: Lesson page: 7 grouped trigger categories, each with an expression list and Italian example sentences. No exercise on the page.

pages.lesson_rules: Seven trigger lists, verbatim: OPINIONS: credere che / pensare che / immaginare che / non sapere se. FEELINGS: non sopportare che / temere che / avere paura che / essere contento che / dispiacersi che. DOUBTS: dubitare che / non essere sicuro che. WILL/DESIRE: preferire che / desiderare che / permettere che. ESSERE+ADJ: e' possibile che / e' utile che / e' meglio che / e' importante che. IMPERSONAL VERBS: occorre che / basta che / puo' darsi che / bisogna che. FIXED EXPRESSIONS: senza che / prima che / nel caso che / a patto che / aspettarsi che / si dice che. Other modeled examples: 'Mia madre crede che Anita sia una mia compagna di classe.', 'Francesca non sopporta che i suoi studenti arrivino in ritardo.', 'Dubito che la tua strategia sia valida.', 'E' possibile che domani nevichi.'

pages.label: altri usi del congiuntivo
pages.format: Lesson page: 4 advanced-use rules, each named in Italian with an English gloss and 2-3 example sentences. No exercise on the page.
pages.skills_tested: Advanced/syntactic subjunctive triggers beyond verb-lists: (1) rhetorical interrogatives, (2) fronted object clauses, (3) relative clauses with final (purpose) value, (4) relative clauses with consecutive value / negated antecedents.
pages.traps_and_distractors: N/A (lesson). Rule 3 implies the killer contrast for item-writing: 'Cerco una segretaria che sappia usare il computer' (non-specific antecedent, subjunctive) vs a known/specific antecedent taking indicative ('Ho una segretaria che sa...').
pages.steals: 'Chi mi assicura che lui poi non faccia di testa sua?' (rhetorical question forcing subjunctive); 'Che fosse strano, lo avevo sempre detto.' (fronted che-clause -> imperfetto congiuntivo even for a statement of fact); 'Devo comprare un libro che spieghi bene la grammatica.' (purpose relative).
pages.lesson_rules: Four rules verbatim: 1) 'Frase interrogativa retorica' - used when we ask something but already know the answer ('Chi mi garantisce non mi stia mentendo spudoratamente?', 'Come faccio a sapere chi lo abbia rotto se io non ero presente quando e' successo?'). 2) 'Frase oggettiva anteposta alla reggente' - object clause moved before the main clause takes subjunctive ('Che dicesse bugie, era chiaro dall'inizio', 'Che cosa ci trovasse di speciale in lui, non l'ho mai capito.'). 3) 'Relative con valore finale' - relative clause expressing purpose/sought quality ('Cerco una segretaria che sappia usare il computer.'). 4) 'Relative con valore consecutivo' - relatives expressing impossibility/non-existence (negation patterns).

batch_tips: Access note: the /free_italian_exercises/*.html quiz pages are Hot Potatoes exports whose items live in a JavaScript array; the fetch pipeline strips scripts, so item text, answers and distractors are unrecoverable through WebFetch even on successful fetches. All 10 quiz pages yielded only format, instruction line, title and lesson links. Raw-HTML access (curl was egress-blocked for this host, web.archive.org policy-blocked) would be needed to harvest actual items. The two WordPress lesson pages fetched fully.

Rate limiting: the host 429s rapid requests to the quiz directory; ~2.5 min spacing between fetches worked reliably after an initial ~7 min cooldown. Budget for this on any future onlineitalianclub batch.

The site's congiuntivo ladder is a clean progression worth copying: B1 = single-tense form production (imperfetto drill) -> binary tense contrast (imperfetto vs trapassato) -> open 3-way tense choice (presente/passato/imperfetto); B2 = trigger recognition via dropdowns (mood-only 'indicativo o congiuntivo?', connector choice, uses 1+2) paired with a production gapfill of the same skill; C1 = unsignposted mixed drill 'use the subjunctive where necessary' where indicative is sometimes correct.

The C1 concordanza instruction 'conjugate the verb in the correct form - use the subjunctive where necessary' is the single best design steal: it punishes subjunctive over-application, which none of our binary drills currently do.

The B2 lesson's 7-category trigger taxonomy (opinions / feelings / doubts / will / essere+adj / impersonal verbs / fixed expressions) with its exact word lists (incl. the easily-missed 'non sapere se', 'si dice che', 'aspettarsi che', 'permettere che', 'dispiacersi che') maps directly onto atomised buckets and gives ready-made seed sentences with tense agreement already modeled ('Pensavo che tu giocassi bene a calcio').

lesser-known lexical triggers: non sapere se, si dice che, aspettarsi che, permettere che, dispiacersi che

fronted object clauses: Che + congiuntivo imperfetto, ... (Che fosse strano, lo avevo sempre detto)

pages.label: concordanze verbali lesson
pages.format: Lesson page with rules and paired example sentences, organised by mood (indicative / subjunctive / conditional) and by main-clause tense (present vs past).
pages.skills_tested: The full concordanza system: mapping subordinate tense to (a) main-clause tense and (b) time relation (future/current/past action). Also shows acceptable doublets (parta/partirà; partisse/sarebbe partito).
pages.traps_and_distractors: n/a (lesson). But the doublets it lists ARE the natural distractor pairs: partiva vs stava partendo vs era partito after 'sapevo'; partisse vs sarebbe partito after 'ho pensato'.
pages.steals: Indicative, past main + future action: 'Ho saputo che sarebbe partito per il Belgio' (future-in-the-past = condizionale passato). Subjunctive, past main: 'Ho pensato che lui partisse / sarebbe partito' vs present main 'Penso che lui parta / partirà domani'.
pages.lesson_rules: Definition: 'Tense agreement (concordanze verbali) ... which tenses should be used with which other tenses.' INDICATIVE - present main: future action = presente o futuro ('So che domani parte / partirà'), current = presente/stare+gerundio, past = passato prossimo ('So che è partito ieri pomeriggio'). Past main (passato, imperfetto, trapassato, remoto): future = condizionale passato ('Ho saputo che sarebbe partito'), current = imperfetto/stava+gerundio ('Sapevo che partiva / stava partendo'), past = trapassato ('Avevo saputo che era partito'). SUBJUNCTIVE - present main: parta/partirà (future), parta/stia partendo (current), sia partito (past). Past main: partisse/sarebbe partito (future), partisse/stesse partendo (current), fosse partito (past). Present conditional main takes congiuntivo imperfetto or trapassato.

pages.skills_tested: Despite the URL, this drills the apodosis only: choosing condizionale presente vs condizionale passato (present-unreal vs past-unreal / future-in-the-past readings) and producing the correct compound form (auxiliary choice + participle agreement).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Not retrievable (JS-embedded). The stated binary (present vs past conditional) is itself the trap axis - time cues in the sentence decide which.
pages.steals: The 'present or past conditional?' framing is a clean atomised micro-drill Linguics can replicate: same verb in brackets, only the time-reference cue changes.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: discorso indiretto lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: tense-shift rules, direct-vs-indirect example pairs, pronoun and adverb change lists, links to exercises.
pages.skills_tested: n/a (lesson) - the reference for both C1 discorso indiretto drills.
pages.traps_and_distractors: n/a. The 'no change when introductory verb is present' rule is the built-in trap for any drill set: 'Marco dice: sono molto stanco' -> 'Marco dice che è molto stanco' (only person shifts, not tense).
pages.steals: Contrast pair worth stealing verbatim: 'Marco dice: "sono molto stanco"' -> 'Marco dice che è molto stanco' vs 'Marco ha detto: "oggi sono stanco"' -> 'Marco ha detto che quel giorno era stanco' (same verb, reporting-verb tense flips the whole transformation).
pages.lesson_rules: GOLD. If introductory verb is PRESENT: no tense changes. If PAST: presente -> imperfetto (or congiuntivo imperfetto); passato -> trapassato; imperfetto -> unchanged; trapassato -> unchanged; futuro -> condizionale passato; imperativo -> infinito. Pronouns/possessives: io/tu -> lui/lei, mio/tuo -> suo. Adverb word-list: qui -> lì; ora -> allora; oggi -> quel giorno; ieri -> il giorno prima; domani -> il giorno dopo.

pages.label: discorso indiretto 1
pages.format: Free transformation exercise - 'Reported Speech. Transform these sentences from direct to reported speech.' Six full direct-speech sentences, learner rewrites each.
pages.skills_tested: Full-pipeline reported speech after a past reporting verb: presente progressivo -> imperfetto progressivo; condizionale stays; habitual present + reflexives (mi alzo, mi vesto) -> imperfetto; futuro -> condizionale passato; se + presente/futuro real conditional embedded in reported speech; congiuntivo presente (spero che... piaccia) -> congiuntivo imperfetto; time-phrase shift (da oggi in poi -> da quel giorno in poi); pronoun/possessive shifts (mi/ti/tua -> gli/le/sua).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Free production, so traps are structural: item 2 has condizionale ('Mi dispiacerebbe tanto farti fare una brutta figura') which does NOT shift - baits over-transformation; item 3 is habitual daily-routine present baiting passato instead of imperfetto; item 4 nests a real conditional ('Se ti applichi, potrai...') so learner must shift both clauses coherently; item 6 combines negation + time adverbial + future.
pages.steals: 'Da oggi in poi non commetterò più sciocchezze.' (forces futuro -> condizionale passato PLUS oggi -> quel giorno: two rules in one short item). 'Spero che questo regalo piaccia a tua sorella!' (congiuntivo downshift + possessive tua -> sua). 'Se ti applichi, potrai riuscire a fare tutto quello che desideri.' (conditional inside reported speech - rare, valuable).
pages.lesson_rules: None on page; links back to the discorso indiretto lesson.

pages.label: discorso indiretto 2
pages.format: Transformation exercise - 'Reported Speech - 2. Change the direct speech to reported speech.'
pages.skills_tested: Second reported-speech transformation set (same pipeline as exercise 1).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Items not retrievable (JS-embedded; two fetch attempts) - unlike exercise 1, this page's sentences did not surface in the extracted text.
pages.steals: None retrievable.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

batch_tips: Site-structure fact: onlineitalianclub.com /free_italian_exercises/*.html pages are Hot Potatoes-style drills whose items and dropdown options live in JavaScript; WebFetch's markdown conversion strips them, so only title + instruction line is retrievable. Lesson pages (WordPress URLs) and plain transformation pages (e.g. concordanze_verbali_c1.html) yield full content. Direct curl to the host is blocked by egress policy (proxy 403), so raw-HTML scraping is not an option in this environment.

Exercise-design pattern across the site: each grammar point gets a minimal lesson (formula + 3 translated examples) plus a graded ladder of formats - matching halves (recognition) -> dropdown MCQ (selection) -> type-in gap-fill (cued production) -> free transformation (full production). Linguics can mirror this ladder per micro-topic.

The site's C1 'concordanze verbali' exercise is actually reported speech - reported speech is treated as the applied test of concordanza dei tempi. Bundling them (as this topic area does) matches how the source site sequences it.

Highest-value verbatim harvest: the C2 concordanze table (indicative + subjunctive, present vs past main clause) and the C1 discorso indiretto shift table with adverb list (qui->lì, ora->allora, oggi->quel giorno, ieri->il giorno prima, domani->il giorno dopo) - together they define the full answer key for this topic area.

Gap in the source site Linguics can exploit: no coverage of colloquial doppio imperfetto ('se lo sapevo, venivo'), no se+condizionale error diagnosis items beyond the vero/falso drill, and no mixed-type ipotetico (past condition, present result).
new_micro_topics: futuro-nel-passato: condizionale passato for future-in-the-past in both concordanze ('Ho saputo che sarebbe partito') and reported speech (futuro -> condizionale passato) - same form, two guises, one drill family
reported-speech adverb/deictic shifts as a standalone word-list drill (oggi->quel giorno, ieri->il giorno prima, domani->il giorno dopo, qui->lì, ora->allora)
imperativo -> infinito in reported commands (stated in the C1 lesson, unexercised on the site)
no-shift recognition: reporting verb in the PRESENT means no tense change - and non-shifting tenses after past reporting verbs (imperfetto, trapassato, condizionale stay put)
clause-role assignment: which slot gets congiuntivo vs condizionale in periodo ipotetico (the 'condizionale o congiuntivo?' binary)
apodosis time-layer choice: condizionale presente vs passato with only a time cue changing
real vs unreal conditional discrimination (type 1 vs type 2) as a selection task
grammaticality judgment (vero/falso) on conditional tense combinations
embedded conditionals inside reported speech (se-clause shifting together with the main clause)
congiuntivo downshift in reported speech: spero che piaccia -> sperava che piacesse

pages.label: imperativo diretto lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: rules + conjugation examples, no interactive exercise
pages.skills_tested: Tu-form imperative formation split by conjugation class (-are takes -a, -ere/-ire take -i); apocopated irregular tu imperatives; reflexive/object pronoun attachment to affirmative imperative; negative tu imperative built on the infinitive (non + infinito) with pronoun attached (non farlo)
pages.traps_and_distractors: No explicit distractors (lesson page), but it sets up the classic cross-conjugation bait: -a vs -i ending swap between verb classes; apostrophe forms (fa' vs fa/fai); pronoun position in affirmative vs negative
pages.steals: "mangiare – mangia! (tu)", "leggere – leggi! (tu)", "fare – fa' (tu)", negative-with-pronoun "non farlo!"
pages.lesson_rules: "Verbs in the first group (mangi-are) use the ending -a whereas verbs in the second and third groups (prend-ere, dorm-ire) use the ending -i." Irregular tu-imperative list quoted in full: fare – fa', andare – va', dire – di', dare – da', stare – sta', essere – sii, avere – abbi, sapere – sappi. Negative imperative = non + infinitive.

pages.label: imperativo
pages.format: Typed gap-fill (Hot Potatoes): complete each sentence with the imperative of the verb in brackets; item bank is JS-embedded and not retrievable, only the worked example is visible
pages.skills_tested: Producing (not just recognizing) tu-imperatives including apocopated irregulars with the apostrophe (va' not va/vai), from an infinitive hint in brackets
pages.traps_and_distractors: Free-typing format so traps are spelling-level: omitting the apostrophe on va'/fa'/di'/da'/sta', or using indicative (vai) instead of imperative; full item list JS-locked
pages.steals: Model item: "Stefano, è tardi, ___ a scuola! (andare)" → "va'" — good template: vocative name + situational cue + gap + infinitive hint
pages.lesson_rules: None on page; links back to The Imperative lesson

pages.label: imperativo indiretto lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: rules + conjugation examples, no interactive exercise
pages.skills_tested: Formal Lei-imperative formation with the ENDING MIRROR of the tu form (-are takes -i, -ere/-ire take -a); irregular Lei subjunctive-based forms; reflexive si and object pronouns placed BEFORE the Lei imperative, never attached
pages.traps_and_distractors: The endings are the exact inverse of the tu imperative (canti vs canta, legga vs leggi) — the single richest cross-bait in this whole topic; also clitic attachment (si accomodi, not *accomodisi)
pages.steals: "studiare - studi (Lei)", "leggere – legga (Lei)"
pages.lesson_rules: "Verbs belonging to the first group (cant-are) add the ending -i whereas verbs in the second and third groups add the ending -a." Irregular Lei list quoted in full: fare – faccia, andare – vada, dire – dica, dare – dia, stare – stia, essere – sia, avere – abbia. Reflexive rule: "the reflexive pronoun 'si' remains separated from the verb" (precedes it).

pages.label: imperativo indiretto, dal medico
pages.format: Dialogue reading ('Dal medico') + true/false (V/F) comprehension statements; the V/F statements are JS-locked but the full dialogue was recovered verbatim
pages.skills_tested: Recognition of Lei imperatives (regular, irregular, reflexive, negative) embedded in an authentic doctor-patient dialogue; comprehension of instructions given via imperative (dosage, behaviour)
pages.traps_and_distractors: V/F statements likely bait negation (told NOT to smoke), temporal detail (fever last night vs now: 'Ieri sera avevo 37.8, ora non ne ho'), and dosage detail ('uno la mattina e uno la sera, a stomaco pieno')
pages.steals: Doctor's turn packs the whole Lei-imperative paradigm in one utterance: "Si riposi, stia a casa per qualche giorno e prenda questi antibiotici per una settimana... Allora non fumi almeno nei prossimi giorni. Si copra bene la gola se esce di casa, e cerchi di rimanere al caldo." Also: "Prego, si accomodi!", "Mi dica", "apra la bocca", "mangi cibi morbidi", "Torni da me se la situazione non migliora"
pages.lesson_rules: None stated; the dialogue itself is a curated inventory of formal imperatives: si accomodi, mi dica, apra, si riposi, stia, prenda, non fumi, si copra, cerchi, mangi, torni

pages.label: imperativo formale e informale
pages.format: Dropdown-selection exercise (Hot Potatoes): 'Choose the correct form from the dropdown menu'; item bank JS-locked, no items recoverable after retry
pages.skills_tested: Choosing between tu and Lei imperative of the same verb from register cues in the sentence (addressee, politeness markers)
pages.traps_and_distractors: By design the dropdown pairs the tu and Lei form of one verb (e.g. mangia/mangi, legga/leggi), exploiting the -a/-i ending mirror; specific items not visible
pages.steals: Nothing recoverable; the format itself (same verb, two registers, context decides) is the steal
pages.lesson_rules: None on page; links to The Imperative lesson

pages.label: imperativo parte 2
pages.format: Lesson page with conjugation/pronoun-placement tables (parlare, scrivere, partire across tu/Lei/noi/voi) + link to a companion exercise 'Esercizio - Imperativo con i pronomi - B2'
pages.skills_tested: Imperative + clitic placement across the full paradigm: direct pronouns, indirect pronouns, combined pronouns (gliela), locative ci, reflexives; the three-way tu negative (non mangiarla / non la mangiare); Lei as the exception where clitics always precede
pages.traps_and_distractors: Placement variation is the trap axis: affirmative attaches, negative allows both attached-to-infinitive and preverbal, Lei never attaches — so *la mangi! (Lei affirmative with attached clitic) and *non mangiala are the natural wrong answers
pages.steals: "TU mangiala! / non mangiarla! / non la mangiare!" and combined-clitic "TU scrivigliela! / non scrivergliela! / non gliela scrivere!"
pages.lesson_rules: Quoted rule: "For persons 'tu', 'noi' and 'voi' the reflexive pronoun is joined to the verb when it is affirmative, while in negative form it can be either joined to the verb or be separated and placed before the verb. With formal person 'Lei', the pronoun ALWAYS precedes the verb."

pages.label: stare + gerundio lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: formation rule + examples, no interactive exercise
pages.skills_tested: Conjugating stare in the present + forming the gerund (-ando for -are, -endo for -ere/-ire); irregular gerunds from Latin stems; knowing when NOT to use the progressive (Italian prefers simple present)
pages.traps_and_distractors: L1-English over-use of progressive is explicitly flagged: 'Vado, ciao!' is preferred over 'Sto andando, ciao!'; irregular stems (facendo not *farendo, dicendo not *direndo, bevendo not *berendo) are the formation traps
pages.steals: "Lisa sta leggendo.", "Sto aspettando il mio turno." and the anti-example pair "Vado, ciao!" (not "Sto andando, ciao!")
pages.lesson_rules: Formation: stare (present) + gerund; "-are verbs: adding the suffix -ando to the root; -ere/-ire verbs: adding the suffix -endo to the root" (lavorare→lavorando, leggere→leggendo). Irregular gerund list: fare-facendo, dire-dicendo, bere-bevendo. Usage note: "this tense form is used less frequently in Italian than in English."

pages.label: stare facendo
pages.format: Sentence-transformation exercise (Hot Potatoes): rewrite present-indicative sentences in the present progressive, as in the example; item bank JS-locked
pages.skills_tested: Transforming simple present → stare + gerundio: conjugating stare to match the subject AND deriving the gerund from the lexical verb in one step
pages.traps_and_distractors: Typed transformation, so errors are stare agreement (sta/stanno) and gerund suffix choice (-ando vs -endo) plus irregular stems; item list not visible
pages.steals: Model transformation: "Lucia mangia la mela." → "Lucia sta mangiando la mela." — clean template for a transformation drill
pages.lesson_rules: Instruction only: "Rewrite the sentences, as in the example"

pages.label: gerundio semplice lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: formation rule, four numbered use cases with before/after transformations, pronoun-placement note
pages.skills_tested: Four adverbial uses of the bare gerund — temporal (simultaneity), causal (perché-replacement), modal (come-replacement), conditional (se-replacement); clitic attachment to the gerund; negated gerund (non + gerundio) with conditional force
pages.traps_and_distractors: Mapping the gerund to the right implicit clause type is the discrimination axis (same form, four meanings); the 'riflessivo affettivo' example (mangiandoci un panino) is flagged as a confuser; pronouns always attach AFTER the gerund forming one word
pages.steals: "Viaggiando in treno, mi piace leggere" (temporal); conditional-with-negation "Non studiando abbastanza, non passerai mai quell'esame"; affective reflexive "mangiandoci un panino"
pages.lesson_rules: Formation: "-are verbs take suffix -ando; -ere and -ire verbs take suffix -endo." Pronoun rule: pronouns "always go after the participle and form a single word with it." Uses enumerated: temporal, causal, modal, conditional.

pages.label: gerundio composto lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: brief formation + usage text with examples, no interactive exercise
pages.skills_tested: Forming avendo/essendo + past participle; anteriority semantics (action completed before the main-clause action, in any tense frame); past-participle agreement when a direct clitic attaches to the compound gerund
pages.traps_and_distractors: Auxiliary choice avendo vs essendo (mentioned but not deeply explored); agreement trap with attached direct pronoun (avendola aiutatA); semplice-vs-composto choice when the time relation is anterior rather than simultaneous
pages.steals: "Avendo studiato matematica, so risolvere le equazioni." and the agreement showcase "Avendola aiutata a studiare, Penelope mi ha ringraziato..."
pages.lesson_rules: Formation: "present participle of 'essere' or 'avere' + the past participle of the main verb." Use: "in clauses which refer to an action taking place before another, in the present, past or future." Agreement: "If the perfect participle is used with a direct pronoun, the past participle needs to agree with the gender of the pronoun."

pages.label: gerundio: presente o passato?
pages.format: Dropdown exercise (Hot Potatoes): 'Select the best option from the dropdown menu', gerundio semplice vs composto; item bank JS-locked after retry
pages.skills_tested: Choosing simple vs compound gerund from the time relation between the gerund clause and the main clause (simultaneous vs anterior)
pages.traps_and_distractors: Dropdown presumably pairs facendo vs avendo fatto for the same verb; anteriority cues (già, dopo che-paraphrases, main verb in passato) vs simultaneity cues are the deciding evidence; specific items not visible
pages.steals: Nothing recoverable beyond the format
pages.lesson_rules: None on page; links to the participle/gerund lesson

pages.label: gerundio o participio?
pages.format: Typed gap-fill (Hot Potatoes): 'Read the sentences and conjugate the verb in brackets' choosing present vs past participle/gerund; item bank JS-locked after retry
pages.skills_tested: Deciding between gerund (-ando/-endo) and past participle (-ato/-uto/-ito) for a verb in brackets based on syntactic slot (adverbial clause vs adjectival/absolute construction)
pages.traps_and_distractors: The bait is the English -ing overlap: both Italian forms can translate English participles; agreement endings on the participle add a second error axis; items not visible

pages.label: gerundio semplice e composto
pages.format: Dropdown exercise (Hot Potatoes): 'Choose the best option from the dropdown menu', simple vs compound gerund; item bank JS-locked after retry
pages.skills_tested: Same discrimination as gerundio_passato_c1: simultaneity → semplice, anteriority → composto; plus auxiliary selection inside the composto option (avendo vs essendo + participle)
pages.traps_and_distractors: Presumed distractor pairs like partendo / essendo partito; essendo vs avendo mismatch is a likely third option; items not visible
