pages.label: infinito, gerundio o participio?
pages.format: Typed gap-fill (Hot Potatoes): 'Conjugate the verbs in the infinitive, present participle or past participle, as most appropriate' — a three-way modi indefiniti production task; item bank JS-locked after retry
pages.skills_tested: Three-way choice among infinitive, gerund, and past participle for one bracketed verb per gap — the full indefinite-moods discrimination in production
pages.traps_and_distractors: Prepositional governance is the implicit trap (after prepositions Italian requires the infinitive where English uses -ing: prima di partire not *prima di partendo); items not visible
pages.steals: Nothing recoverable; the three-way production format itself is worth copying
pages.lesson_rules: None on page; links to the uses-of-the-infinitive lesson

pages.label: l'infinito lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: numbered reference list of ~11 uses of the infinitive with examples
pages.skills_tested: Infinitive after modals (volere/potere/dovere); after verbs governing di; in implicit temporal, causal, purpose, consequence, manner, and exception clauses; instructional/imperatival infinitive; infinitive as a noun; present vs past infinitive (fare vs aver fatto)
pages.traps_and_distractors: Preposition selection before the infinitive (di vs a vs per vs da) and telling purpose from consequence clauses; nominal vs verbal readings of the infinitive; past-infinitive apocope (aver rapinato, aver detto)
pages.steals: "Devo svegliarmi presto domani. Puoi spegnere la televisione?" (modal + attached reflexive) and the proverb "Tra il dire e il fare c'è di mezzo il mare" (infinitive as noun with article)
pages.lesson_rules: Explicit rule: when the infinitive functions as a noun, "If it requires an article, it is always masculine." Past infinitive illustrated with "aver rapinato", "aver detto" (auxiliary avere apocopated to aver)

pages.label: fare + infinito
pages.format: Lesson page: causative construction rules with bolded examples, no interactive exercise
pages.skills_tested: Causative fare + infinitive semantics (subject doesn't act, causes someone else to); expressing the agent with 'a'; agent demoted to 'da' when the infinitive already has both objects; direct vs indirect clitic choice depending on the infinitive's transitivity; reflexive verbs restructured under fare
pages.traps_and_distractors: Clitic case is the trap: intransitive infinitive → direct pronoun on fare (lo faccio dormire), transitive infinitive with its own direct object → the causee becomes indirect (gli faccio leggere il giornale); a vs da for the agent when objects pile up
pages.steals: "Ho fatto comprare la macchina a mio fratello" (agent introduced by a) and "Gli faccio leggere il giornale" (causee as indirect clitic because the infinitive keeps its direct object)
pages.lesson_rules: "We use this construction to explain that we don't carry out personally the action described by the verb but someone else does it." When the infinitive has an indirect object/agent, "we introduce it by the preposition 'a'"; with two objects the agent shifts to 'da'.

pages.label: usi del verbo fare
pages.format: Dropdown exercise (Hot Potatoes): 'Choose the best option from the dropdown menu', expressions/uses of fare; item bank JS-locked after retry
pages.skills_tested: Selecting the right fare expression/collocation (idiomatic and causative uses of fare) per sentence context
pages.traps_and_distractors: Not recoverable; presumably fare-collocations competing within one dropdown (fixed expressions vs literal uses)
pages.steals: Nothing recoverable

pages.skills_tested: Recognition-format version of the indefinite-moods choice: picking among infinitive/participle (and likely gerund) options for a slot

batch_tips: All 8 pure quiz pages on this site are Hot Potatoes apps whose item banks live in JavaScript the fetcher strips — only instructions and the single worked example are recoverable. The rule content and stealable material is almost entirely on the lesson pages and in the medico dialogue; do not budget more fetch attempts on the .html quiz pages.

The tu/Lei ending mirror is the load-bearing trap of the whole imperative cluster: -are verbs take -a (tu) but -i (Lei), -ere/-ire take -i (tu) but -a (Lei). Every formal/informal item can be built as a two-option dropdown of the same verb (mangia/mangi, leggi/legga) with only a register cue deciding.

Clitic placement gives a clean three-way B2 item type from the imperativo parte 2 paradigm: affirmative attached (mangiala), negative both ways (non mangiarla / non la mangiare — BOTH correct, so a great 'select all that apply'), Lei always preverbal (la mangi) — with *non mangiala and *mangila (Lei) as principled distractors.

The medico dialogue is a model for context-embedded imperative testing: one doctor turn naturally chains regular (prenda, torni), irregular (stia, dica), reflexive (si riposi, si copra, si accomodi) and negative (non fumi) Lei imperatives — copy the genre (doctor, waiter, receptionist) rather than isolated sentences.

For gerundio semplice vs composto, items hinge purely on time relation: simultaneity vs anteriority. Seed the sentence with an anteriority cue (main clause result state, 'so risolvere le equazioni') to force composto; add the agreement twist only when a direct clitic attaches (avendoLA aiutatA).

Two rules-of-thumb from lessons worth encoding as explicit feedback strings: infinitive-as-noun 'if it requires an article, it is always masculine'; and progressive under-use 'Italian says Vado, ciao! where English says I'm going' — the anti-example pair is more instructive than the rule.

Causative fare items should test clitic case, not translation: intransitive infinitive → direct clitic on fare; transitive infinitive keeping its object → causee goes indirect (Gli faccio leggere il giornale); agent takes 'a' normally, 'da' when 'a' is already occupied.
new_micro_topics: Apocopated tu imperatives with apostrophe (fa', va', di', da', sta') vs indicative look-alikes (fai, vai) — spelling-level production drill
Irregular subjunctive-based Lei imperatives as a closed list (faccia, vada, dica, dia, stia, sia, abbia) paired against their tu counterparts (fa', va', di', da', sta', sii, abbi, sappi)
Negative tu imperative = non + infinitive, with the double clitic option (non farlo / non lo fare) as a both-correct item type
Lei imperative clitic rule: pronoun ALWAYS precedes (si accomodi, la mangi) — never attaches
Combined clitics on imperatives (scrivigliela / non gliela scrivere)
Irregular gerund stems from Latin (facendo, dicendo, bevendo)
Progressive restraint: when Italian prefers simple present over stare+gerundio (English L1 interference)
Four adverbial readings of the bare gerund (temporal, causal, modal, conditional) including negated conditional gerund (non studiando...)
Gerundio composto anteriority + past-participle agreement with attached direct clitic (avendola aiutata)
Clitics attach to the END of gerunds and infinitives forming one word (mangiandoci, svegliarmi)
Infinitive as masculine noun with article (il dire, il fare)
Past infinitive with apocopated auxiliary (aver detto, aver rapinato)
Causative fare + infinito: agent marking a vs da, and direct vs indirect clitic for the causee
Imperatival/instructional infinitive (recipes, signage) as an alternative command form

pages.label: pronomi diretti lesson
pages.format: Lesson page with rules and examples, no interactive items. Built around a deli-shopping dialogue: each full-noun sentence is shown, then restated with the pronoun replacing the noun (noun-to-pronoun substitution pairs).
pages.skills_tested: Choosing lo/la/li/le by gender and number of the replaced noun; recognizing that direct pronouns share forms with definite articles and vary with 'the spelling of the word following'; ne as the quantity pronoun ('of it/of them'); agreeing quanto/quanta/quanti/quante with ne's referent.
pages.traps_and_distractors: None explicit (instructional page), but the substitution-pair presentation implicitly targets the article-pronoun confusion (gli yogurt -> li, NOT gli: plural article gli does not map to a direct pronoun gli) and gender assignment of food nouns.
pages.steals: "Vorrei due etti di mortadella, ma la vorrei affettata sottile" (fem sg la); "Vorrei un pezzo di parmigiano, lo vorrei fresco" (masc sg before vowel-word noun); "Quanto/Quanta/Quanti/Quante ne vuole?" as the ne-quantity frame. Nice pattern: gli yogurt -> li ho presi (article gli vs pronoun li contrast).
pages.lesson_rules: Stated rules: (1) 'Pronouns are sometimes actually the same words as the definite article (the), which change according to: masculine or feminine; singular or plural; the spelling of the word following.' (2) 'ne ... means "of it" or "of them" and is used when talking about quantity.' No formal mi/ti/lo/la/ci/vi/li/le table, no position or elision rules on this page; links onward to a broader 'Direct & Indirect Object Pronouns' lesson.

pages.label: pronomi diretti
pages.format: Multiple-choice exercise, page title 'Italian Grammar Exercise - Direct Object Pronouns', heading 'Direct Object Pronouns', instruction 'Choose the correct pronoun:'. Items are rendered by JavaScript/form widgets and were NOT extractable via fetch (confirmed on retry) — item count and sentences unavailable.
pages.skills_tested: By title and instruction: selecting the correct direct pronoun (mi, ti, lo, la, ci, vi, li, le) for a given sentence context. Specific distinctions untestable from fetch.

pages.label: pronomi combinati lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: intro explanation, five combination tables (one per indirect pronoun), example dialogues with translations, dedicated 'ne' section.
pages.skills_tested: Forming combined pronouns: vowel change i->e in mi/ti/ci/vi before a direct pronoun (mi+lo=me lo); gender collapse in third person (gli, le, and formal Le ALL become glie-); fusion vs separation spelling (glielo one word vs me lo two words); ordering indirect before direct before verb; combining ne with every indirect pronoun (me ne, te ne, gliene, ce ne, ve ne).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Lesson flags the spelling trap directly: writing separated combinations as one word collides with real nouns (mela = apple vs melo = apple tree) — so *melo/*tela-style fused spellings are the baited error; also the reverse error gli elo / glie lo for third person; and using le lo / gli la instead of gliela.
pages.steals: "Diego, mi puoi prestare la tua bicicletta per un paio d'ore?" / "Sì, te la presto, ma fai attenzione a non rovinarla." (te la formation + attached -la on infinitive in same exchange); "Marco non ci ha ancora dato gli inviti per la festa di venerdì." / "Stasera ce li darà sicuramente." (ce li with future); "Te ne do un'altra, se vuoi." (te ne + quantity).
pages.lesson_rules: Full tables quoted: mi+lo=me lo, mi+la=me la, mi+li=me li, mi+le=me le, mi+ne=me ne; ti+lo=te lo, ti+la=te la, ti+li=te li, ti+le=te le, ti+ne=te ne; gli/le/Le+lo=glielo, gli/le/Le+la=gliela, gli/le/Le+li=glieli, gli/le/Le+le=gliele, gli/le/Le+ne=gliene; ci+lo=ce lo, ci+la=ce la, ci+li=ce li, ci+le=ce le, ci+ne=ce ne; vi+lo=ve lo, vi+la=ve la, vi+li=ve li, vi+le=ve le, vi+ne=ve ne. Rules: order = 'indirect pronoun + direct pronoun + verb'; 'Only third person combined pronouns (the ones beginning with gli/le/Le) form a single word. In all other cases, the two pronouns remain separated' — separation avoids clashes with existing words (mela vs melo). Ne = 'di + thing', three listed uses: quantity, a part of something mentioned before, and verbs like 'parlare di'.

batch_tips: All seven interactive exercise pages on this site render items via JavaScript/form widgets that fetch-to-markdown strips: only titles and instruction lines are recoverable. Do not re-audit these URLs with WebFetch expecting items; a headless browser would be needed. Direct curl to onlineitalianclub.com and web.archive.org are both egress-policy blocked in this environment.

The site's own progression for this topic is a usable template: A1 recognition of single direct/indirect pronouns (dropdown) -> A2 participle agreement with preceding direct pronoun (lesson dialogues) -> B1 combined forms, first two recognition dropdowns then one production gap-fill. Recognition-before-production within the same micro-topic is their consistent pattern.

Their lesson-example style is paired mini-dialogues where the first turn establishes the referent noun and the second turn contains the pronoun — this forces referent-gender lookup and is directly reusable as an item stem format (e.g. 'Ho comprato una maglia...' / 'Quanto l'hai pagata?').

Strongest baits identified for item authoring: (a) invariable -o participle after avere when a direct pronoun precedes (li ho comprato*); (b) elided l' hiding gender so agreement must come from the antecedent; (c) fused vs spaced spelling of combined forms, with real-word collisions as motivation (mela/melo); (d) gli, le, and formal Le all collapsing to glie- (le lo* / gli la* as distractors); (e) definite article gli vs direct pronoun li (gli yogurt -> li).
new_micro_topics: Past-participle agreement with preceding direct pronoun under auxiliary avere (lo/la/li/le + -o/-a/-i/-e endings)
Elided l' before avere forms: recovering antecedent gender to choose participle ending (l'hai pagata vs l'hai pagato)
Fused vs spaced spelling of combined pronouns: glielo/gliela/glieli/gliele/gliene as one word vs me lo/te la/ce li/ve ne as two
Third-person gender collapse: gli, le, and formal Le all -> glie- before direct pronouns
ne combinations (me ne, te ne, gliene, ce ne, ve ne) and ne's three uses: quantity, part of something mentioned, verbs with di (parlarne)
quanto/quanta/quanti/quante agreement with the referent of ne in quantity questions
Definite-article/pronoun false friends: article gli (gli yogurt) vs direct pronoun li

pages.label: pronome relativo che
pages.format: Lesson page with rules and examples, no interactive exercise.
pages.skills_tested: Teaches (not tests) the three functions of che: (1) interrogative adjective/pronoun ('Che stai facendo?', 'Che autobus aspetti?'); (2) conjunction = 'that' ('Io penso che dobbiamo fare la spesa', 'Ho creduto che fossi andato a vivere all'estero'); (3) relative pronoun substituting subject or direct object while connecting two sentences.
pages.traps_and_distractors: N/A (lesson). The lesson's tripartite framing is itself the distractor map for any che item: interrogative vs conjunction vs relative.
pages.steals: "Ho conosciuto un ragazzo che suona la chitarra" (che = subject of relative clause); "Ho restituito il libro che avevo preso in prestito in biblioteca" (che = direct object) — a clean minimal pair for subject-che vs object-che.
pages.lesson_rules: Key rule verbatim: as a relative pronoun che "has a double function – it substitutes the subject or direct object while connecting two sentences." Explicitly separates this from interrogative che and conjunction che.

pages.label: pronome relativo doppio chi
pages.format: Lesson page with rules and paraphrase-equivalence examples, no exercise.
pages.skills_tested: Teaches chi as 'double relative' (antecedent + pronoun fused): chi = 'a person who / people who', replacing quello/colui/questo + che or qualcuno che; contrast with relative clauses after an antecedent, which require che even for people.
pages.traps_and_distractors: N/A (lesson). Supplies the two big traps for chi items: (1) using chi after an expressed antecedent (wrong — needs che); (2) plural verb agreement with chi (wrong — always 3rd singular).
pages.steals: Equivalence pairs ideal for transformation items: "Colui che fa tanto sport non sentirà fatica" = "Chi fa tanto sport non sentirà fatica"; "Uno che lavora nella caserma si chiama carabiniere" = "Chi lavora in caserma si chiama carabiniere".
pages.lesson_rules: Two gold rules, verbatim: (1) "'Chi' is ubiquitous both as a 'question word' (who) and a pronoun, but in relative clauses you need to use 'che' (that) instead, even to refer to people." (2) "The pronoun 'chi' can indicate a plural subject too, though the verb will still be conjugated in the 3rd person singular." Also lists the demonstratives/indefinites chi substitutes: quello, colui, questo, qualcuno/una.

pages.lesson_rules: Gold list, verbatim. batch_tips: All seven interactive exercise pages on this site are Hot-Potatoes-style JS apps: the item banks live in script tags that fetch-to-markdown strips, and direct HTML download plus Wayback are blocked by egress policy. Their concrete items, answers, and distractor pools are unrecoverable in this environment — treat this batch's exercise audits as format-only and mine the five lesson pages, which carried all the substantive content.

The site's architecture is consistently lesson-page + paired dropdown drill (each exercise links back to its lesson), so the lesson's rule list is a reliable proxy for what the paired drill's distractor pool covers.

Best formats to replicate: (1) es6_2's binary REL/CON tagging of che — a cheap atomic identification drill that is the natural prerequisite gate before che-vs-cui production items; (2) the possessive-relatives lesson's two-sentence-merge pattern as a transformation exercise (Laura e' la mia amica + Ho scritto un libro sulla sua vita -> Laura, sulla cui vita...).

Rule-of-thumb gold worth encoding as feedback strings: chi never follows an expressed antecedent (use che, even for people); chi always takes 3rd person singular verb even with plural meaning; cui appears only after a preposition; che/cui are both invariable and both replaceable by il/la quale, i/le quali; ci covers noi-reflexive, c'e'/ci sono, a+place, con/su+X; ne covers quantity, di+X, da+X.

Verification warning: the possessive-relatives lesson misstates its own agreement rule ("the article before the pronoun agrees subject of the relative pronoun" — garbled) and its example 'alla cui casa siamo andati' is stylistically forced. State the correct rule independently (article agrees in gender/number with the possessed noun after cui) rather than copying the site.
The ne example 'ne ho gia' mangiate tre' silently demonstrates past-participle agreement with ne — the site never calls it out, which is a gap Linguics can own as an explicit micro-skill.
new_micro_topics: che function tagging: relative pronoun vs conjunction vs interrogative (identification drill)
subject-che vs direct-object-che within relative clauses (minimal pairs)
chi as fused/double relative = colui che / quelli che / qualcuno che (paraphrase transformation)
chi agreement: 3rd person singular verb despite plural referent
*chi after an antecedent (error-spotting: la persona chi -> che)
preposition selection before cui driven by verb/noun valency (di/a/con/per/in/su cui)
che <-> il quale / cui <-> preposition + il quale substitution with gender-number agreement
possessive relative il/la/i/le cui: article agreement with possessed noun, not possessor
two-sentence merge into preposition + article + cui appositive clause (transformation format)
ci vs ne disambiguation by underlying phrase: a+place vs di+thing vs da+place vs con/su+X vs quantity
past participle agreement with partitive ne (ne ho mangiate tre)
lexicalized pronominal verbs with ci/ne: andarsene, pensarci, contarci, volerci/metterci

pages.skills_tested: a + city vs in + country (gaps 1-2: 'vacanza __ Bologna, __ Italia'); di for course subject ('corso __ italiano'); a scuola bare vs al bar/al pub/al cinema contracted vs a teatro bare vs in discoteca vs in centro vs a casa (gaps 4, 7, 10-15 — the article/no-article and a/in lexical-place system); dalle...alle time ranges (gaps 5-6); da + provenance ('venivano __ tanti paesi'); parlare di + topic (gap 9)
pages.traps_and_distractors: Traps are built from near-identical place nouns taking different patterns: al cinema but a teatro; al pub but in discoteca; in centro but a casa. City/country pair placed adjacently (a Bologna, in Italia) so one rule answers both wrongly if overgeneralised. Time gap pair forces articulated dalle/alle, not simple da/a
pages.steals: "andare (11) __ pub o (12) __ cinema, (13) __ teatro, (14) __ discoteca o stare (15) __ casa" — five leisure-place gaps in one sentence, each a different pattern (al/al/a/in/a) — a superb single-item diagnostic; also "c'era lezione (5) __ 9.30 (6) __ 13.30" for dalle...alle
pages.lesson_rules: None stated; purely applied

pages.label: preposizioni semplici lesson
pages.format: Reference lesson: per-preposition rule inventory with examples for all nine simple prepositions; exercises linked separately
pages.skills_tested: Full functional map of each preposition: di (possession, quality, time, place, reason, quantity 'una certa quantità di', fixed phrases, verb government); da (origin in time and space, source, movement, period, separation/difference, function/specification, consequence, professional context); a (destination, time, 'stare a + infinitive'); in (motion to large geo areas, manner, material, transformation, quantity, specialization); tra/fra (place or time); con (accompaniment, relationships, means, letter closings, verb government); su (place, time, topic, approximation with numbers, proportions); per (movement/place, time, manner, reason, purpose, 'stare per + infinitive', percentage)
pages.traps_and_distractors: Lesson-level, but it names the trap axes: in vs English 'to' for nations/big islands/regions; su = 'about' competing with parlare di; per as a place preposition (unlike English); da for function competing with di
pages.steals: "Vado in Europa / in Sardegna / in via Zamboni / in centro / in chiesa / in ufficio" — one line showing in spanning continent, big island, street address, and bare institutional nouns; "Vengo dall'Italia" vs "Vado a casa" as the origin/destination minimal frame
pages.lesson_rules: Verbatim highlights: "Unlike in English, in Italian we use 'in' (not 'to') as a preposition of movement when referring to nations, continents, big islands, regions" | da "expresses something's origins, whether in terms of time and space" | su used for "approximation with numbers" and topic "like 'about' in English" | "Unlike in English 'per' can be a preposition of movement/place" | aspectual pair: 'stare a + infinitive' (informal ongoing) vs 'stare per + infinitive' (about to) | a is "both a preposition of time and of movement"

pages.label: preposizioni articolate lesson
pages.format: Reference lesson: full contraction table plus idiomatic fixed expressions; no embedded exercises
pages.skills_tested: Which preposition+article pairs fuse into one word vs stay separate; articulated forms inside fixed idioms
pages.traps_and_distractors: The table itself encodes the traps: con contracts only optionally and only with il (col/con il); per, tra, fra NEVER contract (per il, tra il, fra il) — distractor forms like 'pel', 'tral', 'perla' are always wrong; gli-row forms (degli, negli, sugli) vs i-row forms (dei, nei, sui) hinge on noun-initial sound

pages.lesson_rules: Core rule verbatim: "Articulated (compound) prepositions are formed from a preposition and the definite article." Full table recovered: di→del/dello/della/dei/degli/delle; a→al/allo/alla/ai/agli/alle; da→dal/dallo/dalla/dai/dagli/dalle; in→nel/nello/nella/nei/negli/nelle; su→sul/sullo/sulla/sui/sugli/sulle; con→col or con il, otherwise uncontracted; per/tra/fra→never contract

pages.label: prima di
pages.format: Short lesson page: one construction rule + example sentences; no exercises embedded or linked
pages.skills_tested: prima di + infinitive, restricted to same-subject clauses; note the page does NOT teach the prima che + subjunctive contrast — that gap is an opportunity

batch_tips: Extraction constraint for this site: all interactive drills (dropdown and type-in) keep their items inside form/JS elements that the fetch pipeline strips, and direct HTML access to onlineitalianclub.com is blocked by egress policy — so item-level steals must come from lesson pages and the rare body-text cloze (un po' di preposizioni). Budget audit time accordingly on future batches.

The site's distractor model for dropdowns is a FIXED option set per exercise (e.g. always a/in/di/per/con), not per-item handcrafted distractors — Linguics can beat this easily with targeted per-item confusables.

Steal the connected-narrative cloze design (un po' di preposizioni): 15 gaps in one coherent story, with adjacent gaps forming minimal pairs (a Bologna / in Italia; al cinema / a teatro / in discoteca). Far higher signal than isolated sentences.
Steal the word-bank rubric 'You may use them more than once. Not all are used.' — it disables both elimination and one-to-one matching strategies in bank-based gapfills.

Level progression on this site: A1 = binary a/in; A2 = 9-way simple + first articolate + narrative cloze; B1 = curated confusable subsets (in/da/a/per motion cluster); B2 = adds su/tra and 'best option' idiomaticity judgements; C1/C2 = open dropdown + idiom-frozen articulated forms. A sensible template for Linguics bucket ordering.

The C2 articolate lesson's non-contraction facts (per/tra/fra never contract; con contracts optionally and only as col) are the highest-value trap source in the batch — wrong forms like 'pel'/'tral' make perfect distractors.
The prima di lesson teaches only prima di + infinitive and explicitly states the same-subject constraint without covering prima che + subjunctive — build the contrast item set the site is missing.

-eria words take in (in pizzeria, in gelateria)
bare vs articulated place idioms: a teatro / a casa / a scuola vs al cinema / al bar / al pub vs in discoteca / in centro
dalle...alle articulated time ranges (lezione dalle 9.30 alle 13.30)
in + via + street name (in via Zamboni)
per/tra/fra never contract with articles; col as optional contraction of con il
articulated prepositions frozen in idioms (d'ora in poi, dalle stelle alle stalle, alla fine di)
su for approximation with numbers and su = 'about' vs parlare di
stare a + infinitive vs stare per + infinitive
da of function/specification (macchina da caffè) vs di of material/content
per as preposition of movement/place (unlike English)
prima di + infinitive same-subject constraint vs prima che + subjunctive
venire da + country/city for provenance vs essere di + hometown

pages.label: si impersonale lesson
pages.format: Lesson page with rules and examples only; no interactive exercise. Frames si impersonale as a transformation of 'la gente' sentences.
pages.skills_tested: Single micro-skill: choosing 3rd person singular vs plural verb after 'si' based on the number of the following noun (si passivante agreement). Also implicitly: recognising the construction as equivalent to a generic 'la gente/people' subject.
pages.traps_and_distractors: None (no exercise). The lesson itself pre-empts the classic error of assuming 'si' always takes a singular verb.
pages.steals: The minimal pair is worth stealing whole: 'A Bologna si mangia molta carne' vs 'A Bologna si mangiano molti gelati' — same city, same verb, only the object number flips the conjugation.
pages.lesson_rules: Core stated rule: 'You need to choose singular or plural according to the object of the verb.' Explicit walkthrough: 'molta carne – is singular so we use mangia' vs 'molti gelati – is plural so we use mangiano.' No exception lists on this page.

pages.label: si impersonale
pages.format: Production gap-fill, 8 items. Instruction: 'Conjugate the verbs in the correct form:' — learner types the si-form from an infinitive in brackets. (Note: despite the filename 'verbi_pronominali', the page is titled 'The Impersonal Form With Si'.)
pages.skills_tested: 1) si + 3sg for intransitives/no-object ('In campagna si dorme molto bene'); 2) si passivante plural agreement with PREVERBAL plural subject ('I vini bianchi si bevono sempre freddi', 'L'uva si raccoglie a settembre' — singular control); 3) postverbal plural subject ('Oggi si pubblicano molti più libri che in passato'); 4) plural adjective with singular si verb ('In treno si viaggia più comodi che in aereo'); 5) subordinate-clause si ('Tutti dicono che a Bologna si mangia molto bene').
pages.traps_and_distractors: No distractor options (typed answers), but the item set itself is engineered around the sg/pl bait: 'molto bene' adverb (no object → sg) sits next to 'molti più libri' (pl); 'l'uva' is a singular mass noun baiting plural because grapes are semantically plural; 'più comodi' baits a plural verb but takes singular 'si viaggia'.
pages.steals: 'I vini bianchi (bere) sempre freddi' → si bevono (preverbal subject, learner must still agree); 'In treno (viaggiare) più comodi che in aereo' → si viaggia più comodi (singular verb + plural adjective in one item). Also 'Non (chiedere) mai l'età ad una signora' → non si chiede (singular object 'l'età').
pages.lesson_rules: Instruction only: 'Conjugate the verbs in the correct form:'. All 8 sentence stems recovered verbatim.

pages.label: si spersonalizzante lesson
pages.format: Rule-based lesson page with examples, no exercises (links onward to the B2 dropdown exercise).
pages.skills_tested: Covers four sub-rules: agreement with direct object number/gender; default 3sg when no object; auxiliary selection in compound tenses; combining si with reflexive verbs (ci si).
pages.traps_and_distractors: None (no exercise), but the auxiliary rule is explicitly framed against the learner instinct to keep 'avere' verbs with avere.
pages.steals: 'Se non si è studiato abbastanza, è difficile superare il test finale' — compound-tense impersonal with essere auxiliary but invariable participle (studiato, not studiati) because 'studiare' here has no object. Pairs beautifully against 'si è stati' items from the C1 exercise.
pages.lesson_rules: Quoted rules: (1) 'The impersonal use of si allows us to form sentences without referring to a subject or agent.' (2) With a direct object 'the verb is always conjugated following the number and the gender of the object.' (3) With no direct object 'the verb is always conjugated as the 3rd singular person.' (4) In past tenses 'we always use the auxiliary verb essere, even if the verb would normally require avere.' (5) For reflexive verbs 'we use the personal pronoun ci' (i.e. ci si) — page phrases it as when the speaker wishes to include themselves.

pages.label: si spersonalizzante
pages.format: Dropdown multiple choice, 6 items. Instruction: 'Choose the best option from the dropdown menu:'. Sentence stems recovered; the dropdown option lists are generated by JavaScript and could not be extracted.
pages.skills_tested: Choosing the right si-form in context: si + sg verb with adverb ('In quel ristorante ___ molto bene' → si mangia); si passivante plural with plural object ('Se non ___ bene le strade della città' → si conoscono); si + modal ('In classe ___ stare attenti' → si deve); ci si with reflexive/pronominal verbs ('Quando ___ dei propri errori' → ci si accorge/ci si rende conto; 'Nel fine settimana di solito ___' → ci si riposa, coordinated with 'si ha più tempo libero'); si è + participle/adjective ('quando ___ a dormire fino a tardi' → si è abituati).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Exact options unrecoverable, but the stems show the intended contrasts: si vs ci si (reflexive bait), sg vs pl verb ('le strade' plural), and si è abituati vs si abitua. The riposa item baits plain 'si riposa' where the pronominal 'riposarsi' demands 'ci si riposa'.
pages.steals: 'Quando ___ dei propri errori, è giusto cercare di migliorare' (ci si accorge — tests the ci si rule inside a natural sentence); 'E' difficile alzarsi presto la mattina quando ___ a dormire fino a tardi' (si è abituati — singular essere + plural participle).
pages.lesson_rules: Instruction only. Six stems recovered verbatim (quoted in fields above).

pages.label: si + verbo
pages.format: Production gap-fill, 10 items. Instruction: 'Complete the sentences by conjugating the verbs (in brackets):'. All stems recovered.
pages.skills_tested: 1) sg/pl agreement: 'In Italia si (mangiare) molti gelati' → mangiano vs 'si (guidare) a destra' → guida; 2) mass-noun trap: 'Quando si (conoscere) molta gente' → si conosce (semantically plural, grammatically singular); 3) coordinated plural subjects: 'In Germania si (parlare) bene sia il tedesco sia l'inglese' → si parlano; 4) fixed-expression sg: 'si (avere) mal di testa' → si ha, 'si (stare) in vacanza' → si sta; 5) compound tenses with plural participle: 'Quando si (essere interrogato)' → si è interrogati; 'Se si (essere stato) in Spagna' → si è stati; 6) chained impersonals in one sentence: 'Quando si (visitare) molte città, ci si sente padroni del mondo' → si visitano + ci si sente + plural predicate 'padroni'.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Typed answers, so traps live in the stems: 'molta gente' baits plural; 'sia...sia...' coordination baits singular; the bracketed forms '(essere interrogato)' and '(essere stato)' bait keeping the singular participle instead of pluralising to interrogati/stati.
pages.steals: 'In Germania si parlano bene sia il tedesco sia l'inglese' (coordination → plural, rarely drilled anywhere); 'Se si è stati in Spagna, si è probabilmente conosciuto uno spagnolo' (plural participle with essere-type vs singular participle with transitive in the same sentence — a two-rule item).
pages.lesson_rules: Instruction only; ten stems recovered verbatim.

pages.label: forma impersonale con essere
pages.format: Short lesson page: one rule statement plus four example sentences. No exercise.
pages.skills_tested: The si è + plural adjective pattern: singular 'essere' with plural predicate adjective in impersonal statements; also stacking two impersonals in one sentence.
pages.traps_and_distractors: None (no exercise). The inherent trap it teaches toward: learners write 'si è stanco' (singular adjective) because the verb is singular.
pages.steals: 'Se si è amici ci si dovrebbe aiutare' (si è + plural noun-adjective, then ci si + conditional modal — two constructions in eight words); 'Quando si è troppo stanchi si dorme male' (plural adjective, then singular verb clause).
pages.lesson_rules: Stated rule: the construction is 'si + 3rd singular person of essere', 'used in Italian in order to avoid stating a specific subject and be less direct', 'very simple to build.' Notably the page shows plural adjectives (amici, stanchi) in every example but never states the plural-adjective rule explicitly — a gap worth fixing in our own lesson.

pages.label: forma passiva lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: definition, then three parallel passive constructions of the same base sentence, plus further example sets. No exercise on-page.
pages.skills_tested: Recognising three interchangeable passive strategies for the same proposition: essere + participle ('Il bilancio dell'azienda è presentato ogni anno ai finanziatori'), si passivante ('Si presenta il bilancio dell'azienda ogni anno ai finanziatori'), and andare + participle for necessity ('Va presentato il bilancio dell'azienda ogni anno ai finanziatori'). Also modal + passive infinitive ('devono essere parcheggiate') and agent with 'da'.
pages.traps_and_distractors: None (no exercise). The three-way parallel invites transformation drills; venire-passive is conspicuously absent from the page.
pages.steals: The triple 'è presentato / si presenta / va presentato' of one identical sentence is the single best steal in the batch — a ready-made transformation drill template. Also 'Gli spaghetti si mangiano con la forchetta' as the canonical si-passivante example, and 'La mela è stata mangiata dal bambino.'
pages.lesson_rules: Quoted: 'In a passive form there's no need to mention the agent... the agent is implicit'; when specified, the agent takes 'the preposition da'. Andare + participle is presented as a passive of obligation/necessity.

pages.label: forma passiva 2
pages.format: Lesson page: explanation with five example sentences and a link to the B2 exercise. No on-page exercise.
pages.skills_tested: Formation with essere in compound tenses; distinguishing complemento d'agente (person agent) from complemento di causa efficiente (inanimate/animal agent), both introduced by 'da'; passive across tenses shown via examples but tense rules not stated.
pages.traps_and_distractors: None (no exercise).
pages.steals: 'La torta del mio compleanno è stata mangiata da tutti gli invitati' and 'Il vestito della sposa è stato preparato da mia sorella' — both show feminine participle agreement (stata mangiata/stato preparato) driven by the subject, good agreement-drill seeds.
pages.lesson_rules: Quoted: 'The auxiliary verb is always Essere' (venire/andare not mentioned on this page, in tension with the B1 lesson's andare-passive); passive is defined as: 'the subject is the recipient of the action indicated by the verb and the action itself is done by another element'. Terminology pair taught: complemento d'agente (people) vs complemento di causa efficiente (things/animals), both with 'da'.

batch_tips: The whole si_passiva strand on this site is built on ONE load-bearing atom repeated at every level: verb number agrees with the noun after/around si, not with an implied 'people'. Their escalation path: adverb-only 3sg (A1) -> plural noun agreement (A1/B2) -> mass nouns and coordinated subjects (B2 si_verbo) -> compound tenses with plural participles (B2/C1). Copy this ladder.

Best trap stems to reuse: 'molta gente' (semantically plural, takes singular), 'sia il tedesco sia l'inglese' (coordination takes plural), preverbal subject 'I vini bianchi si bevono' (agreement still applies when the noun precedes), and 'l'uva si raccoglie' (singular mass noun baiting plural).

The B1 forma passiva lesson's triple rendering of one sentence (e presentato / si presenta / va presentato) is a ready-made transformation-drill template; note the site never teaches the venire-passive anywhere in this batch, and the B2 passive lesson even claims 'the auxiliary verb is always Essere' — a genuine content gap Linguics can own.

The 'si e + plural adjective/participle' rule (si e stanchi, si e stati, si e interrogati, si e abituati) appears in four different pages but is never stated explicitly as a rule on the B1 lesson that showcases it — state it explicitly and drill the singular-adjective error directly.

Distractor design must be our own: all four interactive exercises hide options in Hot Potatoes-style JS that no fetch route rendered (dropdown option lists were never recoverable, only stems). Obvious option sets given the stems: sg vs pl verb swap, si vs ci si, avere vs essere auxiliary in compound tenses, singular vs plural participle.

Two pages (C1_si_spersonalizzante.html, B2_forma_passiva.html) fetched but their items are locked in JS; direct curl to the host is proxy-blocked (403 CONNECT) and r.jina.ai rate-limits (429) — if items are needed, retry r.jina.ai in a later session or a different egress.

si e + plural adjective (forma impersonale con essere: quando si e stanchi...)
compound-tense impersonal si: always essere auxiliary, with participle plurality split (si e mangiato vs si e stati/partiti)
si passivante with coordinated subjects (si parlano sia X sia Y)
si passivante with mass nouns (molta gente -> singular)
preverbal-subject si passivante (I vini bianchi si bevono)
andare + past participle as necessity passive (va presentato)
modal + passive infinitive (devono essere parcheggiate)
complemento d'agente vs complemento di causa efficiente (da + person vs da + thing/animal)

pages.label: andarsene
pages.format: Type-in gap-fill (Hot Potatoes JS cloze). Instruction: "Complete the sentences with 'andarsene' in the present or past, as in this example". Learner must produce the full clitic chain + conjugated verb, tense chosen from sentence context.
pages.skills_tested: Producing the double-clitic chain me ne / te ne / se ne / ce ne / ve ne / se ne matched to subject person; choosing presente vs passato prossimo from time cues; essere as auxiliary in the past; past participle agreement andato/a/i/e with the subject.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Individual items are embedded in JavaScript and not recoverable via fetch. Design-level traps implied by the task: writing *ci ne* instead of ce ne for noi, using avere as auxiliary, leaving participle unagreed (se ne è andato for Silvia), and misordering the chain (*ne se va*).
pages.steals: Model sentence given on page: "La festa a casa di Paolo era molto noiosa, infatti ce ne siamo andati presto." — good template: imperfetto context clause forcing a passato prossimo answer with plural agreement.
pages.lesson_rules: None stated on the exercise page itself (rules are on the companion lesson page).

pages.steals: No items visible; page confirms a single-verb conjugation drill is a viable A2 format (one verb, six persons, two tenses).
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: farcela 2

pages.skills_tested: Synonym transformation riuscire a → farcela; person switching between question and answer (tu question → io answer: 'Riesci...?' → 'ce la faccio'); producing natural short affirmative answers with the clitic chunk intact.

pages.label: farcela e andarsene lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: prose rules + full conjugation tables (present and past for both verbs) + 10 example sentences. No interactive exercise.
pages.skills_tested: Reference material covering: farcela present (ce la faccio...ce la fanno) and past (ce l'ho fatta...ce l'hanno fatta, participle always feminine fatta); andarsene present (me ne vado...se ne vanno) and past (me ne sono andato/a...se ne sono andati/e, participle agrees with subject).
pages.traps_and_distractors: n/a (lesson). But the tables expose the key asymmetry to test: farcela past participle is FROZEN feminine (fatta agrees with la), while andarsene past participle AGREES with the subject — an excellent contrast trap.
pages.steals: "Sono stanco. Non ce la faccio più a lavorare!" (non...più intensifier); "Signore, ce la fa ad essere qui in ufficio per le sette e mezzo?" (formal Lei form); "Siamo usciti presto di casa, ma non ce l'abbiamo fatta ad arrivare in tempo" (negative past + a-infinitive); "Perchè tu e Gianni ve ne siete andati senza salutare?" (compound subject → voi chain ve ne + andati).
pages.lesson_rules: "farcela is a pronominal verb... composed of a base verb, in this case 'fare', plus pronouns that modify the base verb's meaning" = to manage/achieve. andarsene "means 'to leave' in the sense of 'to quit a place or event'... often used instead of the simpler 'andare'"; the first pronoun is reflexive and changes with the subject while "ne" remains constant. Past of farcela uses avere with elided la: ce l'ho fatta.

pages.label: verbi pronominali al presente
pages.format: Gap-fill with a 4-verb word bank: "Complete the sentences with 'farcela', 'andarsene', 'cavarsela' or 'prendersela'", present tense only.
pages.skills_tested: Two-layer skill: (1) semantic selection among four pronominal verbs from sentence context, (2) present-tense conjugation with the right clitic chain — la-type invariant chains (ce la, se la) vs person-changing reflexive+ne (me ne vado).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Items in JS, not recoverable. The bank itself is the trap set: farcela vs cavarsela are near-synonyms ('manage'), forcing register/context discrimination; prendersela and cavarsela share the -sela shell so learners confuse which conjugates the first pronoun (both do: me la cavo, se la prende) vs farcela (ce never changes).
pages.steals: The 4-verb bank {farcela, andarsene, cavarsela, prendersela} is itself worth stealing as the canonical first mixed-selection set before the full C1 inventory.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page.

pages.label: verbi pronominali lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: structural rule, two model conjugations (andarsene, farcela present + past), and a glossed inventory of 22 idiomatic pronominal verbs. No exercise.
pages.skills_tested: Reference: morphological template of pronominal verbs; the la-feminine-participle rule for compound tenses; meaning of each idiom in the list.
pages.traps_and_distractors: n/a (lesson). List invites minimal-pair traps: avercela con vs volerne a (both 'have it in for someone'); metterci vs volerci (both 'take time'); non farcela più vs non poterne più.
pages.steals: The full glossed list (verbatim from page): avercela con qualcuno [to have it in for someone]; averne (abbastanza) [to have enough of something]; buttarla (sul tragico) [to exaggerate]; cavarsela [to manage to do something]; dirne (di tutti i colori) [to call somebody every name under the sun]; farla finita [to get it over it]; fregarsene di qualcosa [not to care about something]; legarsela al dito [to bear a grudge]; metterci [to take (time)]; mettercela tutta [to try one's hardest]; non farcela più; non poterne più; prendersela con qualcuno [to pick on somebody]; passarci sopra [to overlook]; passarsela (bene/male) [to be well/badly off]; provarci [to take a crack at]; sentirsela di fare qualcosa [to feel like doing something]; smetterla di [to give up]; uscirsene [to come out with]; vederci [to meet]; volerci [to take]; volerne a qualcuno [to have it in for someone].
pages.lesson_rules: Structure rule: pronominal verb = "the verb – a pronominal particle (ci / ne / la) – a reflexive pronoun"; when conjugating, "the reflexive pronoun changes according to the person while the pronominal particle remains unchanged". Compound-tense rule: with la-verbs "you should conjugate the past participle as feminine" (ce l'ho fatta).

pages.label: verbi pronominali 1

Requires handling three different chain types: ne-only (averne → ne ho abbastanza), sela (me la prendo / me ne frego is actually sene!, me la cavo).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Items in JS, not recoverable. The bank bait: fregarsene takes -sene (me ne frego) while prendersela/cavarsela take -sela (me la prendo, me la cavo) — mixing la/ne across the -rs- shell is the obvious trap; also averne needs a quantity word (abbastanza) to sound right.
pages.steals: The bank {averne, prendersela, fregarsene, cavarsela} as an 'attitude verbs' themed drill grouping.

pages.label: verbi pronominali 3

pages.label: metterci, mettere, mettersi
pages.format: Gap-fill: "Complete the sentences with an appropriate conjugation of 'mettere', 'metterci' or 'mettersi'" — base verb vs pronominal vs reflexive three-way contrast on one root.
pages.skills_tested: Distinguishing the three uses of one root: mettere (put something somewhere, transitive), metterci (time taken: ci metto due ore), mettersi (put on clothes / mettersi a + infinitive = start doing); then conjugating with the right pronoun (none / ci / reflexive si-series).

batch_tips: All interactive exercises on onlineitalianclub.com are Hot Potatoes-style JS quizzes: the actual items, dropdown options and answer keys live inside <script> arrays that the WebFetch markdown conversion strips. Only the title, instruction line, word bank, and any worked example in the instruction are recoverable. Word banks + worked examples are the reliably harvestable assets; do not budget time expecting full item lists.

The WebFetch proxy rate-limits this host hard: a burst of ~4 calls succeeds, then HTTP 429 for roughly 5+ minutes. Direct curl to the host is blocked outright by egress policy (CONNECT 403), and r.jina.ai mirror also 429s. Pace fetches at roughly one per 30-60s after an initial burst, and put lesson pages (which have all their content in HTML) first in the queue since they yield the most per call.

Site progression worth copying: A2 = single-verb conjugation drills (farcela alone, andarsene alone) plus a riuscire-a → farcela paraphrase Q&A format; A2/B2 bridge = 4-verb selection bank {farcela, andarsene, cavarsela, prendersela}; B1 = same-root three-way contrast (mettere/metterci/mettersi); C1 = themed 4-verb attitude bank {averne, prendersela, fregarsene, cavarsela}, a 10-verb idiom-stem bank, matching halves for recognition, and dropdown MCQ for chain discrimination.

Key testable asymmetry stated across the two lesson pages: la-type pronominal verbs take avere with a FROZEN feminine participle (ce l'ho fatta, regardless of subject), while ne-type reflexives like andarsene take essere with subject agreement (se ne sono andati/e). Items contrasting these two past-tense behaviours in one exercise would out-teach anything on the site.
new_micro_topics: Past participle agreement split: frozen feminine 'fatta' in ce l'ho fatta vs agreeing andato/a/i/e in andarsene
Elision in la-verb compound tenses: ce l'ho fatta vs *ce la ho fatta
Clitic chain selection by person: me/te/se/ce/ve/se + ne for andarsene vs invariant ce + la for farcela
-sela vs -sene shell confusion: me la prendo / me la cavo vs me ne frego / me ne vado
riuscire a + infinitive → farcela paraphrase transformation
farcela vs cavarsela near-synonym discrimination in context
metterci vs volerci for time (personal vs impersonal construction)
Same-root three-way contrast: mettere / metterci / mettersi (extendable to vedere/vederci, provare/provarci, passare/passarci/passarsela)
Idiom stems with obligatory frozen tails: darsela A GAMBE, legarsela AL DITO, mettercela TUTTA, tirarla PER LE LUNGHE, dirne DI TUTTI I COLORI, buttarla SUL TRAGICO
Negative-only pronominal idioms: non poterne più, non farcela più (with non...più intensifier)
Near-synonym idiom pairs for MCQ distractors: avercela con vs volerne a; prendersela con vs avercela con
Formal register conjugation of pronominal chunks: Lei → 'ce la fa a...?'
sentirsela di + infinitive and smetterla di + infinitive as complementation patterns
averne + quantity word (averne abbastanza) as collocation constraint

pages.label: congiunzioni lesson
pages.format: Lesson page with rules and examples (no interactive exercise) — a reference table of subordinating conjunctions grouped by semantic function, each tagged with the verb form it governs.
pages.skills_tested: Mood selection after subordinators, organised as six function groups: (1) reason conjunctions take INDICATIVE (perché, siccome, visto che, dato che, poiché, giacché, posto che); (2) purpose conjunctions take SUBJUNCTIVE (perché, affinché, cosicché) or infinitive with per; (3) result/consecutive: cosicché/tanto che/così tanto che/in modo che + indicative vs così da/tanto da/così tanto da/in modo da + infinitive; (4) contrast: benché/sebbene/nonostante/malgrado + subjunctive vs anche se + INDICATIVE vs pur + gerund; (5) time: quando/dopo che/una volta che/mentre/(non) appena/finché (non) + indicative, prima di + infinitive, prima che + subjunctive; (6) condition: se + subj/ind, semmai/qualora/nel caso che/nel caso in cui/purché/a patto che/a condizione che + subjunctive, solo che + indicative; modal: come se, senza che + subjunctive.
pages.traps_and_distractors: The page's own structure exposes the trap pairs: perché-reason (+ind) vs perché-purpose (+subj); anche se (+ind) sitting inside an otherwise all-subjunctive contrast group; prima di (+inf) vs prima che (+subj); che-forms (+finite verb) vs da/di-forms (+infinitive) in the result group; solo che (+ind) as the odd one out in the condition group.
pages.steals: "Non sono uscito perchè sta piovendo." (perché = reason, indicative) contrasted with "Puoi uscire con i tuoi amici, purchè prima tu faccia i tuoi compiuti." (purché + subjunctive) — a ready-made minimal pair for a mood-selection drill.
pages.lesson_rules: Full governance lists (gold): REASON + indicative: perché, siccome, visto che, dato che, poiché, giacché, posto che. PURPOSE + subjunctive: perché, affinché, cosicché; per + infinitive. RESULT: cosicché/tanto che/così tanto che/in modo che + indicative; così da/tanto da/così tanto da/in modo da + infinitive. CONTRAST: benché, sebbene, nonostante, malgrado + subjunctive; anche se + indicative; pur + gerundio. TIME: quando, dopo che, una volta che, mentre, (non) appena, finché (non) + indicative; prima di + infinitive; prima che + subjunctive. CONDITION: se + subjunctive/indicative; semmai, qualora, nel caso che, nel caso in cui, purché, a patto che, a condizione che + subjunctive; solo che + indicative. MODAL + subjunctive: come se, senza che.

pages.steals: The 5-word closed set (però, infine, allora, invece, comunque) is a ready-made distractor pool for any B2 discourse-linker cloze.

pages.label: avverbi e congiunzioni come connettori
pages.format: Lesson page: linkers grouped by English gloss with verbatim example sentences and mood/construction notes.
pages.skills_tested: Choosing among same-meaning linkers with different grammar: siccome/poiché/visto che/dato che (because, +ind) vs sebbene/nonostante/benché (although, +SUBJUNCTIVE); inoltre (adverbial 'furthermore', starts a clause) vs oltre a ('apart from/besides', + infinitive); ormai vs già; a parte/eccetto/tranne (exception); però; perciò/quindi.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Built-in trap pairs: inoltre vs oltre a (learners use inoltre + noun/infinitive where oltre a is required); sebbene-group with indicative (needs subjunctive, incl. imperfect subj. in past); siccome-group confused with perché in clause-initial position.
pages.steals: "Oltre a saper parlare cinque lingue, sa usare bene il computer." (oltre a + infinitive) paired against "È puntuale, gentile e inoltre è molto disponibile." — perfect minimal pair for the inoltre/oltre a distinction. Also "Sebbene avesse chiamato a tutte le ore, nessuna segretaria le rispose." (sebbene + imperfect subjunctive in past narration).
pages.lesson_rules: Verbatim groupings: because/since = "siccome / poiché / visto che / dato che"; but/although = "sebbene / nonostante / benchè" (stated to require subjunctive); by now/already = "ormai / già"; furthermore = "inoltre"; apart from = "oltre a" (used with infinitives); exceptions = "a parte / eccetto / tranne"; but = "però"; so = "perciò / quindi". Examples: "Siccome ero ammalato, non sono andato a scuola per qualche giorno."; "Mi piace andare al mare, quindi l'estate prossima andrò in vacanza in Sardegna."

new_micro_topics: siccome vs perché vs quindi: cause/consequence and clause-position (siccome clause-initial only, perché post-main-clause)
perché-reason (+indicative) vs perché-purpose (+subjunctive)
prima di + infinitive vs prima che + subjunctive
anche se (+indicative) vs benché/sebbene/nonostante/malgrado (+subjunctive) vs pur + gerundio
result connectors: che-forms + indicative (cosicché, tanto che, in modo che) vs da-forms + infinitive (così da, tanto da, in modo da)
condition connectors + subjunctive (purché, a patto che, a condizione che, qualora, nel caso in cui, semmai) vs solo che + indicative
inoltre (adverbial linker) vs oltre a (+infinitive)
discourse-linker discrimination: però vs invece vs comunque vs allora vs infine
advanced contrast near-synonyms: ma/però/eppure/anzi/invece/tuttavia/piuttosto/al contrario/in realtà
come se / senza che + subjunctive (modal connectors)
exception linkers: a parte / eccetto / tranne
reverse mood-reading drill: given a conjugated verb (subj vs ind), pick the compatible conjunction
batch_tips: All interactive .html quiz pages on this site inject items via JavaScript; static fetch recovers only headings/instructions — do not expect item-level distractor data from them, but headings often name the exact closed word-set, which is the most useful artifact anyway.
The site's strongest design pattern for connettivi is the tiny closed set (3-5 near-synonym linkers) reused across a whole exercise — atomises well for Linguics drills.

The C2 congiunzioni lesson's function-group tables double as complete distractor pools: pick wrong-mood or wrong-construction members of the same semantic group (e.g. offer 'anche se' where subjunctive follows, or 'in modo che' before an infinitive).
One matching-format exercise (connettivi.html) breaks the dropdown monotony — sentence-half matching tests relation comprehension without production and is worth cloning.

Recurrent site-wide trap axes for connettivi: (1) mood government (subj vs ind), (2) finite che-clause vs infinitive da/di-construction, (3) clause position (siccome vs perché), (4) discourse relation (contrast vs consequence vs concession).

Note: WebFetch proxy rate-limits hard on parallel calls to this domain (HTTP 429, ~5+ min cooldown); fetch sequentially with ~20s gaps. Direct curl to onlineitalianclub.com is egress-policy blocked (403 CONNECT) in this environment.

pages.label: consonanti doppie
pages.format: Two listening exercises: (1) gap-fill where learner hears word pairs and inserts single vs double consonant into partially-written words; (2) 10-sentence listening cloze where whole missing words must be supplied from audio context.
pages.skills_tested: Auditory discrimination of single vs double consonants and mapping sound to orthography, across a wide consonant range (B C D F G L M N P R S T V Z); ex. 2 adds spelling doubles inside connected speech rather than isolated words.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Minimal-pair baiting: word pairs contrast a double-consonant word with a similar single-consonant word (sasso vs palazzo type pairs); trap is hearing length contrast, then spelling it. Sentence cloze hides the doubled word inside running speech (e.g. 'arrivato', 'mezz'ora').
pages.steals: Pair format 'ba___o / li___ro' (balzo vs libro), 'sa___o / pal___o' (sasso vs palazzo), 'pe___a / a___cora' (penna vs ancora); cloze: 'Il treno e' ___ ieri sera con ___'ora di ritardo' (arrivato / mezz).
pages.lesson_rules: Only framing statement: 'One of the biggest difficulty learners find with Italian are double consonants. They are not often easy to perceive, but it is fundamental to spell them correctly in written texts.' No orthographic rule list or exception list given.

pages.label: costruzione scissa
pages.format: Lesson page with rules and examples; links out to one interactive B2 exercise (not on-page).
pages.skills_tested: Distinguishing scissa esplicita ('e' + focused element + che + finite verb) from implicita ('e'/e' stata/sara' + focused element + a + infinitive); choosing correct tense of essere to carry the time reference in the implicit form; identifying which element is focalised.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Confusing che + finite verb with a + infinitive (wrong connector for the chosen construction); wrong preposition in implicita (che instead of a); putting tense on the wrong verb — in implicita the tense lives on essere ('E' stata Monica a cantare'), not on the infinitive.
pages.steals: 'No, e' Lucia che suona la chitarra' (esplicita) vs 'No, e' il cane a mangiare la carne' (implicita); past: 'E' stata Monica a cantare quel pezzo!'
pages.lesson_rules: Two structures stated: esplicita = e' [element] che [verb in appropriate tense], with essere in present; implicita = e'/e' stata/sara' [element] a [infinitive], where essere carries the action's time. Both constructions emphasise the element that follows essere.

pages.label: diminutivi e accrescitivi
pages.format: Lesson page with suffix lists and examples; links to one exercise: decide whether alterati are real or false (falsi alterati).
pages.skills_tested: Attaching the right suffix (-ino/a, -etto/a, -ello/a, -uccio/a, -otto/a diminutive; -one/a augmentative); recognising intensifying prefixes super-/stra- on adjectives; recognising falsi alterati (lexicalised words that only look altered); handling gender shifts of the base under alteration.
pages.traps_and_distractors: False diminutives are the explicit trap: cavalletto = easel, not 'small horse'. Gender-change traps: campana (f) -> campanello (m), porta (f) -> portone (m). Also semantic trap that alteration marks affect, not literal size ('sorellina' = affection, not necessarily younger).
pages.steals: 'bicchiere - bicchierino', 'tavolo - tavolino', 'cartello - cartellone'; affective example: 'Sento molto la mancanza della mia sorellina'; falso alterato: cavalletto.
pages.lesson_rules: Suffix inventory: diminutives -ino/a, -etto/a, -ello/a, -uccio/a, -otto/a; augmentative -one/a; prefixes super-, stra- (adjectives only). Rule: alterati mark size AND affective stance. Exception list (gold): cavalletto (meaning shift), campanello and portone (gender change from feminine base).

pages.label: finche / finche non
pages.format: Pure lesson page (rules + contrastive example pairs), no on-page exercise.
pages.skills_tested: The two readings of finche': 'fino al momento in cui' (until) vs 'per tutto il tempo che' (as long as), and knowing that non is meaning-neutral (optional, pleonastic) in the 'until' reading but meaning-REVERSING in the 'as long as' reading.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Core trap is the asymmetry itself: identical surface strings with opposite truth conditions. 'Sono stato bene finche' ho abitato a Roma' (well WHILE living there) vs 'finche' NON ho abitato a Roma' (well until/BEFORE living there) — perfect distractor pair for MCQ meaning-selection items.
pages.steals: 'Studiavo finche' mi sono addormentato. = Studiavo finche' non mi sono addormentato.' (non optional); contrast pair: 'Sono stato bene finche' ho abitato a Roma' vs 'Sono stato bene finche' non ho abitato a Roma = Sono stato bene prima di andare a vivere a Roma.'
pages.lesson_rules: Rule 1: when finche' = fino al momento in cui, 'non' is optional and changes nothing. Rule 2: when finche' = per tutto il tempo che, adding 'non' completely changes the sentence meaning. Side note: 'Affinche' simply means in order to.' No subjunctive/mood rules given.

pages.label: finche/affinche
pages.format: Interactive dropdown exercise ('Finche' o Affinche'? - 1'); items are injected by JavaScript and not extractable via fetch (curl to host blocked by proxy policy), so item count and sentences unavailable.
pages.skills_tested: Binary choice per gap: temporal finche' (until/as long as) vs final/purposive affinche' (so that) — near-homophone conjunction discrimination driven by clause semantics (and implicitly by mood: affinche' takes subjunctive).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Distractor set is the two near-identical conjunctions themselves; bait is phonetic similarity masking a time-vs-purpose semantic split. Specific item sentences hidden in JS.
pages.steals: None extractable — items JS-rendered. The transferable design is the pure two-option dropdown on a near-minimal-pair conjunction contrast.
pages.lesson_rules: Only instruction: 'Choose the correct option from the dropdown menu'; links back to the finche' lesson page for the rules.

pages.label: finche o affinche?
pages.format: Interactive dropdown exercise, second in the series ('Finche' o Affinche'? - 2'); same JS-rendered engine, items not extractable via fetch.
pages.skills_tested: Same contrast as exercise 1: temporal vs purposive conjunction choice in context, extending practice volume rather than adding a new distinction.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Two-way finche'/affinche' bait; sentences hidden in JS.
pages.steals: None extractable. Note the site's pattern of shipping the same micro-contrast as a numbered series (1, 2) for spaced volume — worth copying for Linguics drill sets.
pages.lesson_rules: Instruction only: 'Choose the correct option from the dropdown menu'; page defers rules to the finche' lesson.

pages.label: mica lesson
pages.format: Lesson page: intro plus three numbered rules, each with example sentences.
pages.skills_tested: Three uses of mica: (1) reinforcing non (= per niente), placed AFTER the verb; (2) replacing non entirely, placed BEFORE the verb; (3) introducing a tentative supposition/question (= 'perhaps/by any chance'). Position-meaning pairing is the skill.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Not stated explicitly, but the rule set implies the natural traps: using mica before the verb WITH non (double placement error), or mid-sentence position with the wrong pairing. Position-swap items write themselves from rules 1 vs 2.
pages.steals: 'Non ho mica detto che devi farlo per forza!' (reinforcement, post-verbal); 'Mica sono stupida! Ti vedo che stai copiando!' (replacement, pre-verbal, no non); 'Mica hai visto mia madre da queste parti?' (suppositional question = 'you haven't by any chance seen...').
pages.lesson_rules: Rule 1: reinforcing negation — mica follows the verb, meaning 'not at all' (per niente). Rule 2: replacing non — mica precedes the verb and no non appears. Rule 3: mica can introduce suppositions/questions meaning roughly 'perhaps'.

pages.label: mica
pages.format: Interactive word-order (chunk reorder) exercise: 'Form sentences by clicking on the sections to reorder them.' Items JS-rendered, not extractable via fetch.
pages.skills_tested: Placing mica correctly relative to verb and non when rebuilding sentences from shuffled chunks — directly operationalises the lesson's position rules (post-verbal with non, pre-verbal without).
pages.traps_and_distractors: Reorder format makes the position itself the trap: plausible-but-wrong orders like 'mica non ho detto' vs 'non ho mica detto'. Specific chunk sets hidden in JS.
pages.steals: None extractable; the format pairing is the steal — teach mica placement via reorder rather than MCQ, since the error IS an ordering error.
pages.lesson_rules: Instruction only: 'Form sentences by clicking on the sections to reorder them'; rules live on the linked mica lesson.

pages.label: quando usare non
pages.format: Lesson page in two sections — 'grammatical non' vs 'pleonastic non' — with bullet rules and italicised examples.
pages.skills_tested: Sorting non into obligatory vs pleonastic: obligatory with mai, niente/nessuno/per niente (post-verbal), a meno che / senza che; pleonastic (optional, meaning-neutral) after non appena and finche'. Plus the negative-concord rule: no non when niente/nessuno precede the verb.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Explicit trap flagged: niente/nessuno/per niente BEFORE the verb excludes non ('Nessuno e' venuto' not 'Non nessuno e' venuto'), vs post-verbal position requiring it. Second trap: assuming non after finche'/non appena is negating (it isn't).
pages.steals: 'Non ho mai letto quel libro.'; 'Non ho comprato niente.'; 'Non appena saputa la notizia, corsi in ospedale agitato.'; 'Sono stato tranquillo finche' non ho ricevuto un rimprovero dalla professoressa.' (pleonastic non).
pages.lesson_rules: Grammatical-non context list: mai; niente/nessuno/per niente (when after verb); a meno che; senza che. Pleonastic-non context list: non appena; finche' — in these it is 'used optionally and does not change the basic meaning'. Word-order rule: negative words pre-verb = drop non.

pages.label: non pleonastico
pages.format: Interactive dropdown exercise: sentences contain NON in capitals; learner classifies each as 'pleonastic' or 'grammatically necessary'. Items JS-rendered, not extractable via fetch.
pages.skills_tested: Binary metalinguistic classification of a highlighted non — can the learner tell semantic negation from expletive non in finche'/non appena/comparative-type contexts.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Two-way classification design; bait presumably sentences where finche' non IS truly negating (the 'as long as' reading) mixed with pleonastic ones. Actual sentences hidden in JS.
pages.steals: The task design itself: 'Decide if the NON written in CAPITALS has a pleonastic meaning or whether it is grammatically necessary' — a classification drill (not production) that Linguics can reuse for expletive-non; item text not extractable.
pages.lesson_rules: Instruction quoted above; rules deferred to the 'quando usare non' lesson page.

pages.label: piuttosto che
pages.format: Short lesson page: definition, correct-usage examples with infinitives, synonym note, and an explicit warning section on the incorrect colloquial use.
pages.skills_tested: Correct 'rather than' preference use of piuttosto che + infinitive; recognising the widespread but censured colloquial use as a disjunctive 'or' between listed alternatives; synonym mapping to pur di non / per non.
pages.traps_and_distractors: The trap IS the topic: piuttosto che chained between options meaning 'or' — the page marks 'Possiamo guardare la tv, piuttosto che andare in discoteca, piuttosto che mangiare fuori' as incorrect, saying it is 'unacceptable to many people' and 'makes them shudder'. Ideal judged-usage MCQ material.
pages.steals: Correct: 'Piuttosto che guardare la tv anche stasera, potremmo andare al cinema.'; synonym: 'Pur di non lavare i piatti, sarebbe disposta a fare le pulizie per una settimana.'; incorrect (chained-or): the discoteca sentence above.
pages.lesson_rules: Rule: piuttosto che = 'rather than', expresses preference of one action over another, governs infinitives; near-synonyms pur di non / per non; the 'or'-listing use is explicitly proscribed as colloquial misuse.

pages.label: prefisso neo
pages.format: Interactive True/False exercise: 'Words With The Prefix Neo- — True or false?'. Statements JS-rendered, not extractable via fetch (retried; only header visible).
pages.skills_tested: Judging whether definitions/claims about neo- formations (type neonato, neolaureato, neologismo, neoclassico) are true — word-formation semantics of the prefix 'newly-/recently-'.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Presumably false glosses of neo- words (definition-mismatch bait); actual statements hidden in JS.
pages.steals: None extractable; the reusable design is true/false definition-judgement on a single productive prefix — cheap to author, tests morphological transparency.
pages.lesson_rules: None on page beyond the True/False framing; no rule text visible.

pages.label: parole difficili
pages.format: Lesson page: glossary-style list of 8 hard function/discourse words, each with meaning, usage note, and example sentences.
pages.skills_tested: Word list covered: man mano ('progressively', usually man mano che + clause); assai (adverb 'very' vs adjective 'many' — disambiguate by context); appunto ('exactly', confirmation); addirittura ('even', marks surprise); a tal proposito ('in that regard'); pur (concessive 'although', combines with GERUND); anzi (correction vs strengthening of prior statement); macche' (emphatic 'absolutely not').
pages.traps_and_distractors: Built-in traps: assai part-of-speech ambiguity; anzi's two opposite functions (contradict vs intensify) resolvable only from context; pur mid-sentence use ('dovrai pur trovare un po' di tempo') vs pur + gerund concessive — same word, different construction.
pages.steals: 'Man mano che salivamo, la strada si faceva piu' stretta.'; 'L'esame e' stato assai difficile (=molto difficile)'; 'Pur avendo studiato per tanti anni spagnolo, alcune regole grammaticali non so come si usino.'; 'Non e' una brutta idea. Anzi, andiamo a fare due passi in centro.'
pages.lesson_rules: Rules stated: pur takes gerund constructions and can also appear mid-clause with modal flavour; anzi requires reading the preceding sentence to decide contradiction vs reinforcement; assai must be disambiguated adverb/adjective by context; macche' = emphatic negation. The 8-word list itself is the gold exception-list for a C1 discourse-adverb bucket.

pages.label: word order: lingua straniera
pages.format: Interactive chunk-reorder exercise: click words/phrases in order to reconstruct QUESTIONS about learning Italian/foreign languages; tied to a conversation-lesson page and an interview audio. Items JS-rendered, not extractable.
pages.skills_tested: Italian interrogative word order at chunk level (question word / verb / clitic / complement sequencing) using authentic conversation questions as targets.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Reorder distractors are inherent (any wrong permutation); specific chunking hidden in JS.
pages.steals: Design steal: questions come from a real interview learners can listen to before/after — reorder as pre/post-listening task. No item text extractable.
pages.lesson_rules: Instruction: 'Click on the words/phrases in the correct order to reconstruct the questions about learning languages'; suggests studying the linked questions first or listening to the interview.

pages.label: word order: lavori o studi
pages.format: Interactive chunk-reorder exercise reconstructing a DIALOGUE from a native-speaker interview ('Lavori o studi?'); listen first, then reorder. Items JS-rendered, not extractable.
pages.skills_tested: Word order in conversational turns (statements and questions about work/study), sequencing of spoken-register chunks, listening-to-syntax mapping.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Permutation-based; specifics hidden in JS.
pages.steals: Design steal: dialogue reconstruction (not isolated sentences) — order within AND across turns; paired audio at 'Read/listen to the original interview'.
pages.lesson_rules: Instructions: 'Listen to the interview with an Italian native speaker. Then click on the words/phrases in the correct order to reconstruct the dialogue.'

pages.label: word order: psicologia
pages.format: Interactive chunk-reorder exercise reconstructing QUESTIONS from the 'psicologia' conversation-lesson prompts. Items JS-rendered, not extractable.
pages.skills_tested: Interrogative word order with abstract/opinion vocabulary (psychology conversation questions); chunk sequencing.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Permutation-based; specifics hidden in JS.
pages.steals: Source of targets: https://onlineitalianclub.com/italian-conversation-lessons-psicologia/ — the conversation-prompt page holds the actual sentences if item harvesting is needed.

pages.label: word order: tecnologia
pages.format: Interactive chunk-reorder exercise reconstructing EXTRACTS from the interview 'Tu e la tecnologia'; can be done before or after the listening. Items JS-rendered, not extractable.
pages.skills_tested: Declarative word order in spoken-register extracts about technology habits; chunk sequencing tied to listening comprehension.
pages.traps_and_distractors: Permutation-based; specifics hidden in JS.
pages.steals: Instruction worth copying for flexible sequencing: 'Click on the words/phrases in the correct order to reconstruct the extracts. Or do this exercise first, and then the listening - it's up to you!'
pages.lesson_rules: Instruction only, as quoted; targets sourced from the linked native-speaker interview page.

batch_tips: Site architecture pattern: rules live ONLY on the lesson pages; the /free_italian_exercises/*.html drill pages carry a one-line instruction and JS-injected items. All item text on the interactive pages is unrecoverable via fetch (markdown conversion strips scripts, and direct curl to onlineitalianclub.com is proxy-denied 403), so harvest items from the linked source pages instead — e.g. the word-order exercises pull their sentences from public conversation-lesson/interview pages like /italian-conversation-lessons-psicologia/.

Strong recurring format-to-skill pairings worth copying: two-option dropdown for near-minimal-pair conjunctions (finche'/affinche'); binary classification ('pleonastic vs necessary') for expletive non; chunk reorder for anything where the error is positional (mica placement, question word order); true/false definition-judgement for productive prefixes (neo-).

The finche' cluster is a coherent three-layer drill set: (a) meaning-flip of finche' vs finche' non under the 'as long as' reading, (b) pleonastic-vs-negating classification of non, (c) finche'/affinche' form discrimination — the lesson pages supply verbatim contrast pairs ('Sono stato bene finche' [non] ho abitato a Roma') that convert directly into MCQ meaning-selection items.

Best exception word-lists in the batch: falsi alterati and gender-shift alterati (cavalletto, campanello, portone) on the diminutives page; the obligatory-non context list (mai, niente/nessuno post-verbal, a meno che, senza che) vs pleonastic list (non appena, finche'); the 8-word C1 discourse lexicon (man mano, assai, appunto, addirittura, a tal proposito, pur, anzi, macche').

OnlineItalianClub ships the same micro-contrast as numbered exercise series (Finche' o Affinche' 1 and 2) — pure volume/spacing, no new distinction — which supports Linguics' atomised-drill model of many small parallel sets per micro-skill.
new_micro_topics: falsi alterati: real vs fake diminutives/augmentatives (cavalletto, campanello, portone) as a classification drill
gender shift under alteration: feminine base to masculine alterato (campana>campanello, porta>portone)
costruzione scissa implicita vs esplicita: che+finite vs a+infinitive, with tense carried by essere
mica position-meaning pairing: post-verbal with non vs pre-verbal replacing non vs suppositional-question mica
negative concord word order: drop non when nessuno/niente precede the verb
pleonastic non classification after non appena / finche' / comparatives (necessary vs expletive)
finche' meaning-flip: non optional under 'until' reading but meaning-reversing under 'as long as' reading
piuttosto che prescriptive trap: preference + infinitive vs proscribed disjunctive-'or' chaining; synonyms pur di non / per non
pur + gerund concessives vs mid-clause modal pur ('dovrai pur trovare...')
anzi bidirectionality: correction vs intensification resolved by prior context
assai part-of-speech disambiguation: adverb 'very' vs adjective 'many'
C1 discourse-word micro-set: appunto, addirittura, a tal proposito, macche', man mano che
productive prefix drills as true/false definition judgements (neo-, and by extension stra-/super- intensifiers)
single vs double consonant listening dictation via minimal pairs and in-sentence cloze (mezz'ora type targets)
